The review found that patients who received an educational program were less likely to visit the emergency department and be admitted to hospital compared with usual medical care. However, there is some uncertainty about the effect of education on other measures of asthma severity such as quality of life, symptoms and lung function. There was great variation in the types of programs used in the trials making it difficult to determine which is the most effective.

We found 130 randomized controlled trials with 8341 participants. The main comparison was between ketamine 10 mg/m² body surface area to 2 to 5 μg/kg/minute and a placebo (a substance containing no medication). Other comparisons were between ketamine and other anaesthetic drugs, or between ketamine and no treatment. The main outcome measures recorded were amount of opioid (morphine-like drugs) used for pain relief, and pain at rest and on movement around the time of surgery and up to 48 hours afterwards. We also recorded side effects. The evidence is current to August 2013. We found that intravenous ketamine administered before and/or during surgery reduces the need for opioids for pain relief after surgery, and the intensity of pain at rest and on movement. This applies equally to different types of surgery, and to larger and smaller studies. Side effects of ketamine were similar to those of the control treatment, except for a reduction in postoperative nausea and vomiting with ketamine. The reduction in pain was five points on a 100-point scale at 24 hours, which may not be enough to justify use of ketamine on its own, but could be relevant if combined with other drugs. Our results are based on moderate- to high-quality evidence, which means we have some confidence in them, although further research might change our conclusions.

The review found five trials including 217 participants; none of these studies looked at the impact of this vaccine on death. There were no severe or unusual side effects at doses of 13 to 15 micrograms of each component, which is much lower than the amount given with current malaria vaccines. The amount of malaria seen in people receiving the vaccine was similar to that in the control group in three trials, but two trials did show some benefit. However, in one trial the people who received the vaccine had less severe malaria episodes. The authors conclude that this vaccine has promise, but more work needs to be done before it will be ready for widespread use.

The four small studies in this review suggest that EMF may be effective in treating these difficult fractures. However, the evidence is far from conclusive and more research is needed before its use can be recommended.

A total of 1831 patients underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy in the 12 randomised clinical trials included in this review. Of these, 915 patients had a drain and 916 patients did not have a drain. The choice of whether a drain was used or not was determined by a method similar to the toss of coin so that the treatments compared were conducted in people who were as similar as possible. Two trials including 199 patients were of high quality; the remaining 1632 patients were divided between the other 10 trials. The average age of patients in nine of the trials was between 48 years and 63 years. The percentage of females in 11 of the trials was between 55% and 79%. There was no significant difference in the number of deaths (1/840 in the drain group and 2/841 in the 'no drain' group), the number of patients who developed serious complications (7/567 in the drain group and 3/576 in the 'no drain' group) or the number of serious complications in each group (drain group 1.5 per 100 patients and 'no drain' group 0.9 per 100 patients) between the two groups. There was also no difference in the quality of life between the groups although only one trial reported on this outcome. The percentage of patients discharged as day-procedure laparoscopic cholecystectomy seemed significantly lower in the drain group than the 'no drain' group. There was no significant difference in the length of hospital stay between the two groups nor in the time taken to return to normal activity and to return to work. Overall, there is currently no evidence to support the routine use of drain after laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Further well designed randomised clinical trials are necessary.

This review examined the effects of various herbal formulations used in Chinese medicine for treating diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Fifty trials were identified which tested 38 different herbal medicines, but most of the trials were of poor quality. There was no evidence to support the effectiveness of these herbal medicines and insufficient evidence to assess their safety.

This review identified five trials, with a total of 1382 patients, that compared IAS to CAS for treatment of localized prostate cancer. All of the studies involved men with more advanced prostate cancer (stage T3 or T4) and were therefore at a higher risk of recurrence. However, none of the studies assessed whether the treatments affected overall survival, prostate-cancer specific survival, or the spread of cancer to other parts of the body. One study, with 77 participants, looked at the effect of each treatment on prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels in the blood. PSA is a substance produced by both normal and cancerous prostate tissue. It has been suggested that an increase in PSA level could indicate recurrence of prostate cancer after treatment. The study found no clear difference in the time to PSA failure between IAS and CAS, but there was a suggestion that PSA failed later in patients with Gleason scores greater than 6 who were treated with CAS. Most of the studies reported on side effects. One study found no difference in side effects (constipation, diarrhea, loss of libido and fatigue) between IAS and CAS, but there were fewer cases of impotence in the IAS group. This review did not find any evidence as to which treatment is better for reducing the risk of dying from prostate cancer or the risk of the cancer spreading around the body. Limited evidence suggested that IAS may be associated with fewer side effects, particularly impotence.

We included 12 randomised controlled trials in this review. These evaluated 7,119 women who had an abortion and either had an IUD inserted right away or got one later on. Five of these trials randomly assigned women to have their IUD put in either right away or later. When the more recent types of IUDs were studied, such as the Mirena and the ParaGard, women who had theirs put in right away were more likely to keep it in than if it was put in later. However, with the older type of IUD, called the Copper 7, women were more likely to have it fall out if it was put in right away rather than later. Inserting an IUD right after abortion appears to be safe and practical. The IUD was less likely to come out on its own when the newer types of IUDs were used right after abortion.

In this review we found one small study on 41 patients with leukaemia. The authors reported that meditation practice might be beneficial for the quality of life of haematologically-diseased patients and might decrease depressive symptoms in patients with haematological malignancies. However, the study had several limitations and the result should be interpreted with caution. To estimate the effects of meditation practice for patients suffering from haematological malignancies, more studies are needed.

The review authors searched the medical literature and found three studies that met the inclusion criteria, all of which involved healthy children. Two of these studies had high or unclear risk of bias. The three studies were not alike; they differed in the vaccine used, length of follow-up and the outcomes measured. Thirteen out of 56 vaccine recipients developed chickenpox compared with 42 out of 54 placebo or no vaccine recipients. Most of the children (88%) who developed chickenpox had mild disease (fewer than 50 skin lesions). None of the studies assessed adverse events following vaccination. The results of this review suggest that varicella vaccine given within three days to children following household contact with a varicella case reduces infection rates and the number of skin lesions in those who are infected. We did not find any studies on adolescents or adults.

We identified 12 randomised controlled trials involving a total of 4704 people. Seven of these trials examined the effects of giving different antibiotics, and five examined the effects of giving antibiotics compared with no treatment. No studies examined the effects of giving two or more antibiotics simultaneously. There were no clear differences in the number of people who developed MRSA infections after surgery when different antibiotics were used as prophylaxis but there was some reduction in the number of people who developed MRSA infections when any antibiotic was used compared with no treatment. However, this result should be interpreted cautiously due to the poor methodological quality of the included studies. None of the studies examined the effect of giving two or more antibiotics together. None of the studies reported whether MRSA was tested before surgery or whether patients were given antibiotics to try to eradicate MRSA carried on their skin. It is not possible to draw reliable conclusions from the available evidence.

The review aimed to compare the benefits and risks of managing premature rupture of the membranes (PROM) in either the home or hospital environment. Two randomised controlled studies (116 women) met the inclusion criteria. Both studies had strict inclusion criteria and relatively few of the women presenting with PROM were eligible for inclusion. The overall quality of the evidence was low. There was insufficient evidence to determine whether there was any difference between the two groups for perinatal death, serious illness of the newborn, gestational age at delivery, birthweight and admission to neonatal intensive care. There was no information on serious complications for mothers or deaths. Women managed in hospital were more likely to have a caesarean section birth, but other factors may have influenced this decision. Mothers at home spent around 10 days less in hospital, were more likely to be satisfied with their care and there were cost savings. Larger studies are needed to confirm these findings and assess other important outcomes.

This review examined which methods of skin closure at caesarean section are better for the mother. The authors identified five randomised controlled trials involving 2097 women who had a caesarean section that compared stapling with subcutaneous suture. When all the studies were combined, there were no differences found in terms of pain, cosmetic results or wound infection between the two methods of closure. Staples did appear to increase the chance that the skin would separate and require re-stitching but this finding was not consistent across the studies. This could be due to the fact that the studies used different types of skin incisions.

We searched for randomised controlled trials which had assessed the effects of drinking tea on adults at a healthy level of CVD risk for at least two years. We did not include people with existing heart disease, diabetes or renal disease. Eleven randomised controlled trials met our inclusion criteria, seven with green tea and four with black tea. We found no trials assessing CVD outcomes, but both green and black tea reduced levels of cholesterol and blood pressure. However, few studies contributed to each outcome and so results should be treated with some caution. There is currently insufficient evidence from randomised controlled trials to assess whether consumption of tea reduces the incidence of CVD. More trials are needed.

The aim of this review was to assess the efficacy of azathioprine when used as a steroid-sparing agent in patients with chronic asthma. Two small trials were included in the review. These studies do not provide enough evidence to assess whether or not azathioprine is effective in reducing oral steroid dose in people with chronic asthma. Further large scale research needs to be done before any recommendations can be made.

We included 56 studies with 4068 participants in this review. The majority of studies (28 out of 56) were conducted in patients with breast cancer. We found that aerobic exercise was beneficial for reducing cancer related fatigue particularly in patients with breast and prostate cancer. However, there was no benefit of exercise on fatigue for patients with leukaemia or lymphoma. Furthermore, there appears to be no benefit of exercise on fatigue when given before chemotherapy or radiotherapy. These results suggest that exercise can be used as a therapeutic option to reduce fatigue but should not be used alone. Future trials should measure quality of life outcomes and should measure fatigue over time using validated fatigue scales.

We searched important medical databases such as CENTRAL, MEDLINE and conference proceedings up to 25 February 2016. We included randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing the survival of patients treated with either standard or escalated BEACOPP. Patients were aged 16 to 65 years with newly diagnosed classical Hodgkin's lymphoma. We found five RCTs with 3427 patients. The RCTs were published between 2000 and 2013 and originated from Germany, Italy and Belgium. The results of this review are based on a meta-analysis, which is a statistical method to combine the results of several studies. We found that the use of escalated BEACOPP compared to standard BEACOPP improves overall survival and progression-free survival. Moreover, there is no evidence for a difference in treatment-related mortality between both regimens. However, patients who received escalated BEACOPP may have a slightly higher risk of developing non-Hodgkin's lymphoma or leukaemia as a second cancer. Whether they will have children is difficult to say because the number of women in the studies was small and their ages were relatively old. We found some evidence that escalated BEACOPP may lead to more severe low blood cell counts (so called haematological toxicities) and infections. However, only one study assessed quality of life, and did not report any results. The overall quality of evidence was moderate to high for survival outcomes and low for second malignancies, febrile neutropenia, infection, anaemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia and bleeding complications. This means that we are fairly confident about the size of the effect for the survival outcomes, but less confident about the other outcomes. The reason for this is that the studies had different designs and the number of patients was small.

We searched databases such as EMBASE, MEDLINE, PsycINFO, CINAHL and Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials to find randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on return to work in adult cancer patients. We included 15 RCTs with 1835 patients. Because some of these RCTs included more than one evaluation we included 19 evaluations in this review. We found that psycho-educational interventions, aiming to educate patients and teach self-care behaviours, have no effect on return to work or quality of life when compared to care as usual. We also found that physical training has no effect on return to work or quality of life in patients with breast cancer. Less radical, functioning-conserving medical interventions have similar return to work rates and quality of life outcomes when compared with more radical treatments. Multidisciplinary interventions, combining physical, psycho-educational and/or vocational components, may help patients to return to work earlier than care as usual. The quality of the evidence ranged from very low to moderate. Future research should aim to improve the quality of the evidence by increasing the number of participants in the included studies and by using other outcome measures such as productivity.

We searched for evidence on 15 May 2015 and identified four trials involving over 13,000 women. All trials were conducted in the UK and Ireland. Three trials were funded by the hospitals where the trials took place and one trial was funded by the Scottish government. Two trials did not report funding sources and none of the trials reported who provided the funding for the trial. One trial reported that authors had no competing interests; the other three trials did not report whether or not author had competing interests. The four trials all compared admission CTG to intermittent auscultation (listening to the baby's heartbeat with a pinching device on the mother's abdomen) in women considered to be at low risk of complications. The results showed that there was probably no difference in outcomes for the babies, but that women who had an admission CTG were more likely to have a caesarean section (moderate quality evidence). They were also more likely to have continuous electronic fetal monitoring and to have their baby's scalp stimulated with gels and wipes during labour (low quality evidence). There was insufficient information to show whether or not there was a difference in the number of babies that died (very low quality evidence). None of the trials followed up the children to assess any long-term development (very low quality evidence). The evidence suggests that admission CTG is not beneficial for women who are considered to be at low risk and may be harmful. The quality of the evidence was moderate to very low. The main reasons for downgrading the quality of the evidence were imprecision, inconsistency between the trials and a lack of blinding for participants and personnel.

The evidence was current to April 2013. We included 32 studies in this review. In 17 of these, women were randomised to be cultured in different media (total 3666); in three, cycles were randomised (total 1018); and in 12, oocytes were randomised (more than 15,230). Unfortunately, each study compared different media, therefore it was not possible to combine the results. Only seven studies reported live birth or ongoing pregnancy. Four of these found no evidence of a difference between the media compared, for either day three or day five embryo transfer. The data from the third study could not be relied upon. Six studies reported clinical pregnancy rate. One of these found a difference, suggesting that for cleavage-stage embryo transfer, Quinn's Advantage was associated with higher clinical pregnancy rates than G5 (odds ratio (OR) 1.56; 95% CI 1.12 to 2.16; 692 women). This study was assessed as low quality. With regards to adverse effects, three studies reported multiple pregnancies and six studies reported miscarriage. None of them found any evidence of a difference between the culture media used. None of the studies reported on the health of offspring. Most studies (22/32) failed to report their source of funding and none described their methodology in sufficient detail. The overall quality of the evidence was rated as very low for nearly all comparisons, the main limitations being imprecision and poor reporting of study methods.

The aim of this review was to look at which way of telling a woman that she has breast cancer is best. However, despite extensive searching, the authors were unable to find any trials that looked at this specific question. Future studies in this area would be very difficult to conduct, especially as it would not be ethical to allocate people to different ways of receiving their diagnosis.

This review examined the effectiveness of any psychological treatment compared to standard care or placebo for people with non-specific chest pain. We included 17 trials with 1006 participants who received either psychological treatment or usual medical care. Psychological treatments were found to be modestly effective in reducing chest pain reports and increasing the number of chest pain free days in the first three months. They did not appear to have any effect on pain severity during this time. Some studies reported that psychological treatments were only beneficial in the short term. Future studies should aim to look at the long-term effects of psychological treatments and compare them to other treatments such as anti-depressant drugs.

We found 30 randomised controlled trials with 4344 participants evaluating the efficacy and safety of topical antihistamines and mast cell stabilisers. These included: azelastine, bepotastine besilate, emedastine, ketotifen, levocabastine, mequitazine, nedocromil sodium, olopatadine, pemirolast potassium, sodium cromoglycate, and the combination of antazoline and tetryzoline. Most studies compared a single drug against a placebo. We did not find any differences in how well the medications worked when we compared different drugs directly. All medications reduced the symptoms and signs of allergic conjunctivitis when compared with placebo in the short term. None of the studies reported any serious side effects. The quality of the studies and reporting was variable, but overall the risk of bias was low. Studies only looked at short-term effects, with a range of treatment of one to eight weeks.

We searched for programs that had been evaluated in trials and targeted Indigenous youth aged 10 to 24 years. We included two completed studies with 1505 participants in this review. Both studies were based in the USA and both focused on Native Americans. The programs were multi-faceted and were developed to be relevant to the specific culture of the tribe in which they were situated. One program looked at the impact of the skills-training component only. There was no clear indication of whether either program was more effective than the other in reducing tobacco use. More research is needed to find out what works best to prevent Indigenous youth from using tobacco.

We searched for evidence on 20 May 2016 and identified only one trial (72 women) comparing routine blood transfusions during pregnancy with transfusions given only as needed. This trial was conducted in Jamaica and the women had sickle cell anaemia (HbSS). The evidence from this trial is of very low quality and suggests that there are no differences in outcomes for mother or baby in terms of death, pulmonary embolism, heart failure, acute chest syndrome, use of analgesia, length of hospital stay, or any other indicators of poor outcome, whether the blood transfusions were routine or given only as needed. There was no information on how the study was done or whether it was funded by an agency with commercial interest in the results. We cannot be sure of the findings because of the lack of evidence and the risk of bias in the one small trial. More research is needed to find out which is the better approach.

We searched the medical literature in order to identify all the randomised clinical trials (where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at different techniques of liver resection. We wanted to investigate whether any of the methods were better than the others in terms of how long the surgery took, how much blood was lost during surgery, how many transfusions were given, how long patients stayed in hospital, and whether they survived or not. We also looked at complications (harms) and whether patients returned to work earlier with one technique than another. We included 67 randomised clinical trials with a total of 6197 participants. The evidence is current to September 2015. Most studies had problems with their design and reporting, which makes their results less reliable. We found some low- to very low-quality evidence that using a radiofrequency dissecting sealer led to more harms than the clamp-crush method. We also found low- to very low-quality evidence that there were advantages with one technique over another for the amount of blood transfused, how much blood was lost, length of overall hospital stay, and operating time, but these were not consistent across the trials. Currently, there is no evidence to suggest that any of the techniques studied are superior to the others for reducing death, morbidity, or blood transfusion requirements. Techniques such as radiofrequency dissecting sealer should not be used as standard practice outside the trial settings because there is evidence of increased harm without any evidence of benefit. Overall, the quality of the evidence was low to very low for all outcomes meaning that we cannot be certain about the results.

This review aimed to assess the various treatments available for this condition. The authors identified 23 randomised controlled trials which involved a total of 724 participants. The trials used a variety of outcome measures and a wide range of treatments. The results showed that oral PUVA and oral retinoids are each beneficial in the treatment of psoriasis, but there is evidence from one trial that the combination of PUVA and retinoids is even more effective. Topical steroids applied under hydrocolloid occlusive dressing may also be useful. There is some evidence that Grenz Ray therapy (a form of narrow band UVB phototherapy) may be useful, as may low dose oral cyclosporin and tetracyclines. Colchicine, which was assessed in two trials, has a number of adverse effects and it is not clear if it is effective. Topical PUVA was not active in one trial. There is no evidence from randomised controlled trials to support the use of hydroxycarbamide. Many studies were rated as having poor methodological quality and there was insufficient evidence on adverse events.

We developed a comprehensive search strategy of all relevant scientific databases to identify all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared the blood pressure-lowering effect of any dose of any loop diuretic with placebo or another antihypertension drug in adults with hypertension. We found nine trials that randomly assigned 460 adult participants to receive a loop diuretic at any dose or placebo or another type of antihypertension drug for a mean duration of 8.8 weeks. On average, loop diuretics lowered systolic/diastolic blood pressure by -8/-4 mmHg. There was no apparent difference in the blood pressure-lowering effect among the five different loop diuretics studied. The included studies were of low methodological quality, had a short duration, and were unlikely to have fully reported harms. We therefore consider our estimate of the blood pressure-lowering effect of loop diuretics to be likely an overestimate.

We identified 58 randomised clinical trials comparing local anaesthetic poured onto the abdomen with no local anaesthetic. Only 16 trials reported all the outcomes we thought were important. The local anaesthetics used varied as did the way they were put onto the abdomen. This makes it difficult to compare results between trials. No-one died as a result of the treatment in the eight trials that reported deaths (410 participants in total). One major complication occurred in one person out of every 500 people receiving local anaesthetic (very low quality evidence). Most trials included people at low risk of complications. Pain was lower in the participants who received local anaesthetic than in those who did not receive local anaesthetic at four to eight hours (32 trials; 2020 participants) and at nine to 24 hours (29 trials; 1787 participants) after surgery (very low quality evidence). We could not tell whether the pain was reduced by as much as 50% because of the variability in the results from the trials. Some people may experience this level of pain reduction, while others may not experience much benefit at all. We cannot know in advance who will benefit and who will not. The trials did not usually include people at high risk of complications. Further trials with low risk of error are necessary before we can be more confident of these findings. These trials should assess the effects of local anaesthetic on pain over a longer period of time, such as six months to one year, and should include other outcomes that are important to the person having the operation, such as quality of life and time to return to work.

In this review, we categorised the available RDTs according to which parasite proteins they detect. We found 74 unique studies evaluating 38 different RDTs. We found that all of the tests evaluated were reasonably accurate, with few missing confirmed cases or incorrectly diagnosing healthy people with malaria. There were two main categories of RDTs based on which parasite protein they detect. Those that detect a protein called histidine-rich protein 2 (HRP-2) from the parasite (Types 1 to 3) were better at picking up current infections but were worse at distinguishing between malaria and non-malaria causes of fever. Those that detect a protein called lactate dehydrogenase (pLDH) from the Plasmodium genus (Types 4 and 5) were better at avoiding false positives but missed more cases. However, even the best RDTs still miss about 10% of cases. It is important to note that HRP-2 persists in the blood for some time after successful treatment, meaning that a positive result does not necessarily mean active infection, and that a negative result does not rule out recent exposure. This means that HRP-2 tests are not suitable for monitoring treatment failure. All of the RDTs evaluated in this review could replace or extend the use of microscopy for diagnosis of uncomplicated P. falciparum malaria in low-resource settings.

We found five studies that examined the effect of altering the length of general practitioner (GP) appointments in the UK. The studies were all conducted in the UK, with four taking place in Scotland and one in England. They all looked at how changing the length of GP appointments affects the amount of time spent during the consultation, but none looked at any actual health outcomes. We found no clear evidence that making GPs' appointments longer or shorter increased or decreased the amount of time actually spent during the consultation. There was also no evidence that it affected the number of referrals made by the GP, the number of tests requested, or patients' satisfaction with their appointment. We could not assess the quality of evidence for most of the outcomes as there was so few studies to analyse, but we judged the overall certainty of the evidence to be very low. There was no evidence on how such a change would affect use of resources. We do not have enough information to be certain about whether altering the length of GP appointments is helpful or not. More research is needed before any firm conclusions can be drawn.

This review found three published and unpublished trials that compared high dose to low dose stavudine use in people on HIV treatment. All three trials were conducted in developed countries, in people who had been on treatment for some time and who had a suppressed viral load at the start of the trial. We could not combine the results of the trials as there were too few trials and the trials differed in their design. The individual results of each trial were imprecise but there was no clear benefit in either virological or aesthetic outcomes with high dose stavudine. No serious side effects were reported and non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors were the primary drugs used in two of the three trials. We conclude that further research is needed to assess the benefits and harms of reducing stavudine doses in HIV positive people who are not yet on treatment or are not virally suppressed.

We searched the literature up to 2 February 2016. We included 10 studies with 1658 adults with ARDS aged between 18 and 75 years of age. The time from diagnosis of ARDS to start of study treatment was between one hour and 12 hours. Six studies reported sources of funding. Four did not report funding sources. One of the six studies funded by an institutional grant was funded by a manufacturer of a mechanical ventilator and two were funded by government agencies. Two of the four studies that did not report funding sources were funded by a pharmaceutical company and a charitable organisation respectively. We found that using techniques to recruit the lungs during ventilation reduced the number of deaths in the first few days after diagnosis of ARDS, but that there was a wide variation in the results of individual studies. The use of recruitment manoeuvres did not result in any additional complications. However, most of the studies used a combination of treatments when applying recruitment manoeuvres, making it difficult to isolate the effects of recruitment alone. The overall quality of evidence was low.

This study aimed to assess whether psychological treatments such as relaxation therapy or cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT) could improve the symptoms, and the self management of people with asthma. The review found 15 studies which included 687 participants. These studies had low methodological quality and many were carried out in the 1980s. They examined the effects of a variety of psychological interventions including relaxation therapy, biofeedback and CBT. Overall, the studies were inconsistent and were too small to be able to give a reliable indication of whether the interventions were effective. Further good quality research is needed if the effectiveness of psychological treatments for adult asthma is to be properly assessed.

This review summarises the evidence for the effectiveness and tolerability of antidepressants and benzodiazepines in the acute-phase treatment of panic disorder. Thirty-five randomised controlled trials (RCTs), including 6785 participants, were included in this review. Not all RCTs examined what we wanted to know, but we were able to combine data from some studies to answer our questions. The quality of evidence was low or very low, meaning that we cannot draw firm conclusions from the results. Most studies did not report whether people dropped out due to side effects or other reasons. Fewer people taking SSRIs experienced side effects when compared to those who took TCAs. There was no clear difference in effectiveness between antidepressants and benzodiazepines. However, fewer people taking benzodiazepines stopped taking their allocated medication before completing the study. Based on these results, both classes of medications seem reasonable options to treat panic disorder in the short term. Each person's characteristics and needs should be taken into account when choosing between them. More research is needed to help us understand how these two types of medications compare in terms of effectiveness and side effects over time. Future studies should be designed to minimise potential biases that could distort the accuracy of study findings.

We reviewed the published research on the effectiveness and safety of endoscopic placement of metal versus plastic stents to open up the bile duct in patients with cancer of the head of the pancreas who had obstructed flow of bile into the intestine. We also looked at different kinds of stents to see whether some were better than others. We included 29 randomised controlled trials with over 1700 participants. Most studies compared metal stents to plastic stents. Some studies compared different kinds of plastic stents. The review found that metal stents worked better than plastic stents to keep the bile duct open. However, in patients with very short life expectancy, the benefits of metal stents may not apply as they do not get used long enough to benefit from the stent keeping the bile duct open. Different kinds of plastic stents were studied in six trials. Only one showed that a particular type of plastic stent kept the bile duct open longer than other types. An anti-reflux valve can be added to some stents to prevent bile leaking back up into the stomach. This improves the time before the stent becomes blocked.

We searched the literature up to 5 January 2016 and found five studies that met the inclusion criteria. The studies were conducted in China, India, South Korea and Qatar. They included a total of 444 arterial cannulations in children aged from one day to 15 years. Four studies compared ultrasound with palpation, and one small study (30 participants) compared ultrasound with Doppler auditory assistance. The number of participants in each study was between 50 and 200. We found moderate-quality evidence that ultrasound guidance increases the likelihood of successful radial artery cannulation at both the first attempt and within two attempts. The benefit seems to be greater in infants and small children. We found no clear evidence about the impact of the level of experience of the person performing the procedure on the success rate. Complications, such as haematoma formation, are less likely to occur when ultrasound guidance is used. No study reported on ischaemic damage. We downgraded the quality of the evidence to'moderate' owing to wide confidence intervals, modest sample sizes and limited numbers of events.

The review authors searched scientific databases and other resources to find studies assessing the impact of restricting the promotion of alcohol products through various types of advertising bans on alcohol use and harm in heavy drinkers. We included four studies in this review: one experimental study (80 male students aged between 18 and 35 years of age who were randomly allocated to watching a movie with either low-level or high-level alcohol advertising content, with measures taken immediately afterwards); and three observational time-series studies (general population studies from Canada, each looking at different types of bans). The experimental study found that young men exposed to movies with a low-alcohol content drank fewer beers compared with those exposed to movies with a high-alcohol content. Young men exposed to programmes with no alcohol advertisements drank fewer beers compared with those exposed to alcohol advertisements. However, the quality of the evidence was very low and the study had no follow-up data. The three time-series studies looked at the impact of introducing or lifting a ban on alcohol advertising. Each study looked at a different type of ban, but their results were mixed and did not provide clear support for or opposition to the introduction of bans. Therefore, there is currently insufficient evidence to draw conclusions about whether restrictions on alcohol advertising are effective in reducing alcohol use or related harms. More high-quality research is needed if the effectiveness of alcohol advertising restrictions are to be fully understood. None of the included studies appeared to have been funded by the alcohol or advertising industries.

This review examined whether there is a difference in growth, gut function, and brain development in healthy preterm infant feeding with either a medium-chain triglyceride (MCT)-enriched formula or a conventional formula containing longer chain fatty acids (LCT). We found eight small studies that enrolled 182 infants. These studies show that there is no difference in short-term growth, gut function, or incidence of severe bowel problems known as necrotizing enterocolitis. More research is needed to determine whether there are differences in longer term growth and development.

We searched for evidence on 20 May 2019 and identified only one study for inclusion in this review. This study involved 34 women with chorioamnionitis who had an amnioinfusion through the cervix with room temperature saline solution at 10 mL per minute for 60 minutes, then 3 mL per minute until delivery. The amnioinfusion was done alongside routine management of the women’s condition with intravenous antibiotics and pain relief. The other comparison group had no amnioinfusion. All women also had a pressure monitor placed in their uterus (intrauterine pressure catheter) and were given analgesia and antibiotics (ampicillin or, if they were allergic to penicillin, gentamycin). The quality of the evidence was assessed as being low for the outcomes of postpartum inflammation of the womb (endometritis), neonatal infection and caesarean section. The study did not report on the death of the baby or severe illness (brain injury due to lack of oxygen, bleeding inside the brain, admission to intensive care) during birth. There was no clear difference in the risk of developing postpartum endometritis (176 per 1000 women with amnioinfusion versus 118 per 1000 with no amnioinfusion) or neonatal infection (0 per 1000 versus 0 per 1000) between the two groups. The study did not report whether there was a difference in the length of time women required antibiotics or how long they stayed in hospital. The temperature at delivery was lower in women receiving amnioinfusion (mean difference of 0.38°C). This might be important because high fever can cause brain injury to the baby. However, this was not a planned outcome of this review. None of the other outcomes we thought were important were reported in the study. Overall, there is not enough evidence to be sure whether amnioinfusion is helpful or harmful for women with chorioamnionitis. More studies with more participants would be needed to know more about the possible benefits and harms.

This review identified three studies evaluating the accuracy of the Mini-Cog in community dwelling individuals, with a total of 1620 participants. The sensitivity of the Mini-Cog in these studies was 0.76 to 0.99 and the specificity was 0.83 to 0.93. There were concerns about the methods used in each of the three studies. It is not possible to accurately assess the performance of the Mini-Cog when applied to community dwelling people from general practice settings based on the information available in this review. Further research is needed to assess the accuracy of the Mini-Cog in screening for dementia in community settings.

As of September 2017, we identified 19 randomised controlled trials (studies where people are randomly allocated to one of two or more treatment groups) that met our inclusion criteria. These studies included a total of 2521 people. All participants received some form of amphetamine medication, and half received a placebo (a dummy pill which looks like the real thing but does not contain amphetamine). There were two main types of amphetamine involved: dexamphetamine (10.2 mg to 21.8 mg per day), lisdexamfetamine (30 mg to 70 mg per day), and mixed amphetamine salts (MAS; 12.5 mg to 80 mg per day). The duration of the studies was typically quite short, lasting only a few weeks. We found no studies that had a low risk of bias in all aspects of the Cochrane ‘Risk of bias’ tool. This was mainly because amphetamines have powerful side effects that may reveal whether someone is taking medication or placebo, but also because some studies were missing data from some participants. Sixteen studies were funded by the pharmaceutical industry, one study was publicly funded, and two studies did not state how they were funded. We found that amphetamines were better than placebo in reducing the severity of ADHD symptoms, both as assessed by clinicians and patients. However, we did not find evidence that amphetamines were better than other drugs in reducing the severity of ADHD symptoms. In addition, we found that people who received amphetamines were more likely to drop out of the studies because of side effects than those who received placebo. Different amphetamine preparations did not appear to be associated with differences in efficacy (i.e. how well the drug works), but only two studies examined this question. Similarly, we found no difference in how well the different preparations worked when compared to placebo. Overall, the evidence we found was of low to very low quality, mainly because it was based on studies with fairly short durations and fairly select groups of people (almost exclusively adults with ADHD, almost exclusively males, and with a combined type of ADHD).

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing vitamin D with placebo (a 'dummy' treatment such as a placebo cream or a fake tablet) in adults aged 18 years or older who had symptoms of chronic pain. Outcomes included pain, functional ability/disability, quality of life, and side effects. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found 10 studies with 811 participants. There was no consistent benefit from vitamin D therapy in any chronic painful condition. Some studies showed benefit from vitamin D therapy, but these were not replicated in subsequent studies. Adverse events and withdrawals due to adverse events were low and similar with vitamin D and placebo treatment. The overall quality of the evidence was low. It is possible that vitamin D may be useful in a few people, but not likely to be of general benefit in chronic painful conditions.

The review found that: Professional interventions can improve the way health professionals manage people with diabetes, but have had less consistent effects on patient outcomes. Organisational interventions can improve the management of diabetes but have not been shown to reduce mortality or morbidity. Patient education alone has only minor effects on diabetes control. However, when combined with other interventions, it can lead to improvements in diabetes control. There are many different types of interventions possible and more research is needed to find out which are most effective.

We found five randomised controlled trials that compared a birth control pill with either a skin patch, vaginal ring, or an intrauterine device (IUD). We also found three trials that compared different IUDs. The studies included a total of 1503 women, with an average of 300 women per study. The women were all ages under 25 years old at the start of the study. The types of birth control used for at least four months were: birth control pills; the vaginal ring; the skin patch; the IUD with levonorgestrel (a hormone); and the IUD with copper. The women knew which type of birth control they were using. The studies lasted from six months to three years. The birth control methods seemed equally effective in preventing pregnancy. Continuation rates were at least 75% for all studies. The studies did not report on side effects or changes in satisfaction. The overall quality of evidence was rated as moderate to low. Limitations included small numbers of women in some studies and not knowing how long women used the methods.

We set out to review the effects of this treatment in children and adults with chronic asthma, who had allergic sensitisation to either house dust mites, pollen, animals or moulds. We included 88 randomised controlled trials. Most studies evaluated people with house dust mite allergy (42 studies), but there were also 27 studies for pollen allergy, 10 for animal dander allergy, two for mould allergy, two for latex allergy and six for multiple allergies. The trials showed that this form of treatment can lead to significant improvements in asthma symptoms and a reduction in the need for inhaled steroids. However, it can cause local reactions such as itching of the mouth and throat, and in rare cases anaphylaxis (a severe whole body allergic reaction). It is still not clear whether this form of treatment should be used in all people with allergic asthma, or only in those who are sensitive to indoor allergens.

The authors searched the literature until April 2013 and identified six randomized controlled trials with 1297 participants. They found no new studies since the previous version of the review. The six studies included in this updated review were published between 1995 and 2004. All six studies compared the use of lower tidal volumes with the conventional ventilation technique. The studies showed a reduction in mortality at day 28 and at the end of the hospital stay with the use of lower tidal volumes. The effect on long-term mortality is unknown.

The evidence is current to September 2016. We found 15 randomized controlled trials with 1833 participants comparing these two techniques in adults undergoing various operations. The quality of the evidence was low. With regard to our primary outcome of time to emergence from anaesthesia, which is the time needed to follow verbal commands, it was longer with isoflurane (an inhalational agent) than with propofol (intravenous agent) and not different between sevoflurane (inhalational agent) and propofol. Time to awakening, which is the time needed to open your eyes on command, was also longer with isoflurane than with propofol and not different between sevoflurane and propofol. We found no clear differences between the two techniques in terms of side effects. Our findings suggest that the intravenous technique is comparable with the inhalational technique (sevoflurane) to provide early emergence from anaesthesia, and adverse events with both techniques are also comparable. Use of isoflurane (an inhalational agent) delays emergence from anaesthesia. These results should be interpreted with caution. Randomized controlled trials based on uniform and standard methods are needed. Study authors did not report funding sources for most of the included studies. The quality of the evidence was low because of small numbers of included studies and small numbers of participants in these studies.

This review examined whether lower blood pressure in older people reduces the risk of Alzheimer's disease and other forms of dementia. Fourteen thousand five hundred and thirty six participants were involved in the four included randomised controlled trials which compared an antihypertensive regimen with placebo. On average the participants were aged 75 years old and had slightly raised blood pressure. The length of treatment varied from 2 to 6 years. There was no clear evidence that lowering blood pressure reduced the risk of developing dementia. However, there were some problems with interpreting the results of the studies due to missing data. A meta-analysis using the individual patient data would give a more reliable result.

This review summarises the results of 12 trials including 3474 patients where PTCRA was used in conjunction with balloon angioplasty (PTCRA/PTCA) to treat heart artery narrowing. For patients with narrowings that are difficult to reach or have a complicated shape, there are limited published studies and no long term data on the effects of PTCRA. However, in patients with narrowings that are relatively easy to reach, PTCRA/PTCA does not appear to improve the outcome compared to balloon angioplasty alone. Patients who had PTCRA/PTCA were more likely to experience a sudden narrowing of the blood vessel, a hole in the blood vessel or temporary blockage of the blood vessel, but were less likely to have a dissection (a tear in the blood vessel) or need a stent placed at the time of the procedure.

The aim of this review was to assess the efficacy and safety of nebulised rhDNase in infants and young children hospitalised with bronchiolitis. Three studies enrolling 333 infants aged less than 24 months were included. All studies used 2.5 mL (1 mg/mL) of nebulised rhDNase compared with placebo, given either as a daily or twice daily dose. Nebulised rhDNase showed no benefit in any clinically meaningful outcome. The overall results did not support the use of nebulised rhDNase in children under 24 months of age hospitalized with acute bronchiolitis.

We searched the literature on 19 June 2015 and identified two studies with a total of 181 participants. Of these, 116 patients underwent the percutaneous technique and 65 were treated by cut-down femoral artery access. One of the studies was relatively small and reporting of study methods was poor. The second study was a large study with few sources of bias and good methodology. We found no difference in death at 30 days between the two techniques (moderate-quality evidence), with only one death occurring overall, in the percutaneous group. One study reported aneurysm exclusion, which was better with the cut-down femoral artery access technique (moderate-quality evidence), but there were only two aneurysms that failed to exclude in the percutaneous group. No wounds became infected in either group across both studies nor were there any major complications, bleeding complications or haematoma formation (high- and moderate-quality evidence respectively). There was no difference in complication rates at six months (moderate-quality evidence), nor in intensive treatment unit and hospital stay between the two groups. The percutaneous approach was significantly faster than the cut-down femoral artery access technique (moderate-quality evidence). The quality of the evidence was downgraded to moderate as a result of the limited number of studies, low event numbers and imprecision. Further research is required to improve the evidence base.

In this review, we found two trials that compared the birth control sponge to the diaphragm. The sponge did not work as well to prevent pregnancy as the diaphragm did. More women using the sponge broke out in allergic rash than those using the diaphragm, but only small numbers had problems with the way the sponge fit. These findings are from the original 1990 review and no new trials have been done since then.

This review focuses on the prevention and treatment of these cognitive changes. We looked at all the published studies where the participants had an average age of 60 years or more, had mild to moderate problems with their thinking skills at the time of participation in the study and had received radiotherapy to the brain within the previous year. We only considered studies that randomly allocated participants to one of the treatments under investigation, as we judged these most likely to produce reliable results. We found six randomised studies that met our selection criteria. Three of these studies examined whether a drug could prevent cognitive decline: two investigated the effects of drugs on people who had not received radiotherapy, and one investigated a rehabilitation programme to prevent cognitive decline. The other three studies examined whether a drug could treat established cognitive problems after a person had already received radiotherapy. No non-drug treatments were found that could prevent or treat cognitive problems. One of the studies that investigated the preventative effects of a drug did not report sufficient information to allow us to draw conclusions about the usefulness of this treatment. However, two studies suggested that drugs may be effective in either preventing or treating cognitive problems caused by radiotherapy. These were donepezil, which is used to treat Alzheimer's disease, and memantine, which is used to treat Alzheimer's disease and vascular dementia. More research is needed to confirm that these findings are correct. Overall, the evidence in this area is scarce. It is difficult for researchers to keep people in the trials long enough to measure the effects of treatments, as many people die from their cancer within a short period of time. Researchers also lose participants who are able to take medications as prescribed, and it is difficult to know how well the remaining participants took their medication. Many of the studies were also flawed in some way, although none were so flawed that we felt we couldn't use the results at all.

The evidence on which this review is based was found to be up to date as of 20 January 2016. We searched for any relevant studies where people had received this type of damage during dental treatment and had then undergone laser treatment or placebo treatment. We identified two studies that met the requirements for the review and included 26 people aged between 17 and 55 years. These studies looked at whether there was an improvement in the feeling of having a 'numb' sensation in the chin and lip area after treatment. One of the studies also looked at how the patients felt about their condition. The limited number of studies available for inclusion in this review made it difficult to draw reliable conclusions. There was some evidence that laser treatment may help improve the feeling of having a 'numb' sensation in the chin and lip area, but the results were not clear. No studies examined the effect of treatment on pain, difficulty eating or speaking or taste. No studies reported on quality of life or side effects. The quality of the evidence was very low because of a lack of well-conducted and well-reported studies. More good-quality studies are needed in order to rate the effectiveness of treatments for this condition.

The review authors searched the medical literature and identified two randomised controlled studies with a total of 281 pregnancies and 282 fetuses. One study involved women with a history of recurrent miscarriage who were randomly assigned to receive either a combination of oestrogen and progestogen or a placebo (dummy treatment). The other study involved women who had undergone assisted reproduction and were given either a combination of oestrogen and progestogen or no treatment after the embryo transfer as part of their cycle of IVF. Both studies were of poor quality in terms of how they were conducted, and the two studies were too different to be combined. The study based on women with a history of recurrent miscarriage showed no clear difference between using combined oestrogen and progestogen and using a placebo for the numbers of miscarriages, perinatal deaths (fetal or neonatal deaths) or preterm births. However, it did show an increased risk of maternal cancer in the reproductive system in those receiving the hormonal treatment. None of the other outcomes assessed by the review were different between the two treatment groups. The study based on women undergoing IVF showed no clear difference in the number of miscarriages between the combined oestrogen and progestone group and the no treatment group. It did not report on any of the other measures in this review. Overall, there is not enough evidence from randomised controlled trials to assess the use of combined oestrogen and progestogen for preventing miscarriages.

This review found that antidepressants are effective for people with depression who consult their general practitioner. Tricyclic antidepressants and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors were shown to be more effective than placebo in this setting, with slightly better results for the former group.

For ibuprofen 400 mg compared to placebo, the number needed to treat (NNT) to relieve pain by two hours was 7.2, meaning that seven people must be treated for one to experience freedom from pain at two hours. Pain was reduced from moderate or severe to no pain by two hours in 5 in 10 people, known as the NNTp value, with a range of 4 to 8. In comparison, ibuprofen 200 mg had an NNT of 9.7 for initial pain relief and an NNTp of 5.2. The higher dose was significantly better than the lower dose for relief at two hours. A soluble formulation of ibuprofen 400 mg was better than standard tablets for relief at one hour, but not two hours. Ibuprofen 400 mg did not differ from rofecoxib 25 mg for initial pain relief or sustained headache relief over 24 hours. About 1 in 10 people experienced side effects with ibuprofen, the same as with placebo, and these were mostly mild and short-lived.

Our review included 43 randomized controlled trials with a total of 3497 participants with dry eye. We did not combine the results of any of these studies because of the many different types of over-the-counter artificial tears formulas and ways that the studies measured their outcomes. Instead, we looked at each individual comparison of one artificial tear against another. We found that most brands of artificial tears seemed equally effective, and we found little difference in drop size and pH level among brands. One brand, however, was rated as better than another based on two studies that used the same measures. That brand used 0.2% polyacrylic acid as its base. The other used 1.4% polyvinyl alcohol as its base. More research is needed to confirm this finding. We found that some drops may cause side effects such as eye irritation, burning, or tearing, but these were generally mild and were experienced by people who used both prescription and over-the-counter artificial tears. Overall, we thought the quality of the evidence was low. The main reasons for this assessment were that many of the studies had high risk of bias and often did not report enough details about how they designed and conducted their trials. This made it difficult to know if we could trust the results from those studies.

The purpose of this review was to assess the benefits and harms of alternative second-line treatments for people living with HIV who are failing their initial first-line treatment. Only one study was found that assessed an intervention where people continued to take lamivudine as part of their second-line therapy and two studies which looked at people who stopped taking lamivudine from their first-line therapy and started a different second-line therapy. There were no differences in the outcomes between those who continued to take lamivudine and those who did not in their second-line therapy. However, there were also no significant differences in the outcomes between the two groups in the observational studies. For people starting second-line therapy with boosted protease inhibitors, the results were very good in the studies that were identified but these were not head-to-head comparisons. The review could only recommend second-line therapies based on what is best for the individual given the lack of evidence.

We included 133 studies with 844,206 participants. We found that none of the tests were accurate enough to be used on their own as a single identifier for predicting difficult airway management. The summary sensitivity (true positive rate) of the tests for predicting difficult laryngoscopy ranged from 0.23 (mouth opening test) to 0.67 (upper lip bite test); for difficult tracheal intubation from 0.25 (thyromental distance) to 0.52 (modified Mallampati test); and for difficult face mask ventilation from 0.17 to 1.00 (modified Mallampati test). The summary specificity (true negative rate) of the tests for predicting difficult laryngoscopy ranged from 0.79 (modified Mallampati test) to 0.95 (Wilson risk score); for difficult tracheal intubation from 0.86 (modified Mallampati test) to 0.94 (mouth opening test); and for difficult face mask ventilation from 0.87 to 0.95 (modified Mallampati test). The upper lip bite test was the best indicator for difficult laryngoscopy, but even this test had a false negative rate of 33%. The modified Mallampati test was the best indicator for difficult tracheal intubation, but even this test had a false negative rate of 49%. None of the tests were accurate enough to be used on their own as a single identifier for predicting difficult airway management. There are limitations of our study. First, the outcome measures were not identical across studies, which makes it difficult to combine the results. Second, we did not include studies with children or patients with abnormal airways. Third, our study relied on the reporting of studies in relation to how the participants were selected and whether the information obtained from the airway examination was assessed independently of the actual difficulty encountered during anaesthesia. We judged the overall quality of evidence to be variable across the studies.

This review included 63 studies with a total of 8014 participants. It showed that probiotics are effective in reducing the duration of acute diarrhoea by about one day and the number of stools per day by about one. There were no reports of side effects due to probiotics. There was considerable variation in the results of the studies, which was not explained by the type of probiotics used, the severity of the diarrhoea, age of the patients, or whether the studies were done in developed or developing countries.

The review includes one study with 52 people who had schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder or other chronic mental illnesses. The study randomised each person to either receive nidotherapy added to their standard care or standard care alone. Standard care usually consists of medication and regular contact with mental health professionals, such as psychiatrists and psychologists. The study lasted 18 months. People who received nidotherapy, in addition to their standard care, showed some signs of improved social functioning and engagement with services, although these improvements were small and more research is needed. There were no other significant differences between the two groups in terms of other outcomes measured in the study, which include mental state, service use, accommodation, employment, satisfaction with life, quality of life, costs and side effects. More research is needed on this potentially promising treatment before it can be recommended for widespread use. This plain language summary has been written by a consumer Benjamin Gray: Rethink Mental Illness www.rethink.org/mental_illness/ information/ nidotherapy/

On 15 January 2015 we performed searches to look for clinical trials where pregabalin was used to treat pain due to fibromyalgia in adults. We found eight studies of decent quality that tested pregabalin against placebo for 12 to 26 weeks, with 3870 participants. Studies were randomised, double blind, and placebo-controlled. All studies included 300 to 600 mg pregabalin, and measured pain with a scale of 0 to 10. The main outcome was the number having at least half their pain reduced; this was 22% with pregabalin 300 to 600 mg, compared with 14% with placebo (high quality evidence). This is a big effect. A lesser effect was also seen with 9% absolute improvement in pain with pregabalin 300 to 600 mg (high quality evidence). Specific side effects were more common with pregabalin than placebo, in particular dizziness, somnolence (sleepiness), weight gain, and peripheral oedema (swelling of feet and ankles), with harm/benefit ratios of 3.7, 7.4, 18, and 19 respectively (high quality evidence). Serious adverse events did not differ between pregabalin and placebo (very low quality evidence), nor did withdrawals due to lack of efficacy (high quality evidence), although there were a few more withdrawals due to adverse events with pregabalin than placebo (high quality evidence). In conclusion, pregabalin 300 to 600 mg reduces pain due to fibromyalgia by a third to a half over 12 to 26 weeks, with tolerable side effects in a small minority, and improves function, sleep, and quality of life. This is comparable to the effects of other medicines approved for the management of fibromyalgia.

This review aimed to evaluate whether giving magnesium sulphate to women at term who are experiencing pre-eclampsia is effective in improving outcomes for their babies. We included one trial involving 135 women with mild pre-eclampsia at term. This trial did not demonstrate any improvement in newborn health outcomes with the use of magnesium sulphate. However, more women experienced side effects such as feeling warm and flushed. Given the importance of this topic, further high-quality studies are required.

The aim of this review was to look at the specific programs that have been set up to help Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people to quit smoking. Four randomised controlled trials were found that included over 2000 Indigenous people. These four trials showed that there was a higher rate of quitting smoking at six months or longer, in people who received treatment to stop smoking, compared to people who did not receive any treatment or were given usual care only. The overall results show that there are successful ways of helping Indigenous people to quit smoking. However, more research is needed using good study designs to strengthen these findings.

The purpose of this Cochrastat review was to find out how well invasive hemodynamic monitoring works in adults in intensive care units. We found 13 randomized controlled trials with 5686 critically ill patients who received either invasive or non-invasive treatment. Most of the studies had problems with design, and half of them were funded by companies that made the devices being studied. We grouped eight of the studies by the type of patient who was enrolled—people undergoing major surgery. We found no difference in the death rate for any of the groups. When we looked at all the studies together, we found that the length of time an individual spent in the intensive care unit and in the hospital was the same for both groups. Hospital costs were higher for the invasive group by an average of $900 per person. We conclude that invasive hemodynamic monitoring does not improve the outcomes for people in intensive care units. This should be taken into account if a person and his or her family are asked to participate in such a study. New, less-invasive monitoring methods need to be compared to the invasive method before they can be used in critical care.

The review found six studies with a total of 478 babies. The pain assessment methods used were diverse and subjective. However, the results of the different scales used correlated with each other. There was a clear difference in pain scale scores between the two procedures, with less pain being experienced by the baby during venepuncture. This finding was consistent whether the baby was given a sweet solution or not. The possibility of a 'learning effect' with repeated venepunctures needs further exploration. Heel lance is a simpler procedure to perform and would be cheaper, but has the potential disadvantage of more pain for the baby.

This review includes two small studies with a total of 52 women. Both studies were conducted in Iran and both looked at the benefits of re-stitching a torn episiotomy wound. One study assessed the wound healing, pain, number of women resuming sexual intercourse and those that were satisfied with the appearance of the wound two months and six months after the repair. The other study only provided data on wound healing at less than four weeks from the repair. There was a trend to better healing, less pain and increased sexual activity at two months and six months in the group that had the episiotomy re-stitched but the results were not statistically significant. More women in the repaired group had resumed sexual intercourse by two months but there was no difference by six months. Neither study gave any information on the effect on breastfeeding. Both studies had weaknesses in their design and the way they were run which could affect the reliability of the findings. We therefore await further evidence from a good quality trial to assess whether there are any benefits with re-stitching broken down perineal wounds.

We included 18 randomised controlled trials with 2521 participants in this review. All 18 RCTs were conducted in China. Four of the included studies received funding from Chinese government departments; two from pharmaceutical companies; and the other 12 did not state their funding sources. Half of the included studies had conflicts of interest. Currently there is no strong evidence for the effectiveness of using Chinese medicinal herbs to prevent or treat influenza, but some herbs may show similar effects to antiviral drugs and some may be superior to antiviral drugs. More high-quality randomised controlled trials are needed.

We searched the literature up to 29 November 2016 and identified eight randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that met our inclusion criteria. These RCTs included a total of 580 patients with HD catheter dysfunction. We do not pool the results of the different studies as each study compares different treatments. Use of clot-dissolving drugs (thrombolytics) to restore catheter function was more effective than placebo (fake infusion), however there was considerable variability in the type of drug, dosage and duration of treatment used, making it difficult to draw conclusions about the best approach. Physical disruption of the blockage using either a wire or a mechanical device was equally effective in unblocking the catheter as using thrombolytics. However, the use of clot-dissolving drugs did not appear to affect the risk of infection or other complications of HD and did not impact on the ability to perform dialysis. In contrast, mechanical disruption of the blockage resulted in a better quality of life and less psychological distress for the patient. Catheter exchange was reported to be superior to sheath disruption with respect to catheter survival. This review found that thrombolytics, physical disruption of the blockage and catheter exchange are equally effective in unblocking a blocked catheter. There is insufficient evidence to suggest which of these approaches is superior with respect to ensuring Dialysis Adequacy or reducing the risk of complications.

This review found that adding one or more drugs to standard treatment regimens can shrink the cancer in more women than standard treatment alone but does not improve overall survival or the length of time before the cancer progresses. Side effects are worse with the addition of drugs to the standard treatment regimen.

The review found some evidence that patients who received NLU were better prepared for discharge from hospital but it was unclear if this was simply a product of an increased length of hospital stay. The number of patients requiring institutional care at discharge was significantly reduced but there was a near significant increase in overall length of hospital stay. The possibility of an increase in early mortality could not be discounted. More research is needed.

We searched for studies that looked at the benefits and harms of using therapeutic ultrasound in adults with CTS. We included 11 studies with a total of 414 participants. Two trials compared therapeutic ultrasound with placebo, two compared one ultrasound regimen with another, and six compared ultrasound as part of a multi-component intervention with another non-surgical intervention (such as exercise and splinting). The duration of the treatments ranged from two weeks to three months. No studies reported on adverse events associated with therapeutic ultrasound and most studies did not report on all outcomes of interest. The quality of the studies was variable, with only two studies judged to be at low risk of bias in all five categories. At present, there is no evidence to support the use of one type of ultrasound regimen over another, or to support the use of therapeutic ultrasound as a treatment with greater efficacy than other non-surgical interventions for CTS, such as splinting, exercises, and oral drugs. More methodologically sound studies are needed to determine the effectiveness and safety of therapeutic ultrasound for CTS.

This review looked at the benefits and harms of statin treatment in people who have already had a stroke or mini-stroke (TIA). We found eight randomised controlled trials which included around 10,000 participants who had an ischaemic stroke or TIA within the previous two to 12 months. We did not include trials of people who had had a bleeding stroke. We found that while there is good evidence that statins reduce the risk of a first heart attack in people with a normal electrocardiogram, their effect on recurrent stroke is less clear. Statins did not reduce the risk of death or sudden death after stroke. However, they did reduce the risk of a major vascular event (stroke, heart attack, or death due to a blood clot) by about one third. Based on these results we would expect that of 1000 patients with a recent ischaemic stroke or TIA, 143 would experience another vascular event if they were not treated with a statin, compared to 94 who would experience another vascular event if they were treated with a statin for five years. It is not clear whether statins should be started immediately after a stroke or TIA, or delayed for some time afterwards. However, since there is a beneficial effect of statins in preventing heart attacks, patients with ischaemic stroke or TIA should be considered to be candidates for statin treatment.

The authors searched the scientific literature for studies evaluating the impact of mass media campaigns on smoking behaviour (prevalence, tobacco use or quit rates) in adults. Eleven randomised and non-randomised controlled trials were identified. Seven of these reported the effect of mass media campaigns on current smoking prevalence and three on tobacco consumption. Eight reported the effect on quitting or reducing the number of cigarettes smoked. Four of these reported the effect on quitting and four on reducing the number of cigarettes smoked. The studies varied in many ways including the type of campaign, the duration of the campaign, the setting, the way the outcome was measured and the time interval between the start of the campaign and the measurement of outcomes. The studies were of mixed quality. Overall, the evidence suggested that mass media campaigns can be effective in changing smoking behaviour in adults. One study continued to show an effect up to eight years after the campaign ended. Another study showed an effect while the campaign was still running. At least one study showed a significant decrease in smoking prevalence as a result of a mass media campaign. No particular characteristics of the campaigns or the populations targeted could be identified that explained whether there was an effect or not.

This review summarises the evidence from 24 randomised controlled trials with a total of 2166 participants. The trials were conducted in various countries and focused on different types of cancer and treatment phases. This review found that yoga may help patients with cancer to improve their quality of life, reduce fatigue and sleep disturbances when compared with no therapy, and may help them to cope with depression and anxiety when compared with psychological/educational interventions. Yoga is not more effective than other exercises in improving quality of life, reducing fatigue or coping with sleep disturbances. Researchers reported no serious side effects from yoga and no studies indicated that yoga is harmful. We could not draw conclusions about the long-term effects of yoga because few studies examined this time point. As yoga is not likely to cause harm and may have positive effects on physical and mental health, we find it reasonable that yoga is offered to people with cancer. However, results should be interpreted with caution as many included studies had some limitations in terms of methodological quality, and the small number of included studies and participants limits the robustness of the results.

We searched multiple sources for studies comparing different timings of imaging after surgery for people with glioma. We included one study with 125 people who had surgery for a type of glioma called glioblastoma. This study compared those who had brain imaging within 48 hours after surgery with those who did not have any early brain imaging. Most people in the study had a full removal of their tumour during surgery followed by radiotherapy and chemotherapy treatment. The results of this study did not show whether any more people who had early brain imaging survived longer than those who did not have early brain imaging. We found no evidence on whether other imaging schedules are better than the current standard follow-up schedule.
