We also found no evidence on how different imaging strategies affect the quality of life of people with glioma. It seems that the current follow-up schedule for people with glioma is based on what is practical rather than what is best. Based on our findings, we recommend that future studies should aim to find out which timing of follow-up brain imaging is best for people with different types of glioma, taking into account how fast the tumour grows, and other factors that can predict survival.

The aim of this review was to compare the effects of chlorpromazine with metiapine for people with schizophrenia. We found three studies that randomised 161 people with schizophrenia to receive either chlorpromazine or metiapine. How well people improved was assessed using the Clinical Global Impression scale and side effects such as Parkinson's disease were also examined. Unfortunately, the information available from these trials was limited and of poor quality, so we could draw no firm conclusions about the effectiveness or safety of these drugs. Chlorpromazine has been used to treat schizophrenia for many years, but there is little reason why metiapine would be better or worse than chlorpromazine and further trials are unlikely.

The aim of this review was to assess the effectiveness of medical therapies for the prevention of postoperative recurrence of Crohn's disease. The medications studied included probiotics, nitroimidazole antibiotics (e.g. metronidazole, tinidazole), mesalamine (a drug containing 5-aminosalicylic acid), immunosuppressive drugs (azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine) and anti-TNF antibody (infliximab). None of the medications were superior to placebo (fake pill) in reducing the risk of postoperative recurrence of Crohn's disease, except for the nitroimidazole antibiotics, mesalamine, and immunosuppressive drugs. Postoperative recurrence of Crohn's disease is a frequent problem that can lead to significant morbidity and mortality. The best medical treatment for preventing this recurrence is unknown. This systematic review found evidence that several treatments (nitroimidazole antibiotics, mesalamine and immunosuppressive therapy with azathioprine or 6-mercaptopurine) are effective in decreasing the risk of postoperative recurrence of Crohn's disease. The decision as to which treatment is best will depend on specific patient characteristics, desired goals of treatment, and healthcare resource availability.

We searched scientific databases and Internet resources to identify randomised controlled trials (where participants are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) published up to July 2015. We identified seven randomised controlled trials with 825 people who used illicit drugs and drank alcohol. The mean age of the participants was 28 years and 77% were men. We looked at five different types of psychosocial interventions: (1) cognitive-behavioural coping skills training; (2) twelve-step programme; (3) brief intervention; (4) motivational interviewing; and (5) brief motivational interviewing. Two studies were considered in two comparisons. The follow-up period ranged from three months to one year. There were no data for the secondary outcome, alcohol-related harm. There was no difference in effectiveness between the different types of psychosocial interventions for reducing alcohol use among people who use illicit drugs. Brief interventions were not superior to assessment-only or to treatment as usual. The overall quality of evidence of the included studies was low to very low. There is insufficient evidence to conclude which type of psychosocial intervention is most effective in reducing alcohol use in people who use illicit drugs.

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing quinine with placebo (a pretend treatment) or another medicine or non-drug intervention used for treating muscle cramps. The evidence is current to August 2014. We found 23 trials with a total of 1586 participants. Fifty-eight per cent of these participants were from five unpublished studies. The participants' ages ranged from 12 to 79 years. Men outnumbered women in the studies. The duration of the trials ranged from one week to 16 weeks. The trials compared quinine with placebo (20 trials), vitamin E (four trials), a quinine-vitamin E combination (three trials), a quinine-theophylline combination (one trial), and xylocaine injections into the gastrocnemius muscle (the calf muscle; one trial). The most commonly used quinine dosage was 300 mg three times a day. We found no new trials when we updated our searches in 2014. The trials had mostly unclear or at-risk of bias in their design and how patients were allocated to treatments. This may mean their results are less reliable. We found that quinine reduced the number of cramps, their intensity, and the number of days with cramps but not their duration. Minor problems that were reported include gastrointestinal symptoms, dizziness, nausea, headache, and rash. Serious problems occurred uncommonly and included anaemia, thrombocytopenia (low blood platelet count), and vomiting. However, with such rare events, larger studies would be needed to get a better idea of whether these are caused by quinine. We could draw no conclusions about the effects of vitamin E, a quinine-vitamin E combination, or a quinine-theophylline combination for treating muscle cramps. Neither were xylocaine injections into the gastrocnemius muscle significantly different to quinine. The overall quality of evidence was low for reducing the number of cramps and moderate for reducing their intensity. For adverse events, we thought the evidence was moderate quality. For serious adverse events, we thought the evidence was low quality. The reason for this was that there were few reports of them and so we couldn't be sure if the observed difference was due to chance or not. We need more studies to be done before we can be certain.

For idiopathic cramps (largely older adults having nocturnal leg cramps), there was no benefit to taking magnesium supplements. One study found a small reduction in cramp frequency and intensity with magnesium and the other did not find any reduction. However, these results are not consistent with each other or with the one other trial in this category which did not show a benefit for magnesium over placebo. Furthermore, the two trials using placebo had different placebos, making them difficult to compare. Overall, it is unlikely that magnesium supplementation provides clinically meaningful cramp prevention in older adults with idiopathic muscle cramps. However, the role of magnesium for pregnant women with rest cramps is less clear and will require further research to resolve this conflict. There were no serious side effects associated with magnesium use and common minor side effects included nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, bloating, flatulence, rash, and weakness. Four of the seven trials were funded by the nutritional council of the food industry. Two of the four trials comparing magnesium to placebo were funded by the nutritional council of the food industry while the other two were publicly funded. The single trial comparing magnesium to no treatment was publicly funded. The two trials comparing magnesium to placebo had mixed funding. One was funded by the nutritional council of the food industry and the other had public and private funding. The overall quality of the evidence was moderate for cramp frequency and intensity and low for cramp duration.

The search is up-to-date to June 2017. One study met the inclusion criteria of the review. The study included 15 adults with spinal muscular atrophy type 3 who were divided into two groups, one receiving home-based exercise training and one given usual care. The study lasted for six months. The people in the exercise group did 2.5 hours of exercise per day, two days a week, and the people in the usual care group maintained their usual level of physical activity. The study evaluated the effect of the exercise intervention on the participant's ability to walk, cardiopulmonary fitness, muscle strength, fatigue, and quality of life. The study reported that there were no differences in any of these areas between the exercise and usual care groups. However, the study was small and the results were not certain. Furthermore, the people in the study knew what they were doing and the person giving them the exercise instructions knew it as well, which may have influenced the study results. We are therefore uncertain about the effects of physical exercise training in people with spinal muscular atrophy type 3 and need further studies to determine if exercise training is safe and effective.

We conducted a review of the literature on the effects of surgical decompression for people with cervical degenerative changes who have symptoms of either a pinched nerve (radiculopathy) or spinal cord (myelopathy). We found two relevant studies, one each from Japan and the USA, which comprised a total of 217 participants. However, both studies had serious methodological problems and were therefore at high risk of bias. Furthermore, many of the study participants were lost to follow-up. The evidence is current to April 2012. One study found that surgery relieved neck pain, shoulder pain, and arm pain more quickly than non-surgical treatments. However, there was no difference in the long term. The other study found no difference in the three-year outcomes of surgery compared with non-surgical treatments for patients with mild myelopathy. Quality of the evidence Overall, the quality of the evidence was low or very low. There is some evidence that surgery may give faster pain relief for patients with cervical radiculopathy, but this result is not reliable. There is no evidence that the short-term risks of surgery are justified by long-term benefits.

The review of trials found that telephone consultation may reduce the number of surgery contacts and out-of-hours visits made by patients to their GPs, without increasing accident and emergency department visits or mortality. However, there are many unanswered questions about the effect of telephone consultation on other aspects of healthcare and more research is needed.

We identified 84 randomised controlled trials (22,872 participants) that had assessed the effects of motivational interviewing (MI) for alcohol in people of all ages. Motivational interviewing is a brief psychological intervention that aims to help people to identify and change their own goals and motivations for changing their behaviour. It has been used to try to reduce the amount and frequency of alcohol consumption and to reduce alcohol-related problems. We looked at studies that compared people who received MI to those who were either not offered any intervention or were offered an alternative intervention. We grouped the studies according to whether they focused on people who drank at levels lower than the recommended daily limits or whether they focused on heavier drinkers. We also grouped them according to whether they measured outcome at four or more months after the intervention, in order to assess whether the intervention had any lasting effect. We found that MI does seem to have some small positive effects on reducing the amount and frequency of alcohol consumption, but these small effects were not found consistently across different types of alcohol outcome and were not found for all subgroups of participants. However, because of concerns about the quality of the research, it cannot be concluded that MI is effective in helping people to reduce the amount and frequency of their alcohol consumption. No studies reported any harm resulting from motivational interviewing. We found that future studies should measure the effects of MI over six months or longer and should measure specific alcohol-related behaviours (such as drink driving or risky drinking behaviour), rather than just general alcohol use or 'problem' drinking.

We found 29 studies including over 2000 adults and children with asthma. Most studies evaluated an intervention that consisted of one or more sessions with a health professional teaching the person how to use the inhaler properly. Other studies looked at the use of computers, DVDs or mobile phone apps to teach inhaler technique, while others used devices to give feedback on the technique being used. We found that it was difficult to compare studies that used different ways to measure technique, so we classified them according to whether they used face-to-face training, multi-media delivery (e.g. DVD, computer or game) or a device to give feedback on the technique being used. Studies differed in many ways, including the severity of asthma among participants. Overall, we found that people learned how to use their inhalers better when trained using either method. However, we found little evidence that improving inhaler technique led to better asthma control or quality of life. This may be because even small improvements in technique can make a big difference, and we couldn't rule out this possibility despite seeing some studies where people in the intervention group didn't do better than those in the control group. We also found no evidence about whether training people in inhaler technique caused any harm. The overall quality of evidence was low to moderate. This was mainly because many of the studies were carried out badly or didn't report important information, and we couldn't combine many of the results. Also, many of the studies were small, which means that chance could have played a role in whether people in the intervention group did better than those in the control group.

The review identified three trials, involving a total of 226 participants. No difference in the risk of death and dependency was found; however, the results were imprecise. There was little information on side effects. There was not enough evidence to support the routine use of mannitol in acute stroke patients. Further trials are needed to find out if mannitol is helpful in acute stroke.
This review shows that D-penicillamine improves disease activity in rheumatoid arthritis. Its effect is comparable to other anti-rheumatic drugs, but it has more adverse effects.

The purpose of this systematic review was to assess whether these herbs are beneficial in reducing the incidence and severity of chemotherapy-induced toxicity. Four studies met the inclusion criteria of this review. Three of the studies compared the effects of chemotherapy plus Huangqi decoctions to chemotherapy alone; in the fourth study, the effects of Huangqi decoctions were compared to those of two other Chinese herbal interventions. The results showed that patients receiving Huangqi decoctions along with chemotherapy had less nausea and vomiting, fewer low white blood cell counts, and increased levels of some components of their immune system. However, these conclusions are tentative because of the poor quality of the studies.

The purpose of this review was to determine if vitamin A or DHA or both in combination would slow the course of disease in retinitis pigmentosa (RP). We found three eligible randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that enrolled a total of 866 participants from four to 55 years of age. Each study used different treatments - one studied vitamin A alone, one studied DHA alone, and one studied vitamins A and DHA compared to vitamin A alone. These studies followed participants for two, five, or seven years. None of the RCTs reported any benefit of vitamin A or DHA or both in slowing the progression of RP. Two of the three RCTs reported improvements in electroretinography (ERG) amplitudes among some subgroups of participants, but these results have not been replicated or confirmed by the other trials. No toxic effects or adverse events were reported in these three trials. Based on the results of the three RCTs included in this review, there is no clear evidence that vitamin A and/or DHA are effective in slowing the progression of RP.

We searched the literature up to 29 May 2013 and found three studies that met our inclusion criteria. These included a total of 414 people who were at risk of job loss. The majority had rheumatoid arthritis but some had ankylosing spondylitis. The interventions in all three studies were directed at the worker and aimed to help them to keep their job. They consisted of work changes or adaptations and person-directed interventions such as vocational counselling, advice or education. There were few interventions directed at the work environment such as workplace visits (one study) or any actions by an occupational physician (one study). The duration of the interventions varied from two 1.5-hour sessions (one study) to six to eight sessions over six months (one study). Two studies investigated job loss, one studied both job loss and work absenteeism, and one studied work absenteeism and work functioning. We found that job loss was significantly reduced in two studies, but not in the third. In the largest study, which also looked at work absenteeism, the number of sick days at six months was similar in the intervention and control groups. Finally, in the smallest study, work functioning improved moderately in the group that received the intervention compared to the control group at six months. We found no information on adverse events. Overall, we thought the quality of the evidence was very low. There were problems with the design of all three studies.

We found three completed studies that were eligible for inclusion in this review; these included a total of 285 newborns, 140 of whom received arginine. The overall methodologic quality of the included studies was good. Studies were published between 2005 and 2010; two were conducted in the USA and one in India. All three studies randomly assigned healthy preterm infants to receive either arginine supplementation or no arginine (placebo or 'dummy' treatment). One study also included a third treatment group that received L-arginine infusion. These studies used different doses of arginine (30 mg/kg, 50 mg/kg and 80 mg/kg per day, given via vein); two studies gave daily treatments for five days and one gave treatment for 10 days. We found that arginine reduced the risk of developing NEC overall, as well as the risk of developing severe NEC (stages 2 and 3). No side effects were noted. However, because information came from just three small trials, there is currently insufficient evidence to support the use of arginine to prevent NEC. Larger studies are needed to provide more guidance.

We found four studies involving 1943 patients with acute sinusitis. These were all well-designed randomized controlled trials comparing INCS with placebo or no intervention for 15 or 21 days. Two of the studies were conducted in the USA, one in Canada and one in Australia. The mean age of the patients was 34 years and 61% were female. The primary outcome was the resolution or improvement of symptoms at day 14 to 16. When we combined the results from the three trials included in the meta-analysis, participants receiving INCS were more likely to experience resolution or improvement in symptoms than those receiving placebo (73% versus 66.4%). However, this was a modest benefit and the results suggest that some people may not experience improvement in their symptoms. No significant adverse effects were reported and there was no significant difference in the drop-out and recurrence rates for the two treatment groups. Limitations of the review include the fact that only a small number of trials were found and that these were conducted in different populations.

The review of 10 small trials found that giving all babies a course of vancomycin immediately after birth reduced the number of infections. However, there are concerns about the development of more drug resistant bacteria that could cause problems for these babies and others. There appear to be no other serious side effects.

The review authors searched the medical literature and identified 14 relevant studies. The quality of these studies was low. The authors concluded that there is no evidence from randomised controlled trials to show which dressings or topical agents promote the best healing of wounds healing by secondary intention. However, foam is a suitable alternative to gauze and may be better for patients in terms of pain and satisfaction. Plaster casts seem better than elastic compression for healing amputation stumps.

Until 2014, 11 randomized controlled trials had studied birth control methods with hormones and breastfeeding. They included 1482 women. We grouped them by type of hormone delivery: 4 used birth control pills, 3 an implant, 2 a different hormonal intrauterine device (IUD), and 2 pills with only the hormone progestin. Studies lasted from six weeks to one year. In five trials, women knew which treatment they got. The others didn’t know. Also, half the women in five trials got the treatment under investigation, and the others got a comparison treatment or a placebo. The main outcomes were how long women breastfed their babies and how much milk they produced. We also looked at the babies’ growth. Two of eight trials reported a shorter breastfeeding duration for the women on hormonal treatment versus those on another treatment or with no treatment. Three trials studying the same treatments did not show a difference. One showed a decrease in breastfeeding at three months for the hormonal IUD group versus the nonhormonal IUD group but no difference at one year. Two older studies reported a lower breast milk volume for the birth control pill group versus the placebo group. The others did not show a difference in breast milk volume or composition. For the babies’ growth, one trial showed greater weight gain for the implant group versus no treatment but less versus another hormone treatment. The others did not show a clear difference. Overall, the evidence was not strong for any particular hormonal method. The studies differed in how well they were done, with most not reporting exactly what was said it would be measured. Also, results were not similar across studies.

The authors searched for studies evaluating the impact of red-light cameras on crash rates at intersections. They found 10 studies from Australia, Singapore and the USA, but none were randomised controlled trials. The authors divided the studies according to how well they were designed to avoid drawing wrong conclusions because of natural fluctuations in crash rates. The studies showed varying results for different types of crashes and violations, but the best-designed studies all found that red-light cameras reduced total casualties. The authors conclude that red-light cameras are effective in reducing total casualties, but more good research is needed to be sure of this.

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing different warfarin dosing strategies. We looked at both new and ongoing trials. The evidence is current up to May 2016. We found four randomized controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) involving 494 participants. Three studies compared a 10-mg warfarin nomogram with a 5-mg warfarin nomogram; one study compared a 10-mg warfarin nomogram with a placebo (a pretend treatment). The three studies that compared a 10-mg warfarin nomogram with a 5-mg warfarin nomogram were conducted in Canada, France, and China; the fourth study was conducted in the USA. Considerable uncertainty remains as to which dose of warfarin is better. This is due to differences in the types of study participants and the length of time that participants were followed.

The evidence on which this review is based was current as of 7 January 2019. We found seven studies with a total of 555 people with dementia or cognitive impairment who had sustained a hip fracture. The average age of participants in three studies was over 80 years and 70% of participants were female. We categorised two studies as providing enhanced care in the inpatient setting, two as providing enhanced care both in hospital and in the community, and three as geriatrician-led care in hospital. The length of follow-up was from the point of acute hospital discharge to 24 months later. We found that some of the ways in which care was reorganised showed some benefit. For example, those who received some form of enhanced care seemed less likely to experience delirium (a state of general illness and confusion) and to have a better outcome in terms of their ability to function independently. However, the certainty of this evidence was low. We were not able to make any conclusions about the effects of the different types of care on activities of daily living and functional performance, as the certainty of the evidence was very low. No study measured quality of life. We consider that further research is needed to determine the best way to provide care for people with dementia who sustain a hip fracture. Currently, there are two large trials ongoing that aim to improve the care provided to people with dementia who have a hip fracture. We suggest that these trials should include measures of quality of life and costs.

The review authors searched the literature and identified seven randomised controlled trials with 840 participants. There were a variety of interventions, outcomes measured and populations being cared for (children with acute and chronic illnesses). The results showed that there is some evidence that children may be discharged earlier from hospital and that this needs further exploration. There were some positive effects on parental anxiety, child behaviour, parental satisfaction, coping and family functioning but these were reported in single studies. In two studies, home care was reported as more costly for service providers but with substantial cost savings for the family. Overall, the current research does not provide any clear evidence of reduced access to hospital services or reduced hospital readmission for children receiving home-based nursing services but there is some evidence for a reduction in length of hospitalisation.

We searched for all the available research up to September 2016 and found 40 studies with over 7500 participants who received either intensive case management or standard care or another type of case management. We found that Intensive Case Management is better than standard care in reducing the number of days spent in hospital and in keeping people in treatment. We also found that Intensive Case Management may help improve people's social functioning, although we are not sure if it has an effect on their mental health. Furthermore, we found that Intensive Case Management may be more effective in reducing hospital use for people who have used services a lot in the past. We also looked at how well the Intensive Case Management teams worked in relation to the Action Plan Intervention (ACT), which is the theoretical basis of Intensive Case Management. We found that the better the Intensive Case Management teams followed the ACT principles, the fewer days the people they managed spent in hospital. Overall, we believe that Intensive Case Management is better than standard care for people with severe mental illness, especially for those with high levels of hospital use. We also believe that Intensive Case Management is at least as good as other types of case management. However, we still do not know how Intensive Case Management compares to non-case management approaches. We recommend that further research is carried out to compare non-case management interventions to standard care.)

We searched for all randomized controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-RCTs published up to July 2015. We included 25 trials in the review (16 trials, 464 participants for box model training versus no training; 14 trials, 382 participants for different methods of box model training). There were no trials comparing box model training versus animal model or cadaveric model training. All the 16 trials in the comparison of box model training versus no training used video trainers. When compared with no training, box model training was superior in all aspects of skill acquisition (time taken for task completion, error score, accuracy score, and overall performance score). However, none of the other outcomes such as death, complications, and trainee satisfaction were reported. Different methods of box model training were compared in 14 trials. There was no significant difference in the time taken for task completion between the different methods of box model training. The error score did not differ between any of the comparisons. The accuracy score was better with Z-maze box training than U-maze box training, and the overall performance score was better with a simple cardboard box trainer than a conventional pelvic trainer, and with reverse alignment than forward alignment box training. However, the other outcomes were not reported. Overall, we found that box model training is superior to no training for learning basic laparoscopic skills. There appears to be no significant differences in the skill acquisition between different methods of box model training. However, the results of this review are not without limitations. First, most of the trials were of low methodological quality, which may lead to misleading results. Second, the effect of box model training on patient outcomes is unknown. Third, the generalizability of the findings to real-world situations is unknown. Therefore, further well-designed trials are necessary to confirm our findings.

The authors found two small randomised clinical trials. One compared biliary lavage with hydrocortisone versus saline and the other budesonide versus prednisone. Both studies were stopped because of serious side effects (the first one because of pancreatitis, cholangitis with septicaemia and psychotic symptoms; the second one because of elevated liver enzymes). None of the investigated drugs showed beneficial effects for the patients.

This review found that early feeding may help to reduce the risk of dying and becoming disabled. More research is needed to confirm this finding. Future studies should assess both nutrition-related outcomes as well as those that reflect the overall severity of illness, such as death and disability.

We found 57 studies with over 34,000 participants. On average, people receiving digital interventions reduced their alcohol consumption by up to three drinks per week more than people who received no or minimal help. Digital interventions had little or no impact on days of heavy drinking or binge drinking sessions compared to no or minimal interventions. When we compared digital interventions with face-to-face interventions, there was little or no difference in impact on alcohol use. Studies mostly did not report if there were any harms caused to participants. The most common types of digital interventions were programmes that helped people set goals, offered feedback on their alcohol use, or taught them to solve problems related to alcohol. More research is needed to find out which type of digital programme works best to help specific groups of people. It would also help to know how such programmes could be used alongside other ways of helping people with alcohol use. We judged the quality of evidence for the overall impact of digital interventions to be moderate, meaning that while there is some confidence in these results, there are risks of bias that might change our conclusions if we had more information. For the comparison of digital versus face-to-face interventions, we judged the evidence to be low quality because of the small number of studies.

The aim of this review was to compare the efficacy and tolerability of benzodiazepines with those of placebo (pretend treatment) in the short-term treatment of panic disorder. Short-term treatment was defined as treatment lasting until the end of the second week after the start of treatment. We found 24 studies with a total of 4233 participants who could be included in the review. All of them received some kind of treatment in addition to their usual care. Our main findings are based on 2124 participants who were randomly assigned to receive either benzodiazepines or placebo. More people who received benzodiazepines improved their anxiety symptoms during the short-term treatment than those who received placebo. We also found that fewer people who took benzodiazepines dropped out of the studies than those who took placebo. However, our findings about the usefulness of benzodiazepines are limited because the included studies had several shortcomings in their design and reporting. For example, it is likely that the participants knew whether they were taking benzodiazepines or placebo, and this knowledge may have influenced the results. In addition, the studies were too short to be able to draw any conclusions about the long-term benefits and harms of benzodiazepines. Furthermore, the results of the present review are relevant only for individuals who are likely to experience a panic attack as the main symptom of their disorder. This review does not provide information about the efficacy and tolerability of benzodiazepines for panic disorder with agoraphobia, where panic attacks are less prominent. Future studies should be designed to avoid the problems in the existing literature and should report how participants were masked to the treatment they received. They should also test the long-term efficacy and safety of benzodiazepines, including the risk of dependence and withdrawal symptoms.

This review examined the effects of tai chi on cardiovascular disease risk factors including blood pressure, lipid levels, body mass index (BMI; measure of overweight/obesity) and waist circumference, and all-cause and cardiovascular mortality. We searched databases up to November 2013 and identified 13 completed randomised controlled trials (RCTs; clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment group) involving 1520 participants. The duration of the interventions in the included studies ranged from four weeks to one year, and age ranges of participants varied from 60 to 85 years. Half of the included studies recruited healthy participants and the other half recruited participants with pre-hypertension (blood pressure just below that considered to be hypertension or high blood pressure), elderly people who were considered to be at risk of falling, or people with hypertension with liver and/or kidney problems. No studies reported on cardiovascular mortality, all-cause mortality or non-fatal endpoints. Most studies did not report on adverse events, costs or the occurrence of type 2 diabetes. The effect of tai chi on blood pressure was inconsistent: nine trials found reductions in systolic blood pressure (the top number of a blood pressure reading; reductions ranged from -22.0 mmHg to -11.5 mmHg) and diastolic blood pressure (the bottom number of a blood pressure reading; reductions ranged from -12.2 mmHg to -4.43 mmHg), two trials found no clear evidence of a difference, and one trial found an increase in systolic blood pressure with tai chi (increase 5.2 mmHg). A similar pattern was seen for diastolic blood pressure. Three trials reported lipid levels and two found reductions in total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol (the 'bad' cholesterol) and triglycerides (a blood lipid; reductions in total cholesterol ranged from -1.30 mmol/L to -0.50 mmol/L; in low-density lipoprotein-cholesterol ranged from -0.76 mmol/L to -0.59 mmol/L; in triglycerides ranged from -0.46 mmol/L to -0.37 mmol/L), and increased high-density lipoprotein-cholesterol (the 'good' cholesterol; increases ranged from 0.61 mmol/L to 0.16 mmol/L)), while the third study found no clear evidence of a difference between groups on lipid levels. One study found that tai chi improved quality of life at three months. Overall, there was some suggestion that tai chi has beneficial effects on cardiovascular disease risk factors; however these results were not consistent across all studies. More longer-term RCTs are needed to enable us to draw firm conclusions.

We identified 27 randomised controlled trials (studies where schools were randomly allocated to receive a particular intervention or to a control) looking at the effects of family interventions aimed at preventing young people from taking up smoking. The family interventions generally aimed to improve parent-child relationships and to help parents set rules and monitor their child's behaviour. They often included teaching skills such as communication, problem solving and refusals skills, and coping with peer pressure. The family interventions were designed to be as attractive and relevant as possible for teenagers and often involved multiple sessions over several months. We found that family-based programmes may be effective in preventing young people from starting to smoke. The best-quality evidence was for high intensity programmes which focused on helping parents to be more authoritative (set clear rules, show interest and care for the adolescent, and use reasoning rather than punishment). For these high intensity programmes, we estimated that family-based interventions might reduce uptake or experimentation with smoking by between 16 and 32%. The overall quality of evidence was moderate, meaning that while there was evidence that family-based programmes are likely to be more effective than no intervention, there were not enough studies to be sure of this.

We searched the literature up to 25 October 2016 and found three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that met the inclusion criteria. These RCTs enrolled 451 adults with cancer, but not receiving active treatment (i.e. no chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or surgery), who developed nausea and vomiting. Two studies compared dexamethasone to a placebo, and the third study compared a number of additional treatments, including metoclopramide, chlorpromazine, tropisetron, and dexamethasone. The length of time that participants were followed up after starting the medication ranged from seven to 14 days. We were able to combine the results from two studies (127 people) that measured the intensity of nausea on day eight. People taking dexamethasone had less nausea than those taking a placebo, but the results were imprecise. Side effects were not more common with either dexamethasone or placebo, and the interventions were generally well tolerated. Overall, we found very low-quality evidence that did not support or refute the use of corticosteroids for nausea and vomiting in adults with cancer not receiving active treatment.

We identified 10 randomised controlled trials enrolling 1936 children. Three included children with HIV infection (1345 children), four with cystic fibrosis (429), one with sickle cell disease (219), one with cancer (160) and one group of low birth weight neonates with underlying respiratory disorders (40). We rated the evidence from studies including children with HIV, cystic fibrosis or cancer as moderate quality. Due to insufficient information we could not assess the quality of the evidence in two studies: one in children with sickle cell disease (low risk of bias), and another in low birth weight neonates with underlying respiratory disorders (high risk of bias). There was no difference in the rate of pulmonary tuberculosis in HIV-infected children during isoniazid prophylaxis. There was no effect on mortality with co-trimoxazole or isoniazid prophylaxis; however, there was a reduction in mortality with co-trimoxazole in one study. One study showed a reduction in hospital admissions with co-trimoxazole. There was no significant difference in Pseudomonas infections in two studies of children with cystic fibrosis receiving ciprofloxacin. Two studies in children with cystic fibrosis found a reduction in pulmonary exacerbations with azithromycin. The effect of antibiotics on growth in children with cystic fibrosis was inconsistent. There was an increased risk of emergence of pathogenic strains with either azithromycin or ciprofloxacin. There was no significant difference in the quality of life (one study). There was no significant increase in the frequency of adverse events with either azithromycin or ciprofloxacin and no evidence of increased antibiotic resistance. In children with sickle cell disease, there was a lower rate of Streptococcus pneumoniae septicaemia with penicillin V. In children with cancer, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole reduced Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia. There was no significant increase in the frequency of adverse events with penicillin V or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. In infants with a low birth weight with underlying respiratory disorders, there was no difference in the rate of pulmonary infection with vancomycin. None of the studies reported time off school or carer time off work. Antibiotics for preventing lung disease in high-risk children are not always beneficial and should be prescribed carefully by clinicians experienced in the management of the underlying condition. The limited number of studies in some categories of high-risk children means that more clinical trials are needed to determine the effectiveness of antibiotics for preventing lung disease in these groups.

We identified nine randomised controlled trials, with a total of 519 participants. There were three trials comparing nitrous oxide with carbon dioxide, five trials comparing helium with carbon dioxide, and one trial comparing room air with carbon dioxide. The choice of gas was determined by a method similar to the toss of a coin so that the treatments were conducted in a random order. The participants knew which gas they were breathing and the anaesthetist and surgeon were also aware of the gas being used. This may have influenced the results as it is possible that the expectation of feeling better may affect how people assess their pain and side effects. Two of the trials were funded by an institutional grant and one by an industrial sponsor. The other trials did not mention the source of funding. Insufficient evidence was available to show any differences between nitrous oxide and carbon dioxide on complications affecting the heart or lungs, or on aspects of surgery performance. There were no major side effects associated with either nitrous oxide or carbon dioxide. However, the quality of the evidence for both nitrous oxide and helium was very low. One trial found that room air cost less than carbon dioxide to use as the filling gas, and caused less pain on the first day after surgery, but the quality of the evidence was very low. Further trials are needed to strengthen the evidence. All of the trials had some limitations in terms of how they were conducted, and there was relatively small pool of participants.

Study characteristics: We found 14 randomized controlled studies. All of them investigated the efficacy of dexamethasone; none assessed other corticosteroids. While there were several studies comparing different dosages, there were only four studies comparing a'standard' versus a 'high' dosage regimen, six studies comparing a'moderate' versus a 'low' dosage and one study comparing a 'pulse' (given at intervals) versus a continuous dosage regimen. One study compared a participant-individualized course with a standard course. In addition, there were four studies comparing the start of therapy at five days of age or earlier with a start of therapy at around the 10th day of life. Most of the studies had relatively few participants. Outcomes: We found that a high dosage regimen compared with a moderate dosage regimen increased the risk of BPD and abnormal neurodevelopment. However, we also found that a low dosage regimen compared with a moderate dosage regimen had no effect on BPD or abnormal development. The risk of death was not significantly different with either a high- or a moderate dosage regimen but was increased with a low dosage regimen. The results regarding the influence of the timing of the start of therapy are inconclusive. Based on the currently available evidence, there is no optimal dosage, timing or duration of postnatal corticosteroid therapy that prevents BPD with the least amount of side effects. Quality of the evidence: Overall, the quality of the evidence was low or very low. All studies were small, and there were differences in study populations and designs. For most comparisons, the number of studies was small, which limits our confidence in the results.

This review summarises trials evaluating the effectiveness of drugs proven to have anti-inflammatory activity in preventing carditis (inflammation of the heart muscle) in people having an attack of acute rheumatic fever. We found eight studies in which 996 adults and children were randomly assigned to receive either corticosteroids, intravenous immunoglobulins or placebo (a pretend treatment). Six of the studies took place between 1950 and 1965; the other two were done in 1990 and 2001. Corticosteroids did not prevent heart disease when compared with aspirin in six studies involving 907 people. Use of prednisone compared with aspirin also failed to reduce the risk of heart disease after one year in two studies with 212 people. Five of the studies did not report on side effects; the three that did reported on side effects noted substantial side effects. However, these studies were conducted many years ago and the way they were done may not be acceptable by today's standards. It is possible that new drugs or new ways of using existing drugs might be more effective in reducing the risk of heart disease in people having an attack of acute rheumatic fever.

This review looked at whether improving the environment could help to reduce the risk of getting trachoma. We included six randomised controlled trials with a total of 12,294 participants from 79 communities. The studies we included were of variable quality. The results showed that spraying houses with insecticides reduced the presence of flies and also reduced the risk of trachoma by 56% to 61%. A second study also found a similar effect of using insecticides. However, another study did not find that insecticides reduced trachoma. Provision of latrines did not reduce trachoma in two studies, but a third study suggested that it may be effective. Health education alone may be effective in reducing trachoma, but two studies found that health education did not significantly reduce trachoma. Overall, the evidence for the effectiveness of improved environmental sanitation in the control of trachoma is poor. More research is needed to assess the impact of environmental improvements on trachoma.

This review examined the effects of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) in adults with chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS), also known as myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME). Fifteen studies met inclusion criteria with 1043 CFS participants. The main comparison was between CBT and usual medical care. Other comparisons were CBT versus other psychological therapies and CBT combined with other interventions versus usual care. The findings indicate that CBT is more effective than usual medical care in improving fatigue symptoms immediately following treatment. CBT may also be more effective than other psychological therapies in improving fatigue symptoms immediately following treatment, but there are too few studies to be sure. There is no evidence about whether these effects last over time. There is also insufficient evidence about the effects of CBT when it is used in combination with other treatments, or when it is compared with other psychological therapies. More research is needed if we are to be confident that CBT is the most effective way to treat CFS.

We searched for trials on 22 September 2015. We found one trial with 39 people taking part. All participants had sickle cell disease. One group was given vitamin D, the other was given a placebo (a substance containing no medication). Neither group knew whether they were getting vitamin D or placebo. The people in the study took the vitamin or placebo by mouth every two weeks for six weeks then monthly thereafter. The study lasted for six months. The average age of the people in the study was 28 years and 77% were male. The study did not report on any funding sources. The study reported on vitamin D levels in the blood and said these were better in the vitamin D group than in the placebo group after three months, four months and five months. Pain episodes were fewer in the vitamin D group than in the placebo group. However, the study also reported on side effects and quality of life and the results were uncertain. The study had some limitations which might affect our trust in the results, for example, not all participants completed the study. We could not draw any conclusions from the limited evidence in this review. More high quality studies are needed to be carried out to determine the benefits and harms of vitamin D supplementation in people with sickle cell disease. We considered the quality of the evidence for each outcome (vitamin D levels in the blood, pain, side effects and quality of life) to be moderate, low or very low. This was because of the small number of participants in the study and the high drop out rate.

We found one study with 26 children from 22 different schools in New South Wales, Australia. The children were aged between 4 and 12 years. They had CAS diagnosed by a speech-language pathologist (SLP), based on a comprehensive assessment, and they had no known cause for their difficulties with speech. The children were divided into two groups, one group received the Nuffield Dyspraxia Programme-3 (NDP-3) and one group received the Rapid Syllable Transitions Treatment (ReST). The study took place in a university clinic. SLP students, under the supervision of an experienced clinician, delivered the treatment in one-hour sessions, four days a week for three weeks. The treatments focused on the production of individual sounds and sound blends, as well as speech consistency and connected speech. The children's progress was assessed before treatment, immediately after treatment, one month after treatment and four months after treatment. However, nine out of 13 children in each group had started seeing their usual SLP between one and four months post-intervention, so we couldn't be sure if any maintained gains made during the intervention. We looked at the results at one month post-intervention. At this time point, there were small improvements in speech accuracy on both treated and untreated words, speech production consistency and connected speech for both treatments. No study assessed function in everyday situations. We didn't find any differences between the two studies in terms of accuracy of production on treated words, speech production consistency and accuracy of connected speech. In contrast, we found that ReST produced slightly better accuracy of production on non-treated words. Neither treatment led to any adverse effects. We found only one study of poor quality evidence, which only included children with CAS of unknown cause. We graded the quality of the evidence as moderate because there was only one study.

We included four randomised controlled trials with a total of 268 participants. All four studies gave participants antibiotics orally. One study conducted in Brazil included both adults and children and compared trimethoprim-sulfamexacol to no treatment over 20 months. The other three studies compared antibiotics to a placebo. One study conducted in the US in adults studied pyrimethamine-trisulfapyrimidine for eight weeks, one study conducted in the UK in children and adults evaluated pyrimethamine for four weeks, and one study conducted in Brazil in adults investigated trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 12 months. In the last study, all participants had active retinochoroiditis and had been treated with antibiotics for 45 days prior to taking part in the trial. Only the study in Brazil of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 12 months, in participants whose lesions had healed, reported the effect of treatment on visual acuity. People treated with antibiotics may have a similar change in visual acuity compared with people treated with placebo after one year. Treatment with antibiotics probably reduces the risk of having another episode of retinochoroiditis, with similar results for acute and chronic retinochoroiditis. The UK study of pyrimethamine for four weeks reported an improvement in inflammation within the eye in treated participants, while the Brazilian study noted that the severity of inflammation was higher in the placebo group. As the Brazilian study used two different treatments, we cannot attribute this difference to the placebo alone. In the US study of pyrimethamine-trisulfapyrimidine for eight weeks, inflammation had almost completely resolved by eight weeks; however, all participants also received steroids, which can mask the effects of antibiotics. Two studies reported an increased risk of side effects in treated participants. These were a fall in red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, nausea, loss of appetite, rash, and arthralgia. Treatment with antibiotics probably reduces the risk of having another episode of toxoplasma retinochoroiditis, but there is currently no good evidence that this leads to better visual outcomes. However, absence of evidence of effect is not the same as evidence of no effect. Further trials of people with acute and chronic toxoplasma retinochoroiditis affecting any part of the retina are required to determine the effects of antibiotic treatment on visual outcomes. The quality of the evidence for our primary outcome of visual acuity was low. This is because there were only two studies of visual acuity and one of these did not report how many participants were recruited or how long it took to conduct the study. The quality of the evidence for our secondary outcomes of recurrence and inflammation was moderate. The studies did not report whether or not they had obtained written informed consent from all participants before they were given treatment. Three studies did not report funding sources or how much the study cost to conduct. One study mentioned that the manufacturer provided the medication but did not state if this meant that the manufacturer contributed financially to the study.

This review included 43 randomised controlled trials. Most of the people who took part were women, with an average age of around 80 years. There were seven comparisons of different implants. We concluded that the sliding hip screw appeared to be the better device when compared with the Gamma nail. More people who had the Gamma nail had their fracture reoperated on, and the Gamma nail was more likely to fail than the sliding hip screw. The two implants had similar numbers of wound infections and deaths. We also looked at whether any of the other implants were better than the sliding hip screw. Fewer people who had an intramedullary nail had problems with fixation than those who had a fixed nail plate, especially for fractures that were below the greater trochanter. However, these results need to be interpreted cautiously. There were relatively few studies, and some of them used different ways to measure complication rates. Also, there were no studies that compared the newer types of intramedullary nails with the older ones.

We searched databases in April 2014 for trials of patients with advanced ovarian cancer and inoperable bowel obstruction. One trial was found. It compared surgery to the control group who received octreotide (a drug). The trial only included patients who were well enough for an operation. The trial found that the risk of dying within 30 days of the procedure was 15% for the surgery group and 5% for the control group with no difference in any other complication rates between the groups. Multivariate analysis found that surgery improved survival but did not specify how much longer the patients who had surgery survived compared to those who did not. The trial did not report quality of life. Therefore, we could not draw any firm conclusions as to which treatment was better, but there was some evidence that surgery might help people to live longer.

The review found four randomised controlled trials of 244 women with PCOS who received statin therapy for 12 weeks or six weeks. Two studies looked at the effects of simvastatin and two at the effects of atorvastatin. The review found no good evidence that statins improve menstrual regularity or spontaneous ovulation, either alone or in combination with the birth control pill. Nor were there any significant effects on weight or hirsutism (excessive hair growth in females). However, statins did lower androgen (male hormone) levels when used alone or with the birth control pill. Statins also improved total cholesterol, LDL and triglycerides but had no significant effect on HDL levels, C-reactive protein, fasting insulin or homeostatic model assessment (HOMA) insulin resistance. No serious side effects from the use of statins were reported in any of the included studies. More research needs to be done in this area.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared different interventions to manage heavy vaginal bleeding (menorrhagia, postmenopausal haemorrhage (postmenopause)) in women with cervical cancer. We also looked at non-randomised studies (observational studies) that reported outcomes of women treated with each of these interventions. We wanted to look at both the effect/aetiology of the treatment and the impact on quality of life (QoL). The evidence is current to April 2015. The review authors found no RCTs that compared different interventions for controlling vaginal bleeding in women with cervical cancer. They found several observational studies reporting on the effectiveness of different treatments, including formalin soaked pack, interventional radiology, and radiotherapy. However, the source of funding was not clear in most of the studies. None of the studies reported whether participants had an advanced stage of cervical cancer. In this review, we did not find any evidence from RCTs to support or refute any of the proposed interventions compared with radiotherapy. Therefore, the choice of intervention will be based on local resources. Radiotherapy techniques for managing vaginal bleeding are not readily available in resource-poor settings, where advanced cases of cervical cancer are predominant. This review has shown the need for a randomised controlled trial assessing the benefits and risks of palliative treatments for vaginal bleeding in women with advanced cervical cancer.

We searched the literature up to 30th April 2013 and we identified randomised controlled trials (RCT) comparing the efficacy and safety of temozolomide with other treatments in newly diagnosed and recurrent HGG. We found that temozolomide combined with radiotherapy is more effective than radiotherapy alone in terms of overall survival and progression-free survival. There are acceptable toxicities associated with this treatment which include fatigue, infection and haematological complications. On the other hand, temozolomide is not superior to standard chemotherapy on overall survival but it is more effective in terms of progression-free survival in patients with recurrent GBM. Furthermore, it may be associated with better quality of life than standard chemotherapy in this setting. In the elderly, temozolomide is as effective and tolerable as radiotherapy alone.

We included two studies. One study conducted in Cambodia suggests that contracting with nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) for the management of health systems may improve access to healthcare services. After contracting with NGOs, there was an increase of 28% in the number of pregnant women attending antenatal care and 14% increase in the use of public facilities. However, no improvement was observed in population health outcomes. The second study conducted in three countries in Latin America found that managers who received intermittent training over a period of 18 months had better performance than those who did not receive the training. We conclude that more research is needed to assess the impact of different interventions to improve the recruitment and retention of district health systems managers in low- and middle-income countries.

The authors searched the databases until October 2014. We included three studies that compared surgical treatment with non-surgical treatment. The studies involved 123 people aged between 18 and 65 years. The cause of thoracoplasty was tuberculosis in two studies and traumatic chest wall injury in one study. The follow-up period ranged from 12 months to two years. Death occurred in two studies, and the causes of death were related to the disease process or the complications developed during treatment. There was no clear evidence of a difference in mortality between treatment groups. Six out of 123 people enrolled in the three studies died. Three studies reported on pneumonia, and there was some evidence that surgery reduced the risk of pneumonia. Two studies reported on chest deformity, and there was some evidence that surgery reduced the risk of chest deformity. Two studies reported on tracheostomy, and there was some evidence that surgery reduced the need for a tracheostomy. Each study measured the outcomes in different ways, so we could not combine the results. There was some evidence from three small studies that showed surgical treatment was preferable to non-surgical management in reducing pneumonia, chest deformity, and tracheostomy. The quality of the evidence was low to moderate.

We found 50 studies that measured the effect of cerivastatin on cholesterol in 12,877 participants. These people were randomly assigned to receive cerivastatin or a placebo (a dummy pill which looks like cerivastatin but has no known effects) for a period of three to 12 weeks. Some of the studies also included comparisons with other statins. The main outcomes were the amount of reduction in different types of cholesterol and whether there were side effects. We only looked at studies where it was decided at random who received cerivastatin and who received the placebo or another statin, as this is considered the best way to evaluate the true effect of a drug. We assessed the quality of all relevant studies and graded the evidence as high, moderate, low or very low. We found that cerivastatin was highly effective at reducing the amounts of LDL cholesterol, total cholesterol and triglycerides in the blood. At the higher doses used clinically, cerivastatin reduced LDL cholesterol more effectively than fluvastatin, atorvastatin or rosuvastatin. However, we did not have enough information from the studies to get a good estimate of whether cerivastatin caused side effects such as muscle pain. The studies were only of short duration and nearly half of them did not report on the occurrence of side effects.

We searched the literature up to 22 September 2015 and tried to contact the authors of five unpublished studies to obtain more information about their studies. We included 28 studies which randomised 6851 patients who had heart or blood vessel surgery. We found that remote ischaemic preconditioning by inflating a blood pressure cuff on the upper arm made little or no difference to the level of kidney function after surgery. We did not find any differences in the need for dialysis, length of hospital stay, or death. The evidence showed that remote ischaemic preconditioning may make little or no difference to the chance of suffering an acute kidney injury. Some people who had remote ischaemic preconditioning experienced serious complications, but these were rare. The overall certainty of the evidence was high for adverse effects, length of hospital stay and all-cause mortality, and moderate for the protection against acute kidney injury. The evidence does not confirm whether remote ischaemic preconditioning is effective in protecting the kidneys during heart or blood vessel surgery.

We searched for trials that investigated the efficacy of treatments for fatigue after stroke. We included 12 trials with 703 participants who had had a stroke at least three months earlier. Most of the trials (eight trials) investigated drug treatments, including fluoxetine, citicoline and a combination of Chinese herbs. One trial each investigated a fatigue education programme, a mindfulness-based stress reduction programme, continuous positive airway pressure for sleep apnoea (a condition where a person stops breathing for short periods during sleep) and a self management programme for recovery from chronic diseases. Only five of the trials reported how the people taking part in the trials were allocated to the different groups. This means that it is possible that the people taking part could have known whether they were in the treatment group or the control group, and this could have affected the results. Only four of the trials stated that the people assessing the outcomes were blinded to which group the participants were in. This means that there may have been bias when the outcomes were assessed. The trials were all very different in terms of the treatments they investigated, the time since the participant's stroke and the severity of the fatigue, so we could not combine many of the results. However, we found that the fatigue severity was significantly lower in the intervention groups than in the control groups for trials that did not use proper methods to prevent the people taking part knowing whether they were in the treatment or the control group and for trials that did not blind the assessors of the outcomes. We found no evidence that any of the treatments were beneficial in reducing fatigue after stroke. There was insufficient evidence on the efficacy of any intervention to treat or prevent fatigue after stroke. The trials to date have been small and very different from each other. The study designs of most of the trials were not of high quality, which makes their results less reliable. Some of the treatments described are feasible for people with stroke, but their efficacy should be investigated in trials with a more robust study design and with enough people to be able to provide reliable results.

The aim of this review was to determine which treatments are effective in reducing high potassium levels in preterm babies born before 34 weeks' gestation. Three small randomised controlled trials were found. None of these studies were of high methodological quality. Treatments included giving insulin and glucose, cation-exchange resins, albuterol inhalation and laser therapy. The combination of insulin and glucose appeared to be more effective than cation-exchange resins in lowering potassium levels. Albuterol inhalation also lowered potassium levels. More research is needed to confirm if the combination of insulin and glucose is superior to albuterol inhalation.

The authors searched the medical literature and identified 12 randomised controlled trials that met their inclusion criteria. They could divide these into three groups: those that compared IVIg with plasma exchange; those that compared IVIg with no treatment; and those that compared IVIg added to plasma exchange with plasma exchange alone. There have been no new trials since a previous Cochrane review in 2009, but there have been changes in the way the results are analysed. This review shows that in people with Guillain-Barré Syndrome who are severely affected, IVIg is as effective as plasma exchange in hastening recovery, and there is no additional benefit in combining the two treatments. In milder cases, there is not enough evidence to be sure whether IVIg is as effective as plasma exchange or not. However, it is certainly safer and more likely to be completed. Recovery with either treatment may be slower if the person has a high level of nerve damage at first. The results hold true whether the IVIg is given over one to three days or two days only. Although there were some problems with the design of most of the studies, the overall quality of the evidence was moderate to high. The results should therefore be applicable to most people with Guillain-Barré Syndrome, although more research is needed in mild cases and in people whose treatment starts more than two weeks after onset of the condition.

The evidence is current to September 2015. We found 28 randomized clinical trials with 9330 participants. Twenty-one of these trials reported on death in hospital, 19 on surgical site infections and 11 on respiratory insufficiency. The trials were conducted in various countries and included patients older than 55 years of age who were undergoing elective surgery with general anaesthesia. We found no clear evidence of benefit of a high fraction of inspired oxygen in reducing the risk of death in hospital or within 30 days of follow-up. Furthermore, we found no clear evidence that a high fraction of inspired oxygen reduced the risk of surgical site infections. However, there was no evidence of harm. The overall quality of the evidence was low. It was impossible to reliably apply the GRADE criteria to most of the outcomes. The main limitations in the quality of the evidence related to poor study design and small numbers of participants. There was no evidence of inconsistency. All trials used a routine fraction of inspired oxygen between 50% and 60%. The trials that used a high fraction of inspired oxygen had a longer mean duration of exposure to a high fraction of inspired oxygen as more of the surgery and anaesthetic time was spent at a high fraction of inspired oxygen. The use of a high fraction of inspired oxygen was not associated with length of stay, respiratory insufficiency, serious adverse events or mortality at the longest follow-up. However, the risk of mortality at the longest follow-up was uncertain by 20% (Grade D). When antibiotics were given routinely before surgery, we found that a high fraction of inspired oxygen was associated with a decreased risk of surgical site infections. This finding was consistent in trials that were adequately blinded to the outcome assessment. The quality of the evidence was very low. The findings in this review are limited by the overall low quality of the evidence. Further studies with long follow-up are needed.

We searched the literature up to 21 November 2014. We screened 25 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) for eligibility according to our criteria, which included children under 16 years of age with OME of at least three weeks' duration. We found that 23 RCTs (with a total of 3258 children) provided useful information for this review. There were several different antibiotics used as well as different times at which progress was measured. We found that children who received oral antibiotics were more likely to have complete resolution of middle ear fluid at two to three months than those given placebo or no treatment, but they were also more likely to experience diarrhoea, vomiting or skin rash. However, there was some concern about the reliability of this finding due to potential biases in how the trials were conducted. When we looked at all the available evidence together, we found that there is probably a beneficial effect of oral antibiotics for resolving OME, but that around one in five children will experience a side effect such as diarrhoea, vomiting or skin rash. The impact of antibiotics on short-term hearing is uncertain and we did not find any evidence that they prevent ventilation tube insertions or perforated eardrums. We did not look at the long-term effects of antibiotics for OME, nor did we look at their effect on speech, language and cognitive development or general quality of life. It is very important to weigh up the potential benefits of antibiotics for OME against the possibility of side effects and the development of bacterial resistance to antibiotics, especially as many children will suffer from recurrent episodes of otitis media. The overall quality of evidence for the main outcomes of this review was moderate to low.

The aim of this review was to find out how well such a diet worked in patients with diabetic nephropathy. We included 12 studies, but most were relatively old and small. There was some suggestion that a low protein diet may slow down the progression of kidney damage, but it was not clear if this was really due to the lower protein intake. It is possible that other factors, such as age, sex, duration of diabetes and treatment with other drugs, could have influenced the results. Adverse effects of a low protein diet were not adequately addressed in any of the studies.

This review is current as of April 2015. We found 19 relevant studies with 3480 participants. We classified the studies as being of either strong, moderate or limited evidence. Strong evidence means that we are very confident in the findings; moderate evidence means that we have some confidence in the findings, but there are some doubts; and limited evidence means that we have little confidence in the findings. Information from strong evidence is generalisable to the wider population; that is, it can be applied to the needs of patients who are representative of the entire population. Information from moderate and limited evidence is more applicable only to the specific circumstances described in the study. For people with mild brain injury, strong evidence suggests that provision of appropriate information and advice alone is sufficient. For people with more serious brain injuries, strong evidence indicates that they benefit from formal intervention, and limited evidence suggests that early commencement of this is beneficial. However, the balance between intensity of intervention and cost has yet to be determined. For people with more serious brain injuries who require neuropsychological rehabilitation, strong evidence suggests that this can be effectively provided in a group setting in a therapeutic environment. It is unclear which treatments work best for which patients over the longer term, and which models of care represent value for money in the context of lifelong care. Future research should aim to gather more practice-based evidence from within the context of routine clinical practice.

We searched for evidence on 20 May 2016 and identified only one study (involving 176 women) that met the inclusion criteria of the review. The included study examined the effect of rooming-in on breastfeeding outcomes. Rooming-in was defined as mothers and their infants remaining together in the same hospital room from birth until the time of discharge from hospital. Separate care was defined as the mother and infant initially spending some time apart and then gradually increasing the amount of time they spent together until discharge from hospital. The study also looked at the type of infant apparel but this is not relevant to this review. The study reported the overall median duration of any breastfeeding to be four months with no difference found between groups. The study did not report the duration of exclusive breastfeeding or the proportion of infants being exclusively breastfed at six months of age. There was no clear difference between the two groups in the proportion of infants receiving any breastfeeding at six months of age. The mean frequency of breastfeeds per day on day four postpartum for the rooming-in group was eight times per day, which was slightly higher than the separate care group who were breastfed at a fixed schedule of seven times per day. However, every infant in the separate care group was breastfed at a fixed schedule of seven times per day (with no variation), resulting in no estimable comparison. The rate of exclusive breastfeeding on day four postpartum before discharge from hospital was significantly higher in the rooming-in group (86%) compared with the separate care group (45%). None of our other pre-specified secondary outcomes were reported. Overall, we found little evidence to support or refute the practice of rooming-in versus mother-infant separation. The quality of the evidence was low.

The results of this review suggested that sanchi may be beneficial for improving neurological deficit and reducing death and dependency in the first 28 days after an acute ischaemic stroke. However, the results should be interpreted cautiously because of the small number of participants and the low methodological quality of most of the included studies. More high-quality studies are needed to confirm whether sanchi is really effective and safe for people with acute ischaemic stroke.

The authors of this review questioned whether there are benefits or drawbacks to placing dental implants either immediately, as part of the same session as tooth extraction, or after a delay of several months. Seven studies were included in the review. There is not enough evidence to be sure whether there are any advantages or disadvantages of any of these approaches. However, there is some evidence that placing an implant into a freshly made socket might lead to better looking results and that it might be possible to avoid at least one surgical procedure by doing this. On the other hand, there is some evidence that immediately placed implants are more likely to fail and to cause complications than delayed implants.

A literature search was run in January 2010 and two studies were found. One study tested the effects of clioquinol (PBT1) compared to placebo over 36 weeks in 36 participants with mild to moderate Alzheimer's disease. This study had weaknesses in its methods and the compound has been withdrawn from development. A second study tested the effects of PBT2, a related compound to clioquinol, over 12 weeks in 78 participants with mild Alzheimer's disease. No measurable benefit was seen in measures of memory or thinking but there was an improvement in some measures of mental flexibility. The study showed PBT2 to have a favourable safety profile. Further larger studies will be needed to see if it is effective.

We looked at the benefits and harms of this class of drugs in adults and children with pulmonary hypertension. We found 36 randomised controlled trials with 2999 participants who received either a phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor or a placebo. On average, trials lasted 14 weeks, with some as long as 12 months. More men than women took part, but two trials included children. Most study participants had pulmonary hypertension due to high blood pressure in the arteries of the lungs (group 1). Some studies included people with pulmonary hypertension due to other causes, including heart problems, lung diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), or blockages of blood flow from the legs to the lungs. Sildenafil, tadalafil and vardenafil improved exercise capacity and quality of life, and reduced risk of death in people with group 1 pulmonary hypertension. However, they also caused more side effects, especially headache, flushing, stomach upsets and muscle aches. It is unclear how much benefit there is for people developing pulmonary hypertension due to heart problems, and further research is recommended.
