For people with pulmonary hypertension due to lung disease, these drugs did not cause any extra harm, but did not help as much as they do for group 1 pulmonary hypertension. For people with pulmonary hypertension due to blockages to blood flow from the leg veins to the lungs, these drugs were no better than placebo. Overall, these drugs seem well tolerated, but more studies are needed to learn if they are effective for people with certain types of heart problems. Also, most of the studies only lasted a few months, so we do not know what happens when these drugs are taken for a longer period.

We searched the databases until May 2015 and found 22 studies with 2193 participants. We reran the search in April 2017 and will deal with the one study found in the update in a subsequent update of this review. Each study compared two or three different methods of injecting local anaesthetic drugs around the brachial plexus nerves. The methods were classified as single injection (one injection through the same needle), double injection (two injections through the same needle) or multiple injection (three or more injections through separate needles). We found that the quality of all the studies was reasonable. The main weaknesses were in how easy it was for the participants to know whether they had received the operation (single-blinded) and whether the decision to which treatment they received was done randomly (allocation concealment). We found that multiple injections produced better anaesthesia than both single and double injections. Double injections were better than single injections and single injections were better than multiple injections in terms of speed of performing the block. There were no clear differences between the three techniques in terms of how safe they were, or how much pain participants experienced after the operation. We concluded that people undergoing hand, wrist, forearm or elbow surgery should be offered multiple injections for their anaesthesia. This is because multiple injections provide the best chance of having effective anaesthesia.

We found three studies including 384 children with mild-moderate asthma. All studies used the same type and dose of steroid inhaler (beclomethasone 200 mcg) and all studied the effect on height over a period of six months to one year. Overall, the children who used the steroid inhalers were slightly shorter than those who used a dummy inhaler. The difference in height was about 1.5 cm (or 0.6 inches) per year. It is not clear if this would eventually catch up as the children get older. We cannot say whether other types of inhaled steroids would affect growth less than the one used in these studies. The main limitation of our review is that it only includes studies of one type of corticosteroid inhaler.

This review aimed to compare these two forms of PD in terms of mortality, infection, dropouts from PD, other complications, residual renal function and health related quality of life. Three studies which included 139 patients were identified. These studies showed no differences between the two modes of PD with respect to death, peritonitis, switch from one form of PD to a different dialysis modality, hernias, PD fluid leaks, PD catheter removal and hospital admissions. Residual renal function was similar on both modes of PD. One study found that peritonitis rates and hospitalisation were significantly less in patients on APD when expressed as episodes per patient-year. Another study found that patients on APD had significantly more time for work, family and social activities. In summary, APD has not been shown to have any significant advantage over CAPD in terms of important clinical outcomes. APD may however be considered advantageous in a select group of patients such as in the younger PD population and those in employment or education due to its psychosocial advantages.

This review presents the individual patient data of 9312 adult non-Hodgkin's lymphoma patients who participated in randomised controlled trials comparing different forms of radiotherapy, chemotherapy or both. We found that combined radiotherapy and chemotherapy is superior to radiotherapy alone in terms of survival and tumour control. Also, reduced-dose radiotherapy (50 Gy or less) after chemotherapy is inferior to standard-dose radiotherapy (at least 40 Gy). There seemed to be more side effects with chemotherapy and more side effects from radiotherapy after chemotherapy, possibly because of a greater need for further treatment. These results hold true for early stages (stages I and II) as well as for advanced stages (stages III and IV). However, it should be noted that many studies included in this review used drugs and treatment techniques which are now considered outdated. Furthermore, very few studies included in the analyses had long-term follow-up (more than 5 years). Therefore, we suggest caution when applying these results to current therapies.

This review found 26 studies with 27 treatment groups (4893 participants) evaluating the benefits and harms of opioids used for the management of cancer pain. Most of the studies had design limitations. Many participants stopped taking their assigned opioid medication because of side effects or because the medications did not reduce their pain enough. However, some people were able to take their opioids for as long as six months or more without this problem. Although many relatively mild side effects (such as constipation, drowsiness, dry mouth, and nausea) were reported, serious problems and addiction occurred infrequently. The effect of opioids on quality of life and function was uncertain.

We found one trial with a total of 212 people taking part. All the participants had a spinal cord injury and had one or more open pressure ulcers that were classified as'serious' (stage III or IV). Most of the participants were men in their 50s and 60s. The trial compared oxyandrolone with a placebo (a substance containing no active medication). The trial reported on the number of pressure ulcers that healed completely after six months of treatment and on the number of people who experienced side effects. The evidence is current to February 2018. We are uncertain whether anabolic steroids improve or reduce the complete healing of pressure ulcers as the certainty of the evidence was very low. The trial found that anabolic steroids may increase the risk of non-serious side effects and may decrease the risk of serious side effects. However, we are uncertain whether anabolic steroids increase or decrease the risk of serious side effects as the certainty of the evidence was also very low. Other outcomes that we thought were important were not reported in the included trial. The trial was funded by the National Institutes of Health, USA. There is insufficient evidence to say whether anabolic steroids help pressure ulcers heal. More research is needed.

The purpose of this review was to look at whether email is a useful way of giving people health information to promote the prevention of illness and disease, and to improve their general health and well-being. Six studies with a total of 8372 participants were included in this review. Four of the studies compared email with standard mail and two compared email with usual care. There were no differences found in the numbers of people who took up screening tests based on whether they received information by email or standard mail. There were also no differences in patient or caregiver understanding and support, or in patient or caregiver behaviour and action, when people were given information about disease prevention through email rather than standard mail. When email was compared with 'usual care', there were no differences in patient health status and well-being, understanding, support, or behaviour and action. None of the studies looked at potential harms from receiving information via email. Overall, the quality of the studies was poor and the results inconclusive. It is not possible to make any recommendations for clinical practice on the basis of the current evidence.

This review looks at the various ways of performing the Epley manoeuvre for the treatment of Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo (BPPV). The main aim is to remove the calcium particles from the semicircular canal of the inner ear which are causing the symptoms of BPPV. One way to do this is to perform the Epley manoeuvre, but there are variations of this procedure. This review compares the standard Epley manoeuvre with two other ways of doing the Epley manoeuvre; adding extra movements to the standard Epley manoeuvre ('augmented'), and adding mastoid oscillation to the standard Epley manoeuvre. The results showed that adding post-Epley postural restrictions to the Epley manoeuvre increases the chances of successful treatment. However, this was a small improvement from an already effective treatment, and does not cause any harm. We found no evidence to support the use of mastoid oscillation or additional steps in the Epley manoeuvre.

We included four studies with a total of 231 people in our review. These studies compared different types of surgery versus medical treatment in adult patients with chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps. Participants had an average age of around 40 years and just over half were women. We looked at symptom scores and quality of life scores, endoscopic scores, complications and recurrence rates. The type of medicine used as the medical treatment varied between studies as did the dose and application of the medicines. The type of surgery also varied between studies. Two of the studies were funded by pharmaceutical companies, one by a university hospital department and one by a patient organisation. Key results The studies showed no clear difference in effect between medical and surgical treatment. However, one study showed an improvement in endoscopic scores after surgery although the study had some limitations in its design. None of the studies reported on recurrence rates. Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was low or very low. There were problems with the design of some of the studies and with the way that results were reported. Also, we could not combine the results of the studies to get an overall picture of the effects of treatment because the studies used different forms of medical treatment and different measures to assess effectiveness.

The review authors searched the literature up to 29 November 2016 and identified eight randomised controlled trials (studies in which people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) involving 709 participants. Seven of the trials were conducted in lower-middle- or upper-middle-income countries, and one was conducted in a high-income country. Four of the six comparisons looked at the effect of adding zinc to staple foods compared with the same food without zinc, and four compared adding zinc to staple foods plus other nutrients with adding other nutrients to staple foods (but not zinc). The length of treatment was between one and nine months. Zinc intake from both types of comparison increased significantly after fortification. However, there was no clear indication of improvement in indicators of zinc deficiency, child growth, or school attendance. No serious adverse effects related to zinc intake were reported, and there was no suggestion of an increase in other nutrient deficiencies. We rated the overall quality of evidence for increasing zinc intake as low, meaning that we have limited confidence in the results because of study limitations and imprecision. For indicators of zinc deficiency, child growth, and other health outcomes, we could not assess the quality of evidence because there were so few studies and participants.

We found 11 randomised controlled studies comparing non-steroidal antiandrogens with castration. We found that use of non-steroidal antiandrogens did not improve overall survival, but did increase the risk of death due to cancer. Non-steroidal antiandrogens also increased the risk of clinical progression and treatment failure. In addition, more men who received non-steroidal antiandrogens stopped treatment because of side effects than those who received castration. Common side effects associated with non-steroidal antiandrogens included breast pain, enlargement of the breasts (gynaecomastia), lack of energy (asthenia), hot flashes and tiredness. The quality of evidence was rated as moderate according to the Grade system. Currently available evidence suggests that use of non-steroidal antiandrogens compared with medical or surgical castration is less effective in terms of overall survival, clinical progression, treatment failure and treatment discontinuation due to side effects. We believe that research on non-steroidal antiandrogens as single therapy is not useful for men with metastatic prostate cancer. However, further research may be helpful for men with non-metastatic prostate cancer.

The authors of this review found that screening reduces the risk of dying from breast cancer, but that the benefit has to be balanced against the adverse effects. The risk of side effects of treatment is increased among screen-detected cancers compared with women with symptomatic disease. Assuming that screening reduces breast cancer mortality by 15% and that 30% of these cases are overdiagnosed, one woman will avoid dying of breast cancer and 10 women will be treated unnecessarily for a cancer that would not have been detected if they had not been screened. More than 200 women will experience important psychological distress including anxiety and uncertainty for years because of false positive test results. This means that the number of women needed to be screened regularly for 10 years to avoid one death from breast cancer is at least 167. Assuming that the relative reduction in mortality with screening is the same for older women as for women in the trials, which is unlikely, the number of women who need to be screened regularly for 10 years to avoid one death from breast cancer at ages 65 to 74 years would be at least 250. In addition, there were no reductions in the overall risk of dying from any cause with screening at ages 40 to 74 years.

We found four randomised controlled trials (studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at the effects of priming with hCG before IVM. These included a total of 522 women. Not all of these women were randomly assigned to the trials, and so were not included in the analyses. Of those who were, 273 were women with PCOS trying to conceive, and 249 were women with unexplained infertility. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found no evidence that hCG priming had an effect on live birth or miscarriage rates. Our results suggested that hCG priming may reduce the chance of making it to the second trimester, but this was based on very low quality evidence. We did not find any evidence on adverse events (other than miscarriage) or on drug reactions. Overall, the evidence was of low quality. The main limitations were that the studies did not blind the women and staff involved in the trials to which group each person was randomised to receive, and there were not enough data to carry out all of the statistical analyses we would like to have done. We need better-quality evidence before we can be certain whether adding hCG to IVM improves outcomes for women.

The review authors searched the literature and identified 24 randomized controlled trials with a total of 15,510 participants who received either hormone replacement therapy or a placebo. However, only 10,114 participants had data that could be used in the analyses. The trials were generally of good methodological quality but were all relatively short-term (less than five years duration). This means that we cannot draw any conclusions about long-term effects of ERT or HRT use. The available studies suggest that neither ERT nor HRT improves cognitive functioning in older postmenopausal women. In fact, there is some evidence that they may have negative effects on cognition. More research is needed to understand if some women may benefit from hormone therapy, especially those who started treatment at an earlier age, had a surgical menopause and are taking combination hormone therapy.

We looked for studies that compared once-daily umeclidinium/corticosteroid inhalers with tiotropium. We included two studies with a total of 880 people with COPD. Both studies compared once-daily umeclidinium/fluticasone to tiotropium. The studies included people with a range of COPD severity. The evidence presented here is current to June 2018. We could not tell whether people who received once-daily umeclidinium/fluticasone had better outcomes than people who received tiotropium because the overall evidence was very low quality and the studies were quite small. This means that there may be considerable uncertainty about how much of a difference there really is between these two treatments. More research is needed to strengthen the evidence. It took place in hospitals and at home. People were followed up for six months. We assessed most of the outcomes that we thought were important but some were not reported. Overall, we felt that the studies were well conducted and that there was no reason to think that the people taking part knew which treatment they were getting. We also thought that the people who were taking part in the studies did not stop taking their inhalers early because of side effects. However, we did not think that the studies were large enough to show us whether one inhaler was better than the other.

This systematic review of randomised controlled trials found four studies with a total of 169 participants that met inclusion criteria. The results suggested that both drug treatment and cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT) may be useful in the treatment of BDD. However, more studies are needed before we will be able to draw any firm conclusions about the efficacy of these interventions.

This review examined whether a desensitization protocol is more effective than a rechallenge protocol for people who have had a mild or moderate reaction to cotrimoxazole in the past. The review found that based on three small trials in adults, a desensitization protocol is superior to a rechallenge protocol in terms of keeping patients on prophylaxis at six months follow up.

We found three small randomised controlled trials comparing midazolam with placebo (two studies), and with morphine (one study). Each study showed a higher level of sedation with midazolam than with placebo or morphine. However, we cannot confirm the effectiveness of midazolam in providing analgesia in preterm infants, because the scales used to measure the level of sedation have not been validated for this purpose. The studies were too small to assess any long-term complications. One study reported shorter hospital stays for infants receiving morphine compared with midazolam, and another reported an increased risk of adverse neurodevelopmental outcome with midazolam compared with morphine. Further research is needed to determine the appropriate dose and duration of midazolam for preterm and term newborns receiving intensive care.

Twelve randomised controlled trials met our inclusion criteria. We found no evidence that antibiotics shortened the duration of diarrhoea, fever or the overall duration of illness in otherwise healthy people with non-typhoidal Salmonella gastroenteritis. A slightly higher number of adverse events were noted in people who received antibiotic treatment. We have very little confidence in these findings because most of the included studies had some problems with their design, and there were not enough data to draw any conclusions about serious side effects.

The evidence is current to September 2015. We included 23 studies (4192 patients) assessing the accuracy of IL-6 for the diagnosis of sepsis in critically ill adults. All studies had problems related to study design. The percentage of patients with sepsis varied from 12% to 78% across the included studies. The main limitations of our review were the differences between the studies in terms of how many patients had sepsis, where the patients were treated, what antibiotics were given, and how the researchers defined which patients had sepsis, among other factors. These differences made it difficult to combine the results from these studies to obtain an overall measure of how well IL-6 works for identifying sepsis. We did not find any studies that assessed the impact of using IL-6 on mortality. Using an average sensitivity of 66% means that out of 1000 patients who have sepsis, 66 will be correctly identified with IL-6, but 34 will be missed and will not receive appropriate and timely antibiotic therapy. Therefore, 66% sensitivity of IL-6 does not seem acceptable for making life or death decisions. Furthermore, 74% specificity of IL-6 means that 26 out of 1000 patients without sepsis would be falsely diagnosed as having sepsis and would receive unnecessary antibiotics, which can cause serious side effects. In summary, the use of IL-6 alone is not sufficiently accurate to ensure that patients with sepsis receive appropriate and timely treatment, and therefore cannot be recommended as a standalone criterion for diagnosing sepsis.

We searched the medical literature up to May 2015 for studies comparing the use of dressings with and without wound exposure after surgery. We included 29 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) with 5718 participants. Four of the trials included people whose wounds were not covered with a dressing; the remaining 25 trials included people whose wounds were dressed with a variety of dressing types. Eleven different comparisons were included in the review. We did not find any studies that examined the effects of wound dressings on surgical site infections. We found very low quality evidence that there was no difference in surgical site infection rates between people whose wounds were covered with a dressing or left unstitched but not dressed, nor between those who had a basic wound contact dressing compared with other types of dressing. We also found very low quality evidence that there was no difference in the level of scar formation, pain, or overall satisfaction with dressings, between people who had a basic wound contact dressing compared with those who had another type of dressing. Overall, we felt that the evidence in this review was of very low certainty. This is because of concerns about the way in which the studies were conducted, and the small number of participants recruited to each study, making it difficult to detect a clear benefit or harm from any particular dressing material.

The review looked at the effects of giving women supplements, including vitamin B6, vitamin B12, folic acid, omega-3 fatty acids and selenium, to help prevent postnatal depression. Two studies were included in the review. One study found selenium had an effect on postnatal depression scores but did not reach statistical significance. The other study found that taking fish oil during pregnancy did not prevent postnatal depression. There were several limitations in both studies that could affect the reliability of the findings. There is currently no evidence to recommend any dietary supplement for the prevention of postnatal depression.

We identified 11 randomised controlled trials (RCTs): 7 RCTs (803 participants) evaluated different infusion durations of various anthracyclines, and 4 RCTs (5280 participants) evaluated different peak doses of specific anthracyclines. In this update, we could include long-term follow-up data from one trial that had previously been published. The participants in the studies with different infusion durations were mainly adults with different types of solid tumours, and those with different peak doses were also adults with different solid tumours. We found that an anthracycline infusion duration of at least six hours reduced the risk of heart failure and of cardiac function reduction. However, there were few studies in children, so we think that further studies are needed in children to determine the best dosage and duration of treatment. We did not find a clear relationship between the maximum dose of anthracyclines and the risk of heart failure. This could be due to the small number of studies, especially studies in children.

We searched for relevant studies up to 15 June 2017 and found 37 studies with 3110 people who received hypothermia as part of their treatment for TBI. Nine of these studies were new since our last review in 2009. Five studies that had been previously excluded were reassessed and included in this review. Two studies are ongoing and two studies await assessment. The people who participated in the studies had an average age ranging from 25 to 75 years and 71% were male. The severity of their brain injury was variable but was usually assessed as moderate to severe. The cooling therapies applied in the studies varied, including how long the body temperature was kept lowered and what target temperature needed to be reached. The duration of treatment was also different, from just a few hours to 12 days. We do not have complete information about any harms experienced by people in the studies. Some studies reported that people who had hypothermia were more likely to die, while others found that it improved survival. This means that we cannot tell whether hypothermia helps people survive TBI or not. Similarly, some studies found that people who had hypothermia did worse in terms of being able to care for themselves and live independently, while other studies found that they did better. Again, we cannot tell if the use of hypothermia helps people with TBI to recover more independence. Some studies found that people who had hypothermia developed pneumonia, while others did not report this problem. We could not combine the results of the studies for death, poor outcome or pneumonia because of differences between the studies. If we were to apply the best available evidence from other Cochrane reviews to a person with TBI, we would not be confident that this would result in the best outcome for that person. The evidence is current to June 2017.

The aim of this review was to determine whether family therapy is an effective treatment for depression. Three high-quality and three low-quality studies, involving 519 people with depression were identified. The studies were very different in terms of the type of intervention, participants, and outcome measures used. This made it difficult to combine the results of these studies. When we combined the positive findings from three studies, we found moderate evidence that family therapy is more effective than no treatment or a waiting list condition in reducing depressive symptoms and improving family functioning. However, for all other comparisons with other active treatments, the evidence was limited or conflicting. Therefore, at this stage, other psychological interventions for which there is already evidence, would seem to be more appropriate than family therapy for people with depression.

This review looked at the effectiveness of Duxil in improving cognition (thinking), daily functioning (activities of daily living), behaviour, quality of life and caregiver burden in people with dementia. The authors found three trials with a total of 206 patients, all suffering from vascular dementia. As the trials were so few and had relatively few participants, there is considerable uncertainty about whether Duxil is effective or not. The authors also found that the trials did not report on behaviour, quality of life or caregiver burden. Adverse effects of Duxil treatment were poorly reported. Therefore, we cannot recommend Duxil as an effective treatment for patients with dementia at present. More research is needed.

We identified seven relevant medical studies, with a total of 960 participants. The majority of participants were women, with an average age of 49 years. In all cases, the surgery was performed to remove cancer from the armpit area. Three of the studies took place in America, two in Europe and one each in Australia and Asia. All studies compared patients who had a drain inserted after surgery to those who did not have a drain inserted. The evidence obtained from this review suggests that inserting a drain following removal of lymph nodes from the armpit area reduces the risk of fluid collecting in the operated area and reduces the number of aspirations (an aspiration is where some of the excess fluid is removed by the surgeon). However, the studies also showed that patients with a drain are likely to stay longer in hospital than those without a drain. This review is based on a small number of studies and there are concerns about the quality of these studies. We recommend further good quality research is undertaken in this area.

The aim of this review was to examine the relationship between consumption of foods or supplements containing flavonoids and the risk of colorectal cancer and adenomas. Eight studies including 390,769 participants were identified. Five of the eight studies were of high methodological quality. The results of the eight studies assessing the association between flavonoids and colorectal neoplasm were conflicting. There was no evidence to suggest that flavonoids decreased the risk of colorectal neoplasm. However, there was some evidence to suggest that increased intake of certain subclasses of flavonoids, in particular flavan-3-ols, procyanidins and phytoestrogens, may reduce the risk of colorectal neoplasm. More research is needed.

The aim of this review was to evaluate the effect of extended treatment with peginterferron and ribavirin for a total of 72 weeks as compared to standard treatment for 48 weeks for patients not achieving an early viral cure. Seven randomised controlled trials were included in this review. All seven randomised controlled trials had problems with their design, which makes it less reliable to draw conclusions from their results. We could not find any data on our primary outcomes. Regarding our secondary outcomes, we found that more patients who received the extended treatment had a sustained loss of the hepatitis C virus after 72 weeks of treatment compared to those receiving the standard treatment for 48 weeks. We also found that the number of patients who relapsed was lower in the extended treatment group. However, we could not identify factors that differentiate between patients who benefit from prolonged therapy and those who do not. The amount of missing data is considerable, and the reporting on adverse events and mortality was insufficient. We conclude that more studies are needed to assess the benefits and harms of an extended treatment for patients not responding adequately to standard treatment with peginterferon and ribavirin.

We searched databases from their inception to 15 September 2014. We included two studies with 34 participants with pancreatic cancer found to have resectable disease on CT scan. None of the studies included participants with periampullary cancers. The main limitation of our review was that we only found two studies. Based on these two small studies, there is significant uncertainty in the role of EUS in people with locally advanced pancreatic cancer. We are unable to make any conclusions regarding its use in people with periampullary cancers or those with pancreatic cancer found to have resectable disease on CT scan as no studies have been done in these groups.

We found 34 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and controlled clinical trials (CCTs) on treatments for blepharitis. We were interested in how effective each treatment was in improving the symptoms of blepharitis, but we also looked for evidence that it eliminated the cause of blepharitis. We divided blepharitis into two categories: anterior blepharitis (occurring at the front of the eyelid margin) and meibomian gland dysfunction (MGD) (occurring at the back of the eyelid margin). Most of the studies (20 RCTs/CCTs, 1661 participants) focused on treatments for anterior blepharitis and 14 RCTs/CCTs (508 participants) focused on MGD. We were unable to combine the results from most of the studies because of differences in the way they were conducted. However, we found that topical antibiotics were effective in eradicating bacteria from the eyelid margin in people with anterior blepharitis. We could not draw any conclusions about the effectiveness of other treatments for blepharitis, including lid hygiene, topical steroids, and oral antibiotics. There is still no strong evidence for any of these treatments in curing chronic blepharitis. Many commercial products are available for treating blepharitis, but there is little evidence to support their use. More research is needed to evaluate the effectiveness of these products. Future trials should separate participants with staphylococcal blepharitis from those with MGD to ensure that participants in each group are as balanced as possible and to increase the chances of detecting significant differences between treatments. Trials should compare treatments to conventional lid hygiene measures, such as warm compresses and eyelid margin washing, to determine how effective they are. They should also directly compare different treatments to show which is more effective. Trial outcomes should be measured in ways that focus on the patient's perspective, such as visual function, quality of life, satisfaction with treatment, and cost. These outcomes should be measured using standardized and validated questionnaires or scales. Studies should follow participants for at least one year to assess chronic outcomes properly.

We searched for studies on 15 May 2016 and identified one study (23 participants) that met our inclusion criteria. The participants were aged between six months and 82 years old. The study did not measure most of the outcomes we thought were important. The study did not show any difference in outcome between the two treatment groups. Some adverse effects were reported, including airway swelling in one child who needed to be intubated a few hours after photodynamic therapy. We found no evidence that photodynamic therapy improves the course of this condition. More research is needed before its use can be recommended. The quality of the evidence was very low.

We included 42 studies with 4220 participants. Most studies had important methodological limitations. The majority of the studies were of poor quality in terms of how they were conducted. Our review shows that both ultrasound modalities are good for excluding an endoleak (high specificity). However, in 7 out of 10 cases, CDUS will miss a case with an endoleak that is detected by CE-CDUS. Therefore, we recommend introducing CE-CDUS as the initial imaging modality in an endoleak surveillance programme. If the CE-CDUS is negative, no further imaging would be required. If the CE-CDUS is positive, then CT scan could be used to confirm the presence and nature of the endoleak, thus avoiding unnecessary interventions.

The purpose of the pain relief options is to help relieve the pain without interfering with the woman's ability to push her baby out. Sterile water injections have been suggested as an alternative to other pain relief options. The purpose of this review is to assess the effect of injecting sterile water into the lower back (injections just above and/or below the waistline) to relieve pain during labour. The injections are given while the woman is in bed, either lying on her side or sitting up. This review includes seven randomised controlled trials with a total of 766 women who experienced low back pain in labour. Four used injections just below the skin (subcutaneous), one used injections just under the skin (intracutaneous) and one used both types. Not many women knew they were getting sterile water and neither did them or their midwives know if it was sterile water or a placebo (such as salt solution). The main outcomes were the immediate effect on pain, and whether the women needed to have an emergency caesarean section or forceps or vacuum extraction to assist with birth. The results were very limited. Most studies did not report on the main outcomes and those that did showed conflicting results. However, most studies showed a slight benefit for sterile water. The information presented is very limited and cannot be used to make recommendations for clinical practice. More research is needed to determine the effectiveness of sterile water injections for relieving pain in labour.

We identified 12 randomised controlled trials comparing glue with suture for mesh fixation in Lichtenstein repair. In terms of chronic pain, the glue group had a 37% reduction in pain scores after surgery compared with the suture group. This result holds true for lightweight meshes but not for heavyweight meshes. Recurrence rates were similar between the two groups. Fixation with glue was superior to suture regarding duration of the operation, haematoma, and recovery time to daily activities. Pooled analyses showed no significant differences between the two groups for superficial wound infection, mesh/deep infection, seroma, persisting numbness, and length of hospital stay. We could not identify any subgroups that would favour one glue over another (synthetic or biological glue). As the studies were small and short term were included in this review, we cannot draw final conclusions regarding which type of glue is superior. Overall, the quality of the evidence ranged from moderate to low.

The review found that a group of processes carried out by biomedical journal editors improves the quality of published research reports.  These processes include revising the structure of the paper, checking facts and ensuring that the reported methods are fully described.  However, very few of the studies found were of high quality and more research is needed.  The review also found that researchers frequently get references and quotations wrong when citing the work of other researchers in their own papers.

This review found 15 studies with 721 adults experiencing cancer pain related to their cancer. The studies were of mixed quality but were all considered to be adequately conducted. However, most were small (fewer than 100 participants) and four were funded by the manufacturer of codeine, making a slight possibility of bias. Codeine was better than placebo at relieving cancer pain, but caused more side effects such as constipation, nausea and vomiting. Codeine was also comparable to other painkillers tested. Codeine is often used as a first-line strong painkiller for cancer pain, usually in combination with paracetamol (which is also known as acetaminophen or N-acetyl-p-aminophenol). More research is needed to find out how well codeine works when taken for several weeks or months, and what other medicines can be taken with it to prevent side effects.

The review included 12 trials with a total of 563 people with sickle cell disease aged between six and 35 years of age. Four trials evaluated patient knowledge, 20 families evaluated caregiver knowledge, two trials evaluated the use of health care services, 10 trials evaluated the recognition of sickle cell disease signs and symptoms, and seven trials evaluated the recognition of sickle cell disease complications. Two trials evaluated coping, five trials evaluated family relations, three trials evaluated health-related quality of life and seven trials evaluated adherence to treatment (although this was not assessed in the quasi-randomized trial). The duration of the educational program ranged from one session to eight weeks. The follow-up period after the intervention ranged from the end of the intervention to 12 months later. We found that education improves knowledge about sickle cell disease in patients and caregivers. Education had a short-term beneficial effect on reducing depressive symptoms in patients. However, education did not seem to reduce the use of healthcare services, nor did it improve coping, family relations or health-related quality of life. These results should be interpreted with caution because of the small number of studies and participants and because some of the studies were of poor methodological quality.

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing brivaracetam given together with other antiepileptic medicines with a placebo (a pretend treatment) given together with other antiepileptic medicines, or with no active treatment. The evidence is current to May 2015. We found six studies with a total of 2411 participants whose data could be used in the analysis. All of the people in the trials had epilepsy that was difficult to treat with medicines. Five of the studies included people with epilepsy where the electrical burst that causes their seizures starts in a specific part of the brain, and one study included people with epilepsy where the electrical burst affects the whole brain. More people who took brivaracetam had their seizures reduced by half or more, and more became seizure-free, than those who took placebo. However, more people who took brivaracetam stopped taking it because of side effects than those who took placebo. Further research is needed to establish whether brivaracetam works well in long-term treatment, and to assess how well it works in other types of epilepsy. Our results are relevant to adults with drug-resistant focal epilepsy, but we cannot draw conclusions about the effects of brivaracetam in other age groups. The quality of evidence was moderate for reduction in seizure frequency and risk of being seizure-free, but low for treatment withdrawal and having one or more adverse events.

We found 38 studies, mostly from high-income countries. Many studies explored parents' perceptions of vaccine communication. Some focused on the measles, mumps, rubella (MMR) vaccine. Overall, parents wanted more information than they were getting. This led to worry and regret about vaccination decisions among some parents. Parents wanted information about both the benefits and harms of vaccination, presented clearly and simply, and information tailored to their situation. They wanted information to be available at a variety of locations, including outside health services, and in good time before each vaccination appointment. Parents saw health workers as an important source of information and had specific expectations of their interactions with them. Poor communication and negative relationships with health workers sometimes impacted on vaccination decisions. Parents generally found it difficult to know which information source to trust and challenging to find information they felt was unbiased and balanced. The amount of information parents wanted and the sources they felt could be trusted appeared to be linked to acceptance of vaccination, with parents who were more hesitant wanting more information. Most of the trials assessed in this review attempted to improve at least one or two key aspects of communication, including providing information prior to the vaccination appointment and tailoring information to parents' needs. No trials appeared to address negative media stories or parental perceptions of health worker motives.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) where people were allocated at random to receive one of these treatments. Ten RCTs met our inclusion criteria, with a total of 599 participants. We included two trials with adolescents, and the remaining eight trials included only adults. The psychological therapies tested were: cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT); cognitive analytic therapy; focal psychodynamic therapy; Specialist Supportive Clinical Management (SSCM); and treatment as usual (TAU). TAU was delivered by a variety of professionals, including psychologists, psychiatrists and general practitioners. All studies compared individual psychological therapies with either TAU or a different type of psychological therapy. Study quality was generally poor. The main problems were small study sizes and a lack of blinding (assessors who are not aware of which treatment the participants have received). Recovery, measured by achieving a good or intermediate level of functioning on the Morgan and Russell Scale, was better with focal psychodynamic therapy than TAU, but this result is uncertain. Body mass index (BMI) was similar across all treatment groups. There was little difference between individual psychological therapies and TAU in terms of completion rates. Specialist Supportive Clinical Management and Optimised TAU were comparable to CBT in terms of BMI. None of the studies reported any harm resulting from taking part in the trials. Overall, we do not find evidence that any specific psychological therapy is more effective than another in treating anorexia nervosa in adults and older adolescents. More research is needed.

The search for studies was done on 23 October 2014. We found three randomized controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) that met our inclusion criteria. One study was from the USA and consisted of two parts: the first part randomized 151 adults to receive either silicone oil or sulfur hexafluoride gas tamponades; and the second part randomized 271 adults to receive either silicone oil or perfluropropane gas tamponades. The second study was a multi-center international trial and randomized 94 participants to receive heavy silicone oil or standard silicone oil. These three studies, which included a total of 516 participants, found that outcomes after surgery with perfluoro-propane gas or silicone oil appeared comparable for a broad variety of cases, as well as heavy silicone oil compared with standard silicone oil. There were no significant differences between these agents in the proportion of participants achieving at least 20/200 visual acuity or macular attachment at a minimum of one year. Sulfur hexafluoride gas was reported to be associated with significantly worse anatomic and visual outcomes than was silicone oil at one year but not at two years. The first two studies were funded by the National Eye Institute, and the third study was funded by the German Research Foundation. Individual subanalyses for recalcitrant retinal detachment, open globe trauma, and open globe postendophthalmitis did not show any significant differences in outcomes between perfluoropropane gas and silicone oil. Heavy silicone oil, which is not available for routine clinical use in the USA, has not demonstrated evidence of superiority over standard silicone oil.

We searched for evidence on 10 May 2017. We identified five randomised controlled studies involving 1819 women. All studies included women diagnosed with pre-eclampsia, and four studies also included women with hypertension. The studies were similar in design, but differed in how far they had gone through the planning and organisation of the trial before it was stopped. Two studies had finished recruitment and started analysing their data when the trial was stopped, which could affect how reliable their results are. The other three studies had not yet recruited enough women to start analysing their data. The evidence from the included studies suggests that, for women who are carrying babies at risk of being born too soon, planned early delivery is safer for the mother than waiting for labour to start spontaneously. However, we do not have much evidence on how this affects the baby's health, and more research is needed. We also need more studies that look at the different types of high blood pressure disorder during pregnancy and the best time for early delivery for these conditions. The level of evidence was assessed as high for the combined measure of maternal death and severe illness (known as 'composite maternal morbidity'), and as moderate for caesarean section and the number of days the mother and baby spend in hospital after giving birth. The evidence for the combined measure of infant death and severe illness (known as 'composite infant morbidity') was assessed as low, because the studies only included a small number of babies and used different ways of collecting data.

We found six randomised controlled studies, including 142 participants, comparing prophylaxis with on-demand therapy. Two of these studies compared three-times-a-week prophylaxis to on-demand treatment and showed a clear benefit of prophylaxis in reducing bleeding events, especially joint bleeds. However, we could not find evidence from randomised controlled trials to confirm the observational evidence that prophylaxis is also effective in adults. Moreover, non-significant increases in both inhibitor and infectious complications were observed in patients on prophylaxis, which occurred more often when using long-term venous access.

This is an update of a previous review published in 2000. We found 13 eligible studies of which 10 had usable data. The small number of patients and small size of the studies meant that there was considerable uncertainty about whether any of the treatments were better than others. There was some evidence from three studies that less intensive treatment produced similar results to more intensive treatment with less toxicity.

The authors searched the literature and identified two randomised controlled trials with a total of 447 RhD negative women who required anti-D immunoglobulin to prevent RHD. Both studies were undertaken in Australia. They compared giving anti-D by intramuscular injection with giving anti-D intravenously. All women received a 1500 IU (300 microgram) dose of anti-D during week 28 of pregnancy. We assessed both studies as being at high risk of bias. Due to the very low numbers of women in each study, it is possible that any observed difference in the level of anti-D in the blood after each treatment may have occurred by chance. Neither study reported whether any of the women developed antibodies to the RhD antigen following birth or a blood transfusion. It appears that anti-D given by intramuscular injection and anti-D given intravenously are equally effective. The choice of which anti-D is given will depend on the anti-D preparation available, the dose required, and patient preference.

This review summarises and discusses the benefits and harms of antiplatelet agents in patients with diabetes mellitus who have had a heart attack or who have at least one risk factor for heart disease. Twenty-one thousand three hundred and seventy nine patients with diabetes were included in eight studies which compared an antiplatelet agent, either ticlopidine or clopidogrel, with placebo or another antiplatelet drug. Most studies included patients with type 2 diabetes. The length of follow up was between one and two years. We could not identify any clear benefits or harms due to the use of these drugs but there was some suggestion that they may reduce the risk of stroke. There were no data on foot problems, eye problems or kidney impairment which may be caused by diabetes and no information about cost. More studies are needed with separate analyses for patients with diabetes.

We included 10 trials with a total of 191 participants from eight countries. The length of treatment ranged from a single session to three months. Six studies compared non-invasive ventilation with another way of clearing mucus and three studies compared non-invasive ventilation with the provision of oxygen. The majority of the studies were short term. We found that non-invasive ventilation may help to clear mucus from the lungs more easily than other methods, and people with cystic fibrosis may prefer it. However, we did not find any evidence that it helps to clear more mucus than other methods. Non-invasive ventilation may help improve lung function more than oxygen. This has to be weighed against any risks of long-term use of this treatment. As the trials were short and with small numbers of participants, we cannot draw many conclusions about the long-term effects of this treatment. More research is needed. We recommend that future studies are larger, longer and measure the outcomes that are important to patients.

We looked for studies where people with severe COPD were treated with NIPPV at night for at least three months. We included seven studies on 245 people in this updated review. This review showed no evidence of benefit of NIPPV on exercise capacity, gas exchange, lung function, respiratory muscle strength or sleep efficiency in people with COPD. There was also no evidence of a significant improvement in quality of life. The studies were too small to determine whether or not there was any difference in mortality. More research is needed to determine if NIPPV is beneficial in selected groups of people with COPD.

We searched for evidence on 14 May 2015 and identified four trials involving a total of 1190 women. We found that inducing labour early to avoid a baby being large did not clearly change the risk of complications during childbirth. However, fewer babies had broken bones with induced labour, and shoulders getting stuck during birth were less likely to occur. One trial found more severe perineal tears in women who had an induction, but this could have been chance. The average birthweight was slightly lower in the induction group. The quality of the evidence was reasonable for most outcomes, although it was low for outcomes that involved assessing whether a baby was harmed during birth, because few babies were reported to have been harmed. If the concern about a baby's size is thought to be important enough to justify induction then the benefits of induction need to be balanced against possible disadvantages, including the possibility of having a caesarean birth, which was not clearly different with or without induction in this review. In the unlikely event of a baby suffering a brachial plexus injury, parents would need to weigh up the pain and disability this would cause their child against the risk of this happening with induction of labour. They would also know that many women are induced for suspect macrosomia and that they themselves may have been worrying unnecessarily.

Fifty-six randomised clinical trials provided usable data on mortality. The trials included 95,286 participants living in North America, Europe, Asia, Australia and New Zealand. The age of the participants ranged from 18 to 107 years. On average, two-thirds of the participants were women. Half of the trials included healthy participants, and the other half included participants with chronic diseases such as cardiovascular or renal disease. Vitamin D3 statistically significantly decreased mortality, whereas vitamin D2, alfacalcidol and calcitriol did not. Furthermore, vitamin D3 combined with calcium increased the risk of nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), and alfacalcidol and calcitriol increased the risk of hypercalcaemia (elevated blood calcium levels). It is possible that vitamin D3 may have beneficial effects on mortality in elderly people living independently or in institutional care. However, because of risks of systematic errors (bias) and random errors (play of chance), further randomised trials are needed. We considered 24 of the 56 included trials to be at high risk of bias. Nine of the 24 trials were funded by the vitamin D industry. Two of the 24 trials received the vitamin D industry's funding support. One of the 24 trials was funded by a health research institution but stated that the sponsor had no role in the conduct of the trial. The remaining 13 trials did not mention who funded them. Seven of the 24 trials with high risk of bias had serious risk of bias in the design, and 17 had serious risk of bias in the implementation of the trial. Three of the 24 trials were terminated before they reached the planned number of participants because of slow recruitment. Four of the 24 trials were funded by the vitamin D industry, and two of the 24 trials received the vitamin D industry's funding support. One of the 24 trials was funded by a health research institution but stated that the sponsor had no role in the conduct of the trial. The remaining 13 trials did not mention who funded them. Seven of the 24 trials with high risk of bias had serious risk of bias in the design, and 17 had serious risk of bias in the implementation of the trial. Three of the 24 trials were terminated before they reached the planned number of participants because of slow recruitment. We considered 32 of the 56 included trials to be at low risk of bias. None of the 32 trials were funded by the vitamin D industry, and none of them mentioned receiving vitamin D industry funding. All 32 trials described (a priori) how the randomisation list was compiled and how blinding of participants and personnel would be ensured. All 32 trials reported on the methods used to conceal allocation. All 32 trials reported on the methods used to mask the treatment allocation. All 32 trials reported on the methods used to randomly allocate participants to groups. Twenty-nine of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to record source data. Twenty-eight of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to identify missing data. Twenty-seven of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to confirm the accuracy of the reported data. Twenty-six of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to randomly allocate participants to groups. Twenty-five of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to mask the treatment allocation. Twenty-four of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to record source data. Twenty-three of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to identify missing data. Twenty-two of the 32 trials at low risk of bias reported on the methods used to confirm the accuracy of the reported data. We considered the overall quality of the evidence to be moderate.

We found twenty-two studies which met our criteria for randomised controlled trial studies. These included 296 participants taking fluoxetine, 2036 taking orlistat, and 1047 taking sibutramine. Overall, the studies show that fluoxetine, orlistat, and sibutramine result in small weight loss over a period of one to two years. The health consequences of this small amount of weight loss are not clear. No adverse events were reported in the fluoxetine studies, but gastrointestinal side effects were common with orlistat, tremor, sleepiness and sweating with fluoxetine, and palpitations with sibutramine. We did not find any studies on other medicines suggested by the expert panel.

The last search for studies was carried out in May 2014. We found five studies with a total of 734 participants who received either vigabatrin or carbamazepine. The evidence is current to May 2014. Due to the small number of studies, which did not report the same outcomes and had different study designs, we could not analyse the data together to obtain a single result. However, we were able to compare some of the results from individual studies and draw our own conclusions. One study was rated as good quality and the other four as poor quality. The main findings of our review were that there was no difference between vigabatrin and carbamazepine on the time to withdraw from treatment and the time to achieve six-month remission; however, a disadvantage for vigabatrin was identified in the time to first seizure after starting treatment. Additionally, more cases of weight gain, skin rash and drowsiness were observed with vigabatrin than with carbamazepine, whereas no differences were seen in visual field defects and visual disturbances. Overall, the evidence is insufficient to assess the risk-benefit balance of vigabatrin compared with carbamazepine monotherapy for epilepsy. As vigabatrin can cause visual field defects, as demonstrated in previous studies, its use in people with epilepsy should be cautious and followed up regularly.

The evidence is current to August 2016. We found two randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which were sufficiently similar to include in our analysis. Both RCTs compared plerixafor plus G-CSF versus G-CSF plus placebo in people with multiple myeloma or non-Hodgkin lymphoma who were unable to mobilise their own stem cells with G-CSF alone. We found no evidence that the addition of plerixafor to G-CSF changed overall survival or the rate of serious side effects. The combined results showed clearly that the use of plerixafor led to more stem cells being collected within a shorter time frame than with G-CSF alone. However, we did not find enough evidence to be sure if the addition of plerixafor had an effect on the time taken for the new immune system to be established after transplantation, nor on the overall quality of life. We considered the quality of the evidence to be high for the outcomes stem cell collection and rates of adverse events. We judged the quality of evidence to be moderate for overall mortality at 12 months because of concerns about imprecision in the estimate.

Twenty three trials, with a total of 1806 women, were included in this review. All of the trials were small, and in many the quality was hard to judge. The trials used different outcome measures, which made the results difficult to combine. However, there was some evidence that cones were better than no active treatment, and they might be as good as pelvic floor muscle training and electrostimulation. More research is needed, but for now, cones could be one of many possible treatments for these women, once other more invasive options have been considered.

We searched scientific databases for randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) looking at the effects of education, decision aids, and self-monitoring plus education in patients with atrial fibrillation who were on oral anticoagulant therapy. We did not include studies where participants had other heart problems. The evidence is current to February 2017. We found 11 studies with a total of 2246 participants. We found that self-monitoring combined with education may have a small effect on time in range, but this is uncertain. Education had a small effect on anxiety and depression, which improved slightly when compared with usual care. Decision aids did not help with decision making. There is insufficient evidence to draw definitive conclusions about the impact of educational or behavioural interventions on time in therapeutic range in atrial fibrillation patients receiving oral anticoagulants. More trials are needed to examine the impact of interventions on anticoagulation control in AF patients and the mechanisms by which they are successful. It is also important to explore the psychological implications for patients suffering from this long-term chronic condition. The overall quality of evidence ranged from very low to low.

This review of 70 randomised controlled trials (11,487 women) found that prostaglandin E2 probably increases the chances of vaginal birth within 24 hours when administered before the first stage of labour. It also increases hyperstimulation of the uterus which can lead to an abnormal heart rhythm for the baby but does not affect the number of operative deliveries. Side effects include vomiting, shivering, fever and headache but these are relatively rare. The effectiveness of PGE2 tablets, gels and pessaries (including sustained release preparations) are similar, any differences between them are marginal but may be important.

The review authors searched the medical literature and identified five studies that compared cots warmed with electric heaters to incubators and one study that compared cots warmed with hot water bottles to incubators. The studies were conducted in the USA, Canada, Germany and Saudi Arabia. The heated cots were fitted with thermometers and humidifiers. The incubators had built-in oxygen supplies and monitoring devices. The five studies that compared cots warmed with electric heaters to incubators showed that there was no difference between the two groups with respect to mean body temperature, episodes of low body temperature or any other measured parameter. The one study that compared cots warmed with hot water bottles to incubators showed increased episodes of high body temperature in the cot nursed infants. There were no significant differences in weight gain, length of hospital stay, death before discharge from hospital, or any other measured parameter. More studies are needed, especially in developing countries, where differences in outcomes may be encountered.

Authors from Cochrane Oral Health carried out this review of existing studies and the evidence is current up to 7 May 2018. It includes three studies published from 2002 to 2016 in which a total of 146 teenagers and adults had their permanent teeth evaluated after they had undergone either an open or closed surgical procedure to expose PDLs that prevented their upper canine teeth from erupting. The main results of the review were based on whether or not the surgery was successful in exposing the tooth. There were no significant differences found between the two techniques for any of the outcomes examined in the review, including exposure rate of the tooth (low-quality evidence), complications (low-quality evidence), aesthetic (the appearance of the teeth in relation to other teeth) (very low-quality evidence), pain and discomfort (very low-quality evidence), periodontal health (the gums around the teeth) (very low-quality evidence) and treatment time (very low-quality evidence). We judged the quality of the evidence to be low or very low for all outcomes. There are currently three ongoing studies that are expected to provide further reliable information to strengthen our findings.

This review of three studies, involving 244 women, found that giving prostaglandins was not better than placebo in reducing the rate of manual removal of placenta, severe postpartum haemorrhage, need for blood transfusion, mean blood loss or the mean time from injection to placenta removal. Side-effects were similar apart from shivering, which was more common with prostaglandins. Quality of the evidence Overall, the included studies were of poor quality and there is little confidence in the effect estimates; the true effect is likely to be substantially different. There is no evidence from randomised controlled trials regarding the effect of prostaglandins on maternal mortality. Prostaglandins are known to cause uterine contractions and can help bring on labour. It has been suggested that giving them after delivery to women with a retained placenta might help the womb contract and expel the placenta. This review of three studies, involving 244 women, found that giving prostaglandins was not better than placebo in reducing the rate of manual removal of placenta, severe postpartum haemorrhage, need for blood transfusion, mean blood loss or the mean time from injection to placenta removal. Side-effects were similar apart from shivering, which was more common with prostaglandins. The included studies were all undertaken over 20 years ago and are very small. They were also poorly conducted and reported, with a high risk of bias. Larger, better studies are needed to confirm whether this treatment works and is safe.

This review includes six small trials with 355 infants. The trials were conducted between 1969 and 1996. They were all performed in newborn units and used similar criteria for selection of infants receiving CPAP. Two studies used CPAP delivered through a face mask, two studies used CNP, one study used CPAP via a nasal prong and one study used both CNP and CPAP. There were no differences in breathing patterns, incidence of bronchopulmonary dysplasia, length of ventilator dependence and outcome at nine to 14 years. Infants receiving CDP had a lower risk of treatment failure and death. However, they also had a higher risk of air leaking into the chest cavity causing lung collapse (pneumothorax). This means that more infants on CDP need to be treated for pneumothorax than those not on CDP. The relatively high rate of pneumothorax is a concern. Future studies need to address which method of CDP delivery is better and safer.

Foot ulcers are a common complication of diabetes and can be difficult to treat. They can lead to infection, disability and even death. A number of different types of dressings are available to manage foot ulcers but there is little research on which is the most effective. The aim of this review was to compare the clinical effectiveness of foam dressings against other types of dressings for treating diabetic foot ulcers. Six randomised controlled trials met the inclusion criteria for the review (157 participants). These were all small studies with short follow up periods. The review concluded that there was no research evidence to suggest that foam wound dressings heal diabetic foot ulcers any faster than other types of dressings.

This review summarises the evidence from randomised trials on the subject. We searched for trials comparing endoscopic, surgical, or conservative treatment with each other. Trials comparing different types of surgery or different techniques of endoscopy were not included. We found three eligible trial reporting on 166 participants in total: 83 had surgery and 83 had either endoscopy or conservative treatment. Key results Endoscopic treatment was compared with surgical treatment in two trials, which included 111 participants (55 had endoscopy and 56 had surgery). The main result was pain relief, which was better with surgery at both middle-long term (two to five years) and long-term follow-up (five years or more). Quality of life and preservation of pancreatic function were also better with surgery at middle-long term, but not at long-term follow-up. There was no difference in the overall complication rate after treatment, but this could not be reliably evaluated due to the small numbers of participants. One trial, with 32 participants (17 had surgery and 15 had conservative treatment), found that surgery relieved pain better than conservative treatment and improved pancreatic function. Quality of life was similar. The trial had several limitations. The number of participants was small and there were concerns about how the people were allocated to the groups. Further trials are needed to confirm the findings.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2012 and found only one randomised controlled trial that explored this question. The trial included 857 patients who had undergone minor skin excision surgery in the primary care setting. The trial compared early post-operative bathing (dressings to be removed after 12 hours with normal bathing resumed) with delayed post-operative bathing (dressings to be retained for at least 48 hours before removal and resumption of normal bathing). The trial found no clear difference in the rate of surgical site infections between the two groups. The results of this review do not provide any conclusive evidence on the effects of early or delayed bathing on wound healing, as there were relatively few participants in the study and because there may have been flaws in the way it was conducted.

We found two relevant studies. One compared prednisone with no treatment, and the other compared prednisolone with high-dose monthly dexamethasone (a drug similar to prednisolone). The first study (36 participants) did not measure our main outcome, which was improvement in arm pain and function. However, after three months, 12 of 19 participants given prednisone and five of 16 given no treatment had improved according to another measurement. The study did not report side effects in detail, but one person given prednisone died. The second study (40 participants) compared the two forms of prednisolone and did not show that either was better in terms of achieving remission (a period of no symptoms), change in disability or muscle strength. Some participants given prednisolone and others given dexamethasone deteriorated. Other side effects were similar, except that sleeplessness was less common and moon facies (moon-shaped appearance of the face) was much less common with high-dose monthly oral dexamethasone than with oral prednisolone. We rated the quality of the evidence using four levels: very low, low, moderate or high. Very low-quality evidence means that we are very uncertain about the results. High-quality evidence means that we are very confident in the results. For oral prednisone compared with no treatment, the evidence was very low quality, so we can say little about whether it helps people with leprosy and its complications. However, experience from large non-randomised studies suggests that corticosteroids are beneficial, but cause serious side effects when used for a long time. The evidence from the single study comparing oral prednisolone with oral dexamethasone was of moderate to low quality, meaning that we are moderately or highly confident in the findings. There was little or no difference between the two drugs in terms of number of people who achieved remission, change in disability or muscle strength. Side effects were similar, except for sleeplessness and moon facies, which were less common with oral dexamethasone.

We found six studies including 2100 adults with asthma. Four of these studies (792 people) compared a remote check-up with face-to-face check-up and we included them in our main analysis. Two studies were special cases: one compared two different types of follow-up appointment for people with difficult asthma, and we present this result separately; the other was a large study that looked at how many people with asthma were offered a review by their general practitioner (GP), and we present this as a separate answer. Most people in the studies took regular inhalers and had mild to moderate asthma, although one study included people with severe asthma. We could not make anyone anonymous in the studies so they knew whether they had a face-to-face or a remote check-up. This might affect the results as people might behave differently if they know what type of check-up they are having. Many people dropped out of the studies, especially the smaller ones, which might also affect the results. We could not tell whether people had more asthma attacks with remote check-ups than face-to-face check-ups. We did not find any clear evidence that people's asthma was worse, or better, with remote monitoring. Because the studies were small, there were too few reports of serious harms or admissions to hospital to know whether remote check-ups are as safe as face-to-face check-ups. Our findings apply mainly to upper-middle income countries with relatively well-resourced primary care systems. Future studies should compare remote check-ups with face-to-face check-ups in lower-income countries and should measure whether people's perceptions of the experience differ between groups.

This review included three studies with a total of 212 children. All three studies were well conducted and at a low risk of bias. There was no evidence that exercise therapy improved function, aerobic capacity or quality of life. However, these results should be interpreted with caution due to the small number of studies and the different types of exercises and duration of treatment used. No short-term detrimental effects of exercise therapy were found. Future trials should use a standardised set of outcome measures and report on adverse events.

This review includes 19 studies involving 2663 people with COPD. We classified 11 studies as outpatients (mild to moderate exacerbations) and seven as inpatients (severe exacerbations); one study had intensive care unit (ICU) patients. We found that antibiotics were better than placebo at reducing the chance of treatment failure, but the effect was small. There was no clear evidence that antibiotics reduced the chance of dying during an exacerbation, or the time spent in hospital. In contrast, we found a large effect of antibiotics on the chance of treatment failure among people admitted to the ICU. Again, there was no clear evidence that antibiotics improved other aspects of health, such as quality of life, but there was a suggestion that they might make little difference to the risk of side effects. Overall, the quality of evidence was low to moderate. This means that there is uncertainty about some of the findings.

We searched for evidence from trials in September 2015 testing the effects of whole grain consumption in healthy adults and those at increased risk of heart and circulatory diseases. We found nine trials with a total of 1414 participants who received either whole grain or refined grain foods. The age range of the participants was 24 to 70 years old with an average age of 45 to 59 years. The length of the trials was between four and 16 weeks. Most of the studies were funded by organisations with possible financial interests in the cereal industry. There were no clear benefits of whole grain intake for any cardiovascular disease outcome. The overall quality of the evidence was low, with all trials having some level of concern in terms of design or implementation. There is a need for better-quality evidence from trials assessing the long-term effects of whole grain intake.

This review includes 38 studies with a total of 1896 participants. Most of the studies investigated people who had broken their ankle bone that had been fixed surgically. We could not tell whether the same conclusions would apply to people whose broken ankles were managed conservatively (i.e. without surgery). The majority of the studies were conducted in high-income countries. The evidence is current to May 2014. There was some evidence from four studies that people who wore a removable splint that allowed them to exercise their ankle had better range of motion and function after 12 weeks. However, about 50% of these studies did not report on who received which treatment, so we cannot be sure of this finding. There was also some evidence from three studies that people who started to put weight on their broken ankle, even if only for a few steps at first, recovered more quickly than those who did not put any weight on it. One of these studies used hypnosis, which had no effect. There was little evidence on what type of cast or brace was better. One study found that people who wore a backslab (a rigid plastic plate) had better range of motion in their ankle than those who wore a bandage. There was no evidence on the effects of exercises, such as stretching or manipulation, or on the benefits of electrotherapy. For the future development of ankle braces and casts, there need to be more studies that are designed to minimise any possible biases that might affect the reliability of the results.

This review found four studies that evaluated interventions to improve adherence among children and adolescents on HAART. No single intervention was evaluated by more than one study. Two studies were from low-income countries.
