One study showed that a home-based nursing intervention improved knowledge and may have improved medication refills, but had no effect on CD4 count or viral load. A second study showed that caregivers who kept medication diaries reported fewer missed doses for children in the diary group, although this difference was not statistically significant. Yet, there was no improvement in CD4 percentage or viral load. A non-randomised study of a peer support therapy group for adolescents demonstrated no change in self-reported adherence, yet the proportion of adolescents with a suppressed viral load increased from 30% to 80%. A second non-randomised study found that children on a protease inhibitor-based treatment were more likely to achieve virologic suppression than those on a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor-based treatment, regardless of their level of adherence. These two studies suggest that some interventions may improve health outcomes even if they do not directly improve adherence. In general, there were problems with the way that all four studies were designed. Therefore, we cannot draw many conclusions about which interventions are most effective in improving adherence to HAART for children and adolescents.

This review assessed how well fenoprofen worked at relieving pain after an operation. It compared fenoprofen 200 mg with placebo (a dummy treatment), other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), and other different dosages of fenoprofen. Only a single study included other NSAIDs and other dosages of fenoprofen. This review found that a single dose of 200 mg of fenoprofen relieved moderate to severe pain as effectively as placebo did, but better than no treatment. 200 mg of fenoprofen relieved pain for 4 to 6 hours. Adverse events were rare and were about the same with fenoprofen and placebo. There were no serious adverse events or withdrawals due to adverse events. More research is needed on other doses of fenoprofen, other comparisons and other outcomes.

This review includes six trials with a total of 975 women, with four trials comparing a pelvic floor muscle training program with a control and two trials comparing a pelvic floor muscle training program as an adjunct to surgery. There were some common themes in the results of the individual trials. All trials reported greater improvement in prolapse symptoms in the pelvic floor muscle training group compared to the control group. This was also true for the two trials that measured the severity of prolapse, with a 17% improvement in prolapse stage in the PFMT group. However, there were some inconsistencies in the measurement of pelvic floor muscle function and urinary and bowel symptoms between trials. In general, the trials were not of very high quality and many did not report how women were allocated to each group. In addition, it is possible that knowing whether they were in the intervention group may have affected how women reported their symptoms. Most of the trials were very short, only lasting a few weeks, so we need more trials lasting longer to show us whether the benefits of pelvic floor muscle training are sustained over time.

This review focused on the effect of topical antibiotics on chronic suppurative middle ear infection in adults. We found 14 relevant studies, which included a total of 1724 ears. The studies were often difficult to carry out properly, and all had some problems with their design. The main problems we found were: some studies did not clearly define chronic suppurative otitis media, some studies used different ways of assessing how well the treatment worked, and some studies did not report on side effects. We found that a quinolone antibiotic was better than no medication in clearing discharge from the ear, and better than an antiseptic solution. There was some evidence that a non-quinolone antibiotic (without steroids) was also better than no medication or an antiseptic solution, but these results were less clear. There was not enough information to decide whether a quinolone antibiotic was better than a non-quinolone antibiotic. Generally, the studies did not look at long enough to assess what happens when the discharge stops - people may still have problems with their hearing, or the infection could come back. Therefore, further research is needed to answer some of the questions raised by this review.

The main aim of this review was to assess how well FRS identify people with schizophrenia. Twenty studies including 6253 people were included in the review. The studies were carried out between 1974 and 2011 but 80% had been conducted earlier than 2000. The quality of these studies was generally poor. When compared to a group of people who did not have schizophrenia, FRS were found to accurately identify schizophrenia in 75% to 95% of cases. This means that 5% to 25% of people with FRS would not have schizophrenia. Although some of these people may not need further help, others may miss out on appropriate treatment. Similarly, 40% of people without FRS may be referred for further testing or assessment, some of whom may not actually need it. It is important to remember that FRS are just one of several ways of diagnosing schizophrenia and should be used in combination with other information, such as a detailed history and physical examination, to get a more accurate picture. Newer methods of diagnosing schizophrenia have not yet been developed, so FRS are likely to remain an important method for identifying people with schizophrenia.

We found eight studies from the previous version of this review that remained suitable to include in this update. We found two additional new studies that were published before 30 September 2015. One of these was an educational study and one was a psychological study. We included 10 randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which involved 2047 participants in total. All were conducted in hospital and community settings. The studies were quite small, with a maximum of 992 participants. Four of the studies had flaws in their design, which could affect our confidence in their results. The studies used a variety of methods to evaluate whether the educational programmes were helpful. Only one study looked at psychological treatments. The largest study looked at a six-session education programme for children aged 3 months to 18 years. The children received either the educational programme plus standard care or just standard care. The study showed that the education programme improved the appearance of the eczema and quality of life for both doctors' and nurses' assessments. Another study showed that an information leaflet and video improved knowledge but not the appearance of the eczema. Three studies showed that the eczema improved more quickly when children attended a clinic run by a specialist nurse. In three studies, children who attended a clinic ran by a doctor-scientist showed a similar improvement in eczema to those who did not attend the clinic. We could not combine the results of these three studies. While some studies showed positive results, others did not. More research is needed to work out which interventions are most effective. Future studies should pay more attention to describing the details of the interventions used and should report on how the interventions were delivered. Future studies should also look at the long-term effects of the interventions and compare different types of interventions against each other.

This review found 14 small trials which suggested that hyperbaric oxygen is helpful in the management of late radiation toxicity. There was some evidence from three trials that it improved the chances of successful treatment of osteoradionecrosis (a complication of radiotherapy) with surgery, and further evidence from single trials that it helped to improve the chances of healing after radiotherapy induced ulcers of the anus and rectum, surgical flaps in irradiated tissue, and dental extractions in irradiated bone. There was no evidence of benefit for complications of radiotherapy to nerves. No side effects were reported in the trials. We concluded that hyperbaric oxygen therapy may help in some patients who have late radiation toxicity but more research is needed to find the best people to treat with it and when.

This review looked at whether using blunt needles reduce the risk of percutaneous injury during surgery. We included ten randomised controlled trials with a total of 2961 surgeons who performed an operation with either sharp or blunt needles. The operations were abdominal closure, caesarean sections, vaginal repair and hip replacement. Sharp needles were associated with a higher rate of glove perforations. For every six operations performed with sharp needles, one operation with a blunt needle will avoid a glove perforation. Also, for every six operations performed with sharp needles, one operation with a blunt needle will avoid a needle stick injury. The force required to pierce the tissue with blunt needles was higher than with sharp needles, however this was not found to be unacceptable in five out of six studies. We conclude that surgeons should consider using blunt needles as an alternative to sharp needles where feasible.

We ran electronic searches up to January 2015 for trials that randomised people with schizophrenia or schizophrenia-like illnesses to receive either trifluoperazine or low-potency antipsychotic medication. We found seven studies with 422 participants. These studies were conducted between 1969 and 1998 and so cover a range of clinical settings and available treatments. Not many details were reported about how the studies were organised and how the people taking part were allocated to each treatment, so we cannot assess whether this would affect our results. The studies all used standard methods for reporting how well the treatment worked and if there were any side effects. Not all relevant information was reported in the studies. All of the studies looked only at men. The average age of participants in most studies was over 40 years old and most had had symptoms of illness for more than 10 years. The doses of trifluoperazine used in the studies varied, but were generally similar to those used today. The doses of low-potency antipsychotics also varied, but were generally lower than those used today. The results of the analysis showed that there was no difference in effectiveness between trifluoperazine and low-potency antipsychotics. The number of people who dropped out due to any reason also did not differ. However, there were more people in the trifluoperazine group who experienced some kind of movement side effect (such as tremors, restlessness, uncontrollable shaking or muscle spasms), making it difficult to draw conclusions about the overall acceptability of treatment based on these results alone. Unfortunately, other important topics such as death, sedation and quality of life were not reported. The number of studies and the quality of the studies were low, therefore further research is required to draw better conclusions about the effects of trifluoperazine in comparison with older, lower potency medications. The quality of the evidence was moderate for "response to treatment" and "number of people with at least one adverse effect", and very low for "movement disorders", "incoordination" and "rigor".

We searched the medical literature widely for studies comparing responsive feeding with scheduled feeding in preterm babies who were being weaned from intravenous nutrition to milk feeds given by a tube into a bottle or directly from a breast or bottle. We identified nine small studies (involving 593 infants) with some methodological weaknesses. The main findings of these studies were that responsive feeding appeared to accelerate the transition from tube to oral feeding but did not have a strong or consistent effect on other important outcomes for preterm infants.

The authors searched for all relevant studies evaluating the effect of treating high blood homocysteine levels with B vitamins in people with PAD. Two randomised controlled trials with a total of 161 participants were identified. Both studies compared a group of B vitamins (folic acid, vitamin B6 and vitamin B12) with a placebo. One study also compared a different form of folate (5-methyltetrahydrofolate) with placebo. Overall, the studies suggest that treatment with B vitamins may improve the blood flow in the leg but much larger trials would be needed to be sure of this. There were no reports of adverse effects or side-effects from the B vitamin supplements. We cannot recommend using B vitamins to treat people with high blood homocysteine and PAD at present. More research is needed.

We searched for relevant studies evaluating the impact of different payment systems on the care provided by primary care dentists up to July 2015. We found two studies that met our inclusion criteria. One study took place in some of the most deprived areas of Scotland and the other study was a large trial involving 503 dental practices across England. Both studies compared two types of payment: capitation payment (a fixed amount is paid for every patient on a dentist's list) and fee-for-service (a payment is made each time a specific service is provided). One study also tested whether a payment to dentists to undertake an educational module had an effect on how often they placed sealants in children's back teeth. What happens when dentists are paid differently? When primary care dentists are paid on a capitation basis, they tend to: - see their patients less often - do fewer fillings and extractions - give more preventive advice However, when primary care dentists are paid on a fee-for-service basis, they tend to: - see their patients more often - do more fillings and extractions - give less preventive advice There was no difference in the amount of money earned by dentists between the two payment systems. The body of evidence for how payment systems affect the care provided by primary care dentists is very small. The two included studies were of low quality and there were concerns about aspects of their designs. It is not possible to apply the results of this review to many countries as the payment systems are very different.

The review covered 21 randomised controlled trials (over 17,000 women) and found a 14% reduction in preterm births with zinc supplementation during pregnancy, a smaller effect than previously thought. There was no convincing evidence of other benefits and the authors suggest that improving general nutrition of women in poor countries may be more important than supplementation with individual nutrients.

We included 10 randomized controlled trials with 1656 participants. None of the trials were funded by the pharmaceutical industry. All trials evaluated the use of probiotics as add-on treatment to antifungal drugs. This review found that the use of probiotics in addition to antifungal drugs leads to a higher rate of short-term clinical and mycological cure and a lower relapse rate at one month. However, this effect was not maintained at one and three months after the end of treatment. The use of probiotics did not affect the rate of adverse events. The overall quality of evidence was low or very low for all outcomes. There is a need for new studies with better methodological quality and longer follow-up.

This review includes seven randomised controlled studies involving 696 women with early pregnancy complications (before 24 weeks of gestation). These were carried out in several countries around the world. Six of the trials used progestogens (natural or synthetic hormones similar to the naturally occurring hormone progesterone) and one trial used natural progesterone. Four trials compared progestogens to a placebo and three trials compared progestogens to no treatment. Two trials included women with threatened miscarriage diagnosed by ultrasound, and five trials included women with threatened miscarriage diagnosed by bleeding. The follow-up period for all studies was until the end of the pregnancy. This review found that progestogens are probably effective in reducing the risk of miscarriage when compared to placebo or no treatment but may have little or no effect on the rate of preterm birth. The evidence about whether progestogens have any effect on the rate of congenital abnormalities is uncertain. The overall quality of the evidence was moderate for both miscarriage and preterm birth. The quality of the evidence for congenital abnormalities was very low because there were only two small trials with very few babies and the studies were at a high risk of bias.

We searched the literature up to September 2015 and identified five randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that met our inclusion criteria. These included a total of 4786 people (9503 eyes). Four RCTs were set in the USA, one in the UK and one in Japan. The age range of participants was from 30 to 77 years. Four of the studies evaluated laser treatment usingargon laser and one study investigated use of another type of laser. Three studies compared laser treatment to no intervention and two studies compared laser treatment to deferred laser treatment. The benefits of laser treatment are long lasting but not immediately apparent. There was little difference in vision at one year between those who received laser and those who did not receive laser treatment (or had deferred treatment), but at five years there was a benefit to treatment with laser. Laser treatment reduced the risk of severe visual loss by over 50% at one year and significantly reduced the risk of progression of diabetic retinopathy with a similar reduction in risk of vitreous haemorrhage (bleeding from new blood vessels in the eye). However, none of the studies reported near vision (i.e. reading vision) or patient-relevant outcomes such as quality of life, pain, loss of driving licence or adverse effects such as retinal detachment. The overall quality of evidence was moderate or low, depending on the outcome. This is partly related to the fact that some of the trials were old and conducted before more advanced forms of laser treatment (panretinal photocoagulation) became available. This review shows that laser treatment is effective for people with certain stages of diabetic retinopathy. We have planned further Cochrane Reviews on variations in the laser treatment protocol. Future research on laser photocoagulation should investigate the combination of laser photocoagulation with newer treatments such as anti-vascular endothelial growth factors (anti-VEGFs).

We searched the literature up to 7 July 2016 and identified two randomised controlled trials that compared non-invasive ventilation with oxygen therapy. These studies included 269 participants who were mostly men with an average age of 65 years. Both studies recruited participants from intensive care units in China and Italy (one was a multicentre study). The participants had breathing difficulties after upper abdominal surgery. One study looked at the effects of CPAP and one study looked at the effects of bilevel NPPV. The duration of treatment ranged from 10 minutes to four hours. The two studies were small and at high risk of bias. We found that CPAP and bilevel NPPV may reduce the need for intubation. The combined results showed that for every 11 people treated with CPAP or NPPV, one fewer person needs to be intubated. It was not clear if there was any difference between the interventions in terms of death, hospital length of stay, anastomotic leakage, pneumonia-related complications, and sepsis or infections. Blood analysis one hour after the intervention showed better oxygen levels and pH (acidity) in those who received NPPV. There was no information on the following outcomes that we wished to assess: gastric insufflation, fistulae (an abnormal passage), pneumothorax (air in the space between the lung and chest wall), bleeding, skin breakdown, eye irritation, sinus congestion, oronasal drying, and patient-ventilator asynchrony (when the patient and ventilator breathe at the same time). The overall quality of the evidence was low or very low. We can draw no definitive conclusions regarding the use of CPAP or NPPV in adults with breathing difficulties after upper abdominal surgery. More good quality studies are needed to confirm whether the benefits associated with these treatments are durable over a longer period of time.

This review identified four controlled studies involving 388 women who received analgesia for pain relief for forceps delivery. The drugs used for pain relief were diazepam, ketamine, vinydan-ether and lignocaine. These were given either by mouth, into the muscle or into the spine. The type of pain relief given had no effect on the success of the procedure or on the birthweight of the baby. Women who had diazepam were less likely to vomit than those receiving vinydan-ether. Babies born to mothers receiving diazepam showed slightly lower Apgar scores at five minutes than babies born to mothers receiving vinydan-ether but this may have been due to the fact that more of these mothers had epidurals. Babies born to mothers receiving spinal analgesia had slightly lower Apgar scores at five minutes than babies born to mothers receiving pudendal nerve block. No drug caused any serious problems for mother or baby, although information on this outcome was limited. More research is needed to determine which form of pain relief is best.

We looked at the research published up to 25 November 2016. We found 15 studies which met our inclusion criteria. These studies included a total of 1048 people with asthma aged between six months and 72 years of age. They had received varying levels of yoga instruction from three times a week to daily for a period of two weeks to as long as five years. The studies suggested that yoga may lead to small improvements in quality of life and symptoms of asthma, but the results were not consistent. There was also some evidence that yoga may help to reduce the amount of medication people with asthma need to take, however the studies did not find any improvement in lung function. While most studies did not report any side effects, there was some concern about the quality of the research methods in many of the studies. We therefore judged the overall quality of evidence to be moderate, meaning that while we are reasonably confident in the findings of this review, there is a possibility that further research could change our conclusions.

We found 10 randomised controlled trials with a total of 249 participants. Most of the people who took part had moderately severe or severe disease. The trials used a variety of different measures to record how well the treatment worked, but most used a scale of muscle strength. Most of the trials lasted for about a year, but some were shorter. One study did not report its source of funding; three were funded by academic sponsors; four by pharmaceutical companies (two by two different Sanofi-Aventis companies and one each by Pfizer Inc and Merck & Co); and one by a charitable foundation. Interferon beta-1a and methotrexate were tested in two trials each, but neither was shown to be effective in slowing down the progression of IBM. An open trial of anti-T lymphocyte globulin combined with methotrexate suggested an advantage for the combination treatment, but this was a small study with some concerns about its reliability. We were unable to combine the results from three trials of intravenous immunoglobulin because of differences in the way the trials analysed their data, and we were not able to re-analyse the data ourselves. Trials of oxandrolone, azathioprine plus methotrexate, and arimoclomol did not provide usable results, but a full analysis of the effects of arimoclomol is still pending publication. Two trials are ongoing. There were problems with the design of all the analysed trials, which made their results less reliable. These problems included small numbers of participants and a short duration of just under two years, which may not be long enough to detect an effect on a slowly progressing condition like IBM. None of the trials fully reported harmful effects of the treatments, and no trial considered harms to be an important part of the trial. The overall quality of the evidence was low or very low, meaning that we cannot be confident in the results. More research is needed to find a treatment that can stop or slow the progression of IBM. Future trials need to be designed to be of higher quality, with more people taking part and lasting for a longer time.

We identified nine randomised controlled trials with 3144 participants comparing the safety and effectiveness of linezolid with vancomycin in treating skin and soft tissue infections. The infections were caused by Staphylococcus aureus bacteria and sometimes methicillin-resistant S aureus (MRSA). All but one trial took place in the USA; the other trial took place in Canada. The study participants were aged between 6 and 79 years and had been diagnosed with an SSTI. They received either linezolid or vancomycin for seven to 14 days. One study also included a third treatment - cefazolin. The main outcomes were the number of people who were cured of their infection and the number who died as a result of the infection or treatment. The secondary outcomes were adverse events associated with treatment and cost of treatment. Adverse events included red man syndrome (a reaction to the infusion which causes flushing and rash), itching, rash, low platelet count, low white blood cell count, liver damage, kidney damage, hepatitis, gastrointestinal effects and hypertension. We found that linezolid was more effective than vancomycin at curing SSTIs, including those caused by MRSA. There was no clear difference in death rates. Adverse events were similar except for red man syndrome, itch, rash and hypertension, which were fewer with linezolid, and thrombocytopenia and nausea, which were more common with linezolid. We could not assess the reliability of the evidence because most of the studies were supported by the manufacturer of linezolid and there was some evidence of bias in the way patients were allocated to receive linezolid or vancomycin.

We included eight randomised controlled trials with a total of 512 adult patients who underwent pancreaticoduodenectomy for any type of pancreatic head cancer. The choice of the type of surgery was determined by a method similar to the toss of a coin so that the treatments were as similar as possible. We found no differences in how well the surgeries were performed or how the patients recovered from the surgery with either operation. One outcome, delayed gastric emptying, was better with the standard operation. The time taken to do the surgery, the amount of blood lost during the operation, and the need for blood transfusions were all better with the standard operation. Overall, there was no difference in how long people lived after each operation nor in the overall rate of death. However, our findings should be viewed with caution because of the small number of studies and the fact that many of these studies were not of very high quality.

The review found six studies with 1862 participants. The effect of calcium channel blockers on the risk of death was reported in five of the RCTs. The pooled odds ratio (OR) for the five studies was 0.91 (95% confidence interval [95% CI] 0.70 to 1.16). For the five RCTs that reported death and severe disability (unfavourable outcome), the pooled OR 0.97 (95% CI 0.81 to 1.18). In the two RCTs which reported the risk of death in a subgroup of traumatic subarachnoid haemorrhage patients, the pooled OR 0.59 (95% CI 0.37 to 0.94). Three RCTs reported death and severe disability as an outcome in this subgroup, and the pooled OR 0.67 (95% CI 0.46 to 0.98).

This review is current to June 2017. We found four randomized controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) with a total of 3090 enrolled participants. One of these four trials had a relatively high risk of bias, and the other three had a relatively low risk of bias. The results from our review were imprecise for all of the outcomes that we assessed. This was due to the small number of participants in each study as well as the fact that some of the studies did not fully report on their methods. We cannot be sure if the chances of being resuscitated successfully, surviving to hospital discharge or having no neurological injury from the heart attack are any different with the two treatments. Neither can we be sure that neither approach is better than the other for survival at one year. Neither approach had any adverse effects. We need better, larger studies to know which approach is better.

This review aimed to determine which interventions used during controlled ovarian stimulation are most beneficial for women who are considered 'poor responders' to ovarian stimulation. The review found that there is not enough evidence to support the use of any particular approach. More good quality studies are needed.

The aim of this review was to evaluate the effects of any kind of family intervention for people with schizophrenia. The authors wanted to know whether these interventions were effective in reducing relapse, hospital admission, general functioning and overall quality of life. They also looked at whether they had any effect on the outcome of leaving care early. The results of the review show that family intervention can lead to a reduction in relapse and hospital admission. It is less clear how much of an effect they have on other aspects of general functioning, such as work or relationships, or on the levels of stress and anxiety within the family. The authors recommend further studies are carried out to confirm the effectiveness of these interventions.

The authors of this review assessed the effectiveness and safety of drug-eluting balloons in the treatment of in-stent restenosis of the femoral artery. They searched scientific databases from their inception to May 2016 and identified three randomized controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) that met the inclusion criteria. All three trials examined the treatment of symptomatic in-stent restenosis within the femoropopliteal arteries (a large artery that is located deep in the thigh and supplies blood to the lower limb). These trials were carried out in Germany and Austria and used paclitaxel as the agent in the drug-eluting balloons. Two of the three trials were industry sponsored. Two companies manufactured the drug-eluting balloons (Eurocor, Bonn, Germany; Medtronic, Fridley, Minnesota, USA). The trials examined both anatomical and clinical endpoints. We noted heterogeneity in the frequency of bailout stenting deployment between studies as well as in the dosage of paclitaxel applied by the DEBs. Most participants were followed up to 12 months, but one trial followed participants for up to 24 months. Trial results show no difference in the incidence of amputation between DEBs and uncoated balloon angioplasty. DEBs showed better outcomes for up to 24 months for target lesion revascularization (the reconstruction of a blocked or narrowed artery that has previously been opened surgically or by angioplasty) and at six and 12 months for binary restenosis (narrowing of the artery that can be seen on a scan) and improvement of one or more Rutherford categories (an improvement in the level of peripheral arterial disease). Data show no clear differences in death between DEBs and uncoated balloon angioplasty. Data were insufficient for subgroup or sensitivity analyses to be conducted. Evidence suggests an advantage for DEBs compared with uncoated balloon angioplasty for anatomical endpoints such as target lesion revascularization and binary restenosis and for one clinical endpoint - improvement in Rutherford category post intervention for up to 24 months. However, the certainty of evidence for all these outcomes is very low due to the small number of included studies and participants and the high risk of bias in study design. Larger and better studies are needed to assess the role of drug-eluting technologies in the management of in-stent restenosis.

We searched relevant databases for all randomised controlled trials comparing steroids to placebo (a pretend treatment) or no intervention in children with Kawasaki disease. The search was last updated in January 2016. Seven studies with 922 participants were included in this review. Pooled results show that steroids reduced the likelihood of heart problems caused by Kawasaki disease and the length of time children spent in hospital. Steroid treatment also reduced the level of inflammatory markers in the blood, which can take longer to reduce in children with Kawasaki disease. We found no evidence on long-term (more than one year) heart problems. Further research is needed to establish which children would benefit most from steroid treatment, especially with regards to heart problems. The evidence presented here suggests that a long course of steroids should be considered for all children with Kawasaki disease until further studies are performed. Overall, the reliability of our findings was moderate to high.

This review examined the effectiveness of Parent-Infant Psychotherapy (PIP) in improving the mental health of parents and the attachment security of their infants. We searched databases until March 2015 and found eight randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that met our inclusion criteria. These studies included a total of 846 participants and examined the impact of PIP for mothers with postpartum depression, anxious or insecure attachment, a history of maltreatment, or who were incarcerated. We ran separate analyses comparing PIP with no treatment or treatment-as-usual and PIP with another treatment. The main outcomes were parental depression and anxiety, parent-child interaction, infant attachment and infant behaviour. When we compared PIP with no treatment or treatment-as-usual, there were no differences in the effects of PIP on parental depression or parent-reported levels of depression. However, there were some improvements in infant attachment security, including a decrease in avoidant attachment and an increase in the number of infants securely attached at post-treatment. There were no differences between PIP and another type of therapy (video-interaction guidance, psychoeducation, counselling or cognitive behavioural therapy) in the effects on parental depression, parent-child interaction, infant attachment and infant behaviour. There were no differences between PIP and control or alternative treatments in the remaining outcomes. The quality of the evidence was low or very low for most outcomes. This was due to various methodological weaknesses in the individual studies, including small numbers of participants and missing data. It should be noted that the review authors did not blind the researchers or participants to whether they received PIP or were in a control or alternative treatment group. Overall, the findings of this review suggest that PIP is a promising approach in terms of improving infant attachment security in high-risk families, but its effects are comparable to no treatment or treatment-as-usual, and other types of therapeutic interventions for parents and infants.

The use of routine supplementation with oxygen for women having a caesarean section under regional anaesthetic is a common practice. However, its effectiveness in terms of improving outcomes for the mother and her baby has not been proven. We included 11 randomised controlled studies with 753 women. The design of these studies was generally poor. The majority of the studies did not report on the primary outcome of the review, which was to assess the effect on the newborn's Apgar score at one and five minutes. Two studies reported funding from a pharmaceutical company, whereas the remainder did not state their sources of funding. No clear evidence was found that the babies of mothers who received oxygen during caesarean section had better Apgar scores at one or five minutes than those who breathed air. There was also no evidence of any difference in the number of births where the baby was stillborn or died shortly after birth (early neonatal mortality). Although the evidence for most of the outcomes assessed in this review was of low/very low quality, the findings were consistent. This suggests that there may be little or no difference in outcomes for the mother or her baby when the woman breathes oxygen during a caesarean section under regional anaesthesia.

We searched for randomised controlled trials which evaluated the effects of reminder packaging on adherence to oral medication in any setting. We included twelve studies in this review. We found that reminder packaging may help people to take their medication more regularly. When we combined the results of six trials we found that, on average, people using reminder packaging took an additional 11% of their medication. However, the results of two other trials showed no improvement in adherence. We also found evidence from two trials that reminder packaging can lead to lower blood pressure and less glycosylated haemoglobin in the blood (a measure of glucose control in diabetes). No adverse effects of reminder packaging were reported in the trials. However, these are unlikely as the packaging does not contain any active ingredients. The studies reviewed here focused on people with chronic illnesses such as heart disease, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus. We conclude that reminder packaging may represent a simple method for improving adherence for patients with selected conditions. However, before it can be recommended for routine use, further research is needed to improve the design and targeting of these devices.

This review showed that there are many ways to try to reduce the damage to the liver caused by not having enough blood flowing through it during surgery. However, the results of this review should be viewed with caution because of the small number of patients included in the trials and the potential for serious over-estimation of benefits and under-estimation of harms.

The evidence is current to September 2014. We found 61 articles from 25 countries. Fifty-two of these articles evaluated the occurrence of renal problems in 13,327 people of interest, of whom at least 4499 had undergone renal function testing. The occurrence of renal problems varied from 0% to 84%. Occurrence of chronic kidney disease (a permanently damaged kidney) varied from 2.4% to 32%, impaired glomerular filtration rate (GFR; amount of functional volume of the kidneys) from 0% to 74%, signs of glomerular dysfunction (damage to the filtering units of the kidneys) from 0% to 67%, proteinuria (presence of excess proteins in the urine) from 3.5% to 84%, hypophosphataemia (low level of phosphate in the blood) from 0% to 36%, impaired tubular phosphate reabsorption (kidney's ability to reabsorb phosphate) from 0% to 63%, and hypertension (high blood pressure) from 0% to 50%. We found no studies on the occurrence of acute kidney injury (sudden damage to the kidneys). Given the large differences in the occurrence of renal problems and in the type of treatment used, it was not possible to perform a combined analysis of the studies. Furthermore, most studies had some methodological weaknesses. Therefore, we cannot give any definitive answers with regard to the occurrence of renal problems and treatment-related risk factors. It is worth noting that all studies had some limitations in their design. Future studies should focus on improving the quality of research on this topic. In the meantime, we strongly advise all childhood cancer survivors to enroll in a long-term follow-up programme to monitor their renal function and blood pressure.

The review authors searched for trials on the use of plant medicines in people with sickle cell disease and found two randomised controlled trials of two different herbal medications, Niprisan® (also called Nicosan®) and Ciklavit® (from the seed of Cajanus cajan). These trials suggested that these two plant medications may be beneficial in reducing the number of severe sickle cell crises and improving exercise capacity. There were no reports of any side effects from the use of these herbal medications. However, the trials had their limitations and the results need to be interpreted cautiously. Further studies on the use of herbal medications in people with sickle cell disease are needed to confirm these preliminary findings.

We searched important medical databases for clinical trials comparing PET-guided treatment (PET-adapted therapy) with standard treatment (with or without PET scanning) in adults with HL. The evidence is current to February 2016. We included three trials with 1999 participants, which evaluated 1879 participants, reporting progression-free survival, but none reported any overall survival data. We found that, to date, only two small studies have reported on adverse events (short- and long-term), but neither has reported on any long-term adverse effects or quality of life. There were no data on overall survival, response rate or treatment-related mortality. We found moderate-quality evidence that progression-free survival was shorter in people with early-stage HL and a negative PET scan who received chemotherapy only (PET-adapted therapy) than in those receiving chemotherapy and radiotherapy (standard therapy). More studies with longer follow-up periods would be needed to obtain more information on adverse effects, treatment-related mortality and quality of life, and to find out if this approach translates into better overall survival.

Until July 2014, we did computer searches for randomized controlled trials of birth control methods with hormones and studies comparing the same type of pill or other hormonal method with another. Birth control pills included types with both estrogen and progestin and pills with only progestin. Also included were the vaginal ring, injection, and implant. We also wrote to researchers to find other trials. The studies could compare different types of birth control or the same type with a break in use. The women did not have diabetes when they entered the trial. Of 31 included studies, 21 compared birth control pills with either a different type of pill or a different dose. Eight studies looked at pills with the hormone desogestrel versus pills with levonorgestrel (a different hormone). Differences between the pills were small. One study showed a higher fasting glucose for desogestrel pills. Another study showed a higher glucose response two hours after eating and higher insulin level for desogestrel pills. Five studies looked at pills with the hormone norethisterone. One study showed less change in glucose response two hours after eating for a pill with norethisterone than for a pill with levonorgestrel. For an injectable, higher glucose levels were seen with depot medroxyprogesterone acetate than with norethisterone enanthate. Two newer pills with a different estrogen showed better glucose values than levonorgestrel. An extended-release pill had a greater effect on glucose than a type that came out every month. In a small study, women who took the lower of two low-dose pills did not have higher glucose than those who took the higher dose. The studies generally did not look at side effects or whether the birth control method affected the chance of getting pregnant later. Most of the studies had fewer women than we would like to get a clear result. Not all studies reported how the women were assigned to the different treatments.

We searched for randomised controlled trials (a type of scientific experiment in which people are randomly allocated to one or more treatment groups) looking at the effects of exercise in people with hip osteoarthritis (OA); we found 10 studies with 509 participants. We were interested in studies that looked at the effects of exercise in terms of pain, physical function (activities of daily living), quality of life and adverse events (side effects). We summarised the results of four studies using patient-reported pain measures, which showed that exercise reduced pain by an average of 8 points on a 0- to 100-point scale. This improvement in pain was similar whether the exercise programme focused on the hip or the knee. Five studies measured physical function using activities of daily living questionnaires and reported that exercise improved physical function by an average of 7 points. Again, this improvement was similar whether the exercise programme focused on the hip or the knee. Only three studies measured quality of life, and found no benefit of exercise. No serious side effects were reported and any adverse events that were reported were due to increased pain. The improvements in pain and physical function were maintained at least three to six months after completing the exercise programme. We rated the quality of the evidence as high for pain and physical function, moderate for adverse events and low for quality of life, as there were few studies reporting quality of life and they were small. We conclude that exercising can improve pain and physical function in people with hip OA.

The authors of this review tried to assess whether exercise therapy can improve the lives of people with MS. They searched scientific databases for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing exercise therapy to either a non-exercise treatment or to another type of exercise therapy. Nine RCTs with a total of 260 participants were included in the review. The findings suggested that exercise therapy may lead to small improvements in muscle strength, walking speed and overall ability to engage in physical activity. However, the results also indicated that there are many variables that affect the outcome of exercise therapy and more research is needed.

We performed a thorough search of the medical literature in order to identify studies that compared early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 24 hours of the onset of symptoms) with delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy (more than 24 hours but less than six months after the onset of symptoms) for people with symptomatic gallstones. We identified one study that met our inclusion criteria. This study included 75 participants who were randomly assigned to an early operation (within 24 hours) or a delayed operation (at an average of four months). Only information on deaths and complications requiring a hospital visit could be obtained for all participants. Information on the other outcomes was available for less than half of the participants. There were no deaths reported in either group. There were no major complications related to the surgery in either group. The participants in the delayed group developed several complications during the waiting period between their initial presentation and their scheduled operation. These complications included pancreatitis (inflammation in the pancreas), empyema of the gallbladder (pus accumulating in the gallbladder), gallbladder perforation (a hole in the gallbladder), acute cholecystitis (inflammation of the gallbladder), cholangitis (infection of the bile duct system), obstructive jaundice (yellowing of the skin due to a build up of bile in the body), and biliary colic (pain caused by a stone blocking the bile duct). A total of 14 participants required hospital admissions for the above-mentioned symptoms. All of these admissions occurred in the delayed group as all the participants were operated on within 24 hours in the early group. The proportion of people who developed serious adverse events was 0/28 (0%) in the early group, which was significantly lower than in the delayed laparoscopic cholecystectomy group 9/40 (22.5%) (P = 0.0082). This trial did not report quality of life or return to work. There was no significant difference in the proportion of people who required conversion to open cholecystectomy (where a larger incision is made in the abdomen) in the early group 0/28 (0%) compared with the delayed group 6/35 (17.1%). There was a statistically significant shorter hospital stay in the early group than in the delayed group by an average of 1.25 days. There was a statistically significant shorter operating time in the early group than the delayed group by an average of 14.80 minutes. We conclude that based on evidence from only one trial with a high risk of systematic errors (ie, there was not a equal chance of being in either group) and random errors (there were results were not exactly the same each time the study was conducted), that early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (less than 24 hours after diagnosis of biliary colic) decreases the rate of complications during the waiting period for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy (mean waiting time 4.2 months), hospital stay, and operating time. However, more trials are needed to confirm or refute this finding.

We searched several important medical databases on 25 May 2016 and found 11 relevant studies that met the inclusion criteria of our review. Seven of these studies provided usable data for the main comparison and the primary outcome. These seven studies randomised 4732 participants who were undergoing orthognathic surgery to receive either long-term antibiotics, short-term antibiotics or a single dose of antibiotics before their operation. The source of funding of four of the seven studies was unclear, two were funded by pharmaceutical companies related to the antibiotics used, one was funded by a government grant and one stated that it received no funding. Key results Long-term antibiotics probably reduce the risk of developing a surgical site infection after jaw surgery (moderate-quality evidence). We are uncertain whether short-term antibiotics are better than a single dose of antibiotics (low-quality evidence). No reports described adverse effects associated with the drugs in those trials that reported in this outcome. No trial followed up patients to assess how the treatment options affected their quality of life. The evidence in this review is current to May 2016.

This review aimed to compare the effects of planned preterm birth (elective preterm birth) versus waiting for labour to start spontaneously on outcomes for babies with gastroschisis and their mothers. We found only one randomised controlled study (involving 40 infants and 42 women) that compared planned preterm birth at 36 weeks' gestation versus waiting for labour to start spontaneously. This study was too small to be sure if either policy was better in terms of newborn survival, incidence of major complications, or impact on the mother.

We searched the literature on 20 May 2016 and found three studies involving 122 adults who received paracetamol 650 mg or a placebo (sugar pill) in addition to their usual painkillers. These studies lasted up to one week and took place in Denmark, France, and the UK. The participants had various types of cancer and some were also taking other painkillers. No studies in children were found. The results suggest that paracetamol did not significantly reduce pain perception compared to placebo but we cannot say this with certainty. There was no difference in side effects between those who received paracetamol and those who received placebo. Overall, the reliability of the studies was very low, so we are highly uncertain about the results.

The authors searched for randomised controlled trials in children with nocturnal enuresis which investigated the effectiveness of drug treatment. Fifteen studies met the inclusion criteria. The majority of the trials were conducted in China and all in hospital settings. The trial quality was poor with little information about how patients were allocated to receive one treatment or another, and there was much missing information in the reports which made it difficult to draw reliable conclusions. However, the findings of the review suggest that some drugs are more effective than others in treating bedwetting. Antihistamines and anticholinergics appear to be more effective than placebo. There were some adverse effects reported, mainly dry mouth and drowsiness, especially with the use of anticholinergics. However, the studies did not report on other potential side effects of these medications and the follow up of the children in the trials was short. Overall, there is not enough evidence to recommend any particular medication for this condition.

We found six randomized controlled trials (RCTs), which included a total of 195 participants with multiple sclerosis. A total of 18 participants (˜ 10%) dropped out due to various reasons, but all trials reported no serious side effects. Most study participants had mild to moderate disease. The duration of the training lasted from four weeks to 24 weeks. Two RCTs investigated inspiratory muscle training with a threshold device; three RCTs, expiratory muscle training with a threshold device; and one RCT, regular breathing exercises. Threshold devices are devices that provide variable resistance during breathing, which increases as the user tries to breathe in or out against the device. Breathing exercises without threshold device were included in this review because they are widely used by people with MS and were evaluated in one RCT. The average age of the study participants was between 43 years and 60 years. The duration of the studies ranged from four weeks to 24 weeks. The fact that most of the studies were short term suggests that the effects of training have not been fully explored. The results showed that training the inspiratory muscles with a threshold device is moderately effective postintervention for improving the strength of the inspiratory muscles in people with MS. Training the expiratory muscles with a threshold device showed no significant effect. No study adequately assessed changes in quality of life. There was no evidence of any harmful effects of training, but the studies were too small and short to address this adequately. Overall, the quality of the evidence was low. Future studies should aim to establish whether any benefits of training are long lasting and whether training has an impact on overall well-being. They should also seek to determine if the benefits are applicable to people with more severe forms of MS.

This review included one trial with 38 women (41 pregnancies) who were randomly assigned to either betamethasone or no medication. The trial found no clear evidence that betamethasone was effective in increasing platelet count or reducing the risk of neonatal thrombocytopenia or bleeding. This review is limited by the fact that there is only one small trial with data on outcomes for the mother and her baby. There is no evidence from this review that betamethasone is effective in treating ITP in pregnancy. There is insufficient evidence to support its use.

Data from two randomised controlled trials, with a total of 111 participants, were included in this review. Both studies took place in Iran and recruited participants who had moderate to severe keratoconus. Participants were assigned to treatment with either deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty or full thickness penetrating keratoplasty. The smaller study had 12 month follow-up data for all participants. For the larger study, follow-up length for the remaining 77 participants ranged from 6.8 to 36.4 months, with all followed up for at least three months after the removal of the sutures. There were no differences found between the two techniques in terms of vision, astigmatism or spherical correction at any of the time points examined in this review. Rejection of the donated cornea occurred in both treatments but was more common in the penetrating keratoplasty group. However, there were problems with the design of the trial that means we cannot be certain about the accuracy of this finding. No cases of graft failure were reported in the DALK group of either study. Four cases of DALK did not succeed in the larger study because of technical difficulties, but these eyes were not included in the analysis. Complications of the surgery, of varying severity, were reported in both treatment groups and included post-operative astigmatism, steroid-induced raised eye pressure and persistent wound defect. Overall, the quality of the evidence was assessed as being very low to moderate across all outcomes. This was due to various methodological shortcomings in the two studies as well as the imprecision of findings and the fact that data from one study could not be included in the analysis.

We included 76 published reports, which contained information from 67 randomised controlled trials of daily iron supplementation, with a total of 8506 women. The trials lasted for five days to 12 months. We found that daily iron supplementation effectively reduces the prevalence of anaemia and iron deficiency, raises haemoglobin and iron stores, improves exercise performance and reduces symptomatic fatigue. However, almost a quarter of all women experienced some adverse gastrointestinal symptoms, including constipation, diarrhoea, nausea, stomach pain and bloating. Due to safety concerns, four trials were excluded from the analysis. Three of these trials included women with heavy menstrual bleeding and one included women with inflammatory bowel disease. Overall, we judged the quality of the evidence to be low to moderate. This review of 67 randomised controlled trials, with over 8500 women, provides high quality evidence that daily iron supplementation effectively reduces the prevalence of anaemia and iron deficiency, raises haemoglobin and iron stores, improves exercise performance and reduces symptomatic fatigue. However, it also shows that over 20% of women experience adverse gastrointestinal side effects, so women need to carefully weigh up these risks and benefits when considering whether to take iron supplements.

We performed a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials to compare the efficacy and safety of the combination of irinotecan and either 5-FU or capecitabine versus irinotecan alone in patients with advanced or metastatic colorectal cancer. A total of 1,726 patients were analysed from five published studies. We did not find any evidence to suggest that the addition of 5-FU or capecitabine to irinotecan improves overall survival. However, we found that the combination may prolong progression-free survival, but this finding should be interpreted with caution as it was based on only one large study. Common adverse events such as diarrhoea and alopecia occurred more frequently in the combination therapy arm, while there were fewer cases of neutropenia. Overall, the evidence for overall survival was of low quality, mainly due to concerns about how the study was conducted, while the quality of evidence for progression-free survival was moderate.

The authors concluded that a transverse or oblique incision is best suited to most patients because it causes less damage to the diaphragm and therefore would have less impact on breathing after the operation. However, the choice of incision type ultimately depends upon the surgeon's preference and the type of operation being performed.

This updated review includes nine randomized controlled trials with a total of 981 participants who were aged between 23 to 73 years. Five studies were conducted in Europe and four in North America. The duration of the treatment programs ranged from two to 16 weeks. In comparison with usual care, people with subacute back pain who received manipulative and biomechanical retraining had less pain and less disability, and were more likely to return to work and have fewer sick leave days. However, the size of this benefit was small. When comparing MBR to other treatments (i.e. brief intervention with features from a light mobilization program and a graded activity program, functional restoration, brief clinical intervention including education and advice on exercise, and psychological counselling), we found no difference in pain, disability, or time away from work. None of the included studies reported whether there were any side effects caused by manipulation. As it is not clear whether MBR is better than other treatments, and since the quality of the studies was low, future high-quality research is needed to determine the value of MBR for people with subacute low-back pain.

We searched several important medical databases for clinical trials comparing the effects of bisphosphonates to no bisphosphonates or other treatments in men with prostate cancer and bone metastases. The search is up-to-date as of April 2014. We identified 18 trials with 4843 participants. Pain was assessed with various scales and questionnaires. Quality of life was assessed with various scales but we could not extract any usable data. We found no clear evidence that bisphosphonates result in a higher pain response rate than control regimens (no treatment or placebo). However, bisphosphonates probably reduce the number of skeletal-related events (SREs) (e.g. fracture, radiotherapy to bones) and the number of participants with disease progression. One thousand men would need to be treated with bisphosphonates to prevent one case of SRE or one case of disease progression. On the other hand, 1000 men would have to experience at least one adverse event (renal impairment, nausea) related to bisphosphonates. This means that for every 1000 men treated with bisphosphonates, there would be one less case of SRE or disease progression but also one additional case of renal impairment or nausea. The relative risks for mortality and serious adverse events were similar in both intervention groups. The quality of the evidence was low for pain response, very low for osteonecrosis of the jaw and acceptability of treatment, and moderate for all other outcomes.

We searched several important medical databases such as the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), MEDLINE and EMBASE until June 2012. Five eligible studies met our inclusion criteria: four studies in previously untreated patients and one study in patients with relapsed disease. All five studies compare high-dose therapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation to standard chemotherapy or immunotherapy with or without rituximab, a monoclonal antibody directed against CD20 expressing B-cells. The quality of the five included studies is moderate. Due to the small number of studies in each analysis (four or less), we could not assess whether the studies were heterogeneous. We found that there is an advantage in progression-free survival for patients treated with high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplantation compared to conventional therapy in previously untreated follicular non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. However, there is no overall survival advantage for this treatment yet. Toxicities occurred more often in the high-dose therapy arm but were manageable. In patients with relapsed disease, there is evidence that high-dose therapy and autologous stem cell transplantation leads to a longer overall and progression-free survival. However, this result is based on only one small study.

The Information Specialist of Cochrane Schizophrenia ran an electronic search of their specialised register up to 30 March 2015 for trials that randomised people with schizophrenia to receive either WDD or standard treatment. We found 15 trials that could be included. These trials randomised 1437 adults with schizophrenia to receive WDD or standard treatment. Most trials compared WDD added to antipsychotic medication with standard care (the antipsychotic medication only). One trial compared WDD with placebo (a sham treatment) and one trial compared WDD with another type of psychological therapy called cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT). We found some evidence that WDD improved the short-term global state of participants more than placebo or no treatment, however when WDD was compared with other antipsychotic drugs there was no effect on global or mental state, but WDD was associated with fewer side effects. When WDD was used as an add-on treatment with an antipsychotic, positive effects were found for global and mental state, and the combination caused fewer side effects. The quality of evidence was low. More research is needed to fully and fairly test the effects of WDD for people with schizophrenia.

Authors from Cochrane Oral Health carried out this review of existing studies and the evidence is current up to 15 January 2018. We included 12 studies published from 1999 to 2016 in which a total of 799 teenagers aged between 10 and 18 years received treatment with different types of arch wires made of various materials. The studies compared: • stainless steel wires versus nickel-titanium (NiTi) wires • stainless steel wires versus thermoelastic NiTi wires • conventional NiTi wires versus superelastic NiTi wires • conventional NiTi wires versus thermoelastic NiTi wires • single-strand superelastic NiTi wires versus coaxial superelastic NiTi wires • superelastic NiTi wires versus thermoelastic NiTi wires All studies evaluated the effects on tooth movement and pain. None evaluated root resorption. Greater movement does not necessarily mean faster treatment. The evidence available for each comparison was of low to very low quality. For single-strand superelastic nickel-titanium wires compared to coaxial superelastic nickel-titanium wires, there was moderate-quality evidence of greater tooth movement over 12 weeks with the coaxial superelastic nickel-titanium wires. For multi-strand stainless steel wires compared to superelastic nickel-titanium wires, there was moderate-quality evidence of no difference in pain on the first day of wear. For the other comparisons, there was insufficient evidence to determine whether any particular arch wire material was better than any other in terms of rate of alignment, time to alignment, pain and root resorption.

We found only one study that assessed the effects of de-escalation for agitated behaviours in nursing homes. This study was funded by the French Ministry of Health and Social Care and the National Institute for Research in Psychiatry (INSERM). It took place in 16 nursing homes in France and included 306 people with dementia aged between 75 and 103 years of age. Participants had an average of six agitated episodes per week, lasting between two and 10 minutes. The study compared de-escalation with usual care. The de-escalation programme consisted of 10 educational sessions provided over a three-month period, which covered topics such as recognition of agitated behaviour, observational skills, communication techniques, and conflict resolution. The study measured changes in agitated behaviour using three scales: the Cohen-Mansfield Agitation Inventory (CMAI), the Neuropsychiatric Inventory (NPI), and the Observation Scale (OS). The CMAI assesses agitated behaviour and includes questions about physically non-aggressive behaviours (such as pacing and restlessness), verbally non-aggressive behaviours (such as repetition and questioning), physically aggressive behaviours (such as hitting and pushing), and verbally aggressive behaviours (such as swearing and cursing). The NPI is similar to the CMAI but includes questions about delusions and hallucinations as well as the behaviours already mentioned. The OS focuses on the observation of agitated behaviours. The study reported improvement in all four CMAI scales (global and the four domains) in favour of the de-escalation programme. However, it did not report whether there was any reduction in challenging behaviours, hospital admissions, or use of medications. Furthermore, the study did not measure our primary outcomes and, therefore, cannot be considered sufficient to guide practice or inform policy. We judged the quality of this evidence to be very low. There is an ongoing study that may provide more information in the future.

In September 2016 we found 13 relevant randomised controlled trials with 1316 participants. Nine trials compared different topical skin care products, such as a combination of products. Two trials tested a structured skin care procedure. One trial compared topical skin care products alongside frequencies of application. One trial compared frequencies of application of topical skin care products. We found little evidence on the effect of interventions for preventing and treating IAD in adults. The overall quality of evidence was low to very low. We found that soap and water performed poorly in the prevention and treatment of IAD. A skin cleanser might be more effective than soap and water. A structured skin care procedure, which includes cleansing, moisturising, and protecting the skin, might also be more effective than soap and water. Avoiding soap and applying a leave-on product (e.g. a moisturiser or skin protectant, or a combination of these) seems to be more effective than not applying any leave-on product. However, there were no studies on the satisfaction of patients with their treatment and no information on side effects. There is a need for high quality trials that test products and procedures that have been standardised and compared in similar ways, especially in different settings (e.g. in hospitals and nursing homes), to increase the certainty of the evidence. We recommend the development of a core outcome set, including validated measurement tools.

We identified seven randomised controlled trials with a total of 333 participants. We found that it is unclear whether giving antiviral drugs to people with IM improves their recovery from the condition. Some study results suggest that antiviral medications may help improve the symptoms of IM, reduce the time taken to recover, and reduce the time it takes for swollen lymph nodes to return to normal size. However, these findings were based on relatively small numbers of participants and so may not be reliable. The studies did not report any serious side effects caused by the antiviral medications, but we cannot be certain that any observed side effects were not due to the participants' underlying condition rather than the medication. Overall, we are very uncertain about the effects of using antiviral medications to treat IM. This is because the number of studies is small, the quality of these studies is poor, and the results of some of the studies contradict each other.

In this review we compared the long-acting insulins insulin detemir and insulin glargine in people with type 2 diabetes mellitus who were inadequately controlled on oral antidiabetic drugs. Four studies fulfilled our inclusion criteria. Insulin detemir was associated with similar glycaemic control, risk of hypoglycaemia and weight gain as insulin glargine. Insulin detemir was dosed once-daily in three studies and twice-daily in one study. We conclude that insulin detemir and insulin glargine are comparable treatment options for people with type 2 diabetes mellitus.

We performed a systematic review of the literature on the effectiveness and safety of sacral nerve stimulation for the treatment of faecal incontinence and constipation. We searched the scientific literature databases in April 2013 for studies that evaluated the effectiveness and safety of sacral nerve stimulation. We identified eight randomised clinical trials that met the inclusion criteria. Six studies assessed the effects of sacral nerve stimulation for faecal incontinence and two for constipation. The studies on faecal incontinence involved a total of 196 participants with a mean age of 58 years and severity of faecal incontinence ranging from mild to severe. The studies on constipation involved a total of 61 participants with a mean age of 48 years. We found that sacral nerve stimulation improved faecal incontinence in some but not all of the studies. However, information was not sufficiently robust to allow any firm conclusions to be drawn as to its overall effectiveness for this symptom. Sacral nerve stimulation did not improve constipation. Some adverse events were reported in those studies that reported them. These included pain at the site of the operation, seroma, and excess tingling in the vaginal region. Further rigorous trials are necessary to assess the effects of sacral nerve stimulation with more certainty.

The aim of this review was to evaluate the success of fixed appliances used to correct the position of the upper molar teeth in children and adolescents aged under 18 years. Two studies met the inclusion criteria of the review. Both studies compared the use of bands to the use of tubes and neither study had any conflicts of interest. The studies were considered to be at low risk of bias. One study was supported by an educational grant from a dental manufacturer but there was no suggestion of bias in the way the information was analysed or presented. There were fewer failures with the bands than with the tubes, both in terms of the number of first time failures and the total number of failures. One study looked at decay around the teeth and found less evidence of decay when the bands were used. There were no other differences between the two types of appliance in terms of other potential problems associated with their use.

Sixty-six studies met our inclusion criteria and were included in the review. In general, the methodological quality of the included studies was good. The main limitations of the studies were related to the inconsistency of the results and the fact that the studies did not report how the clinicians interpreting the endoscopic ultrasonography were blinded to the results of the other preoperative investigations. Overall, the findings of our review suggest that EUS can provide additional information to endoscopic appearance alone, and that the combination of both may improve the preoperative staging of early gastric cancer. However, a significant variability in the performance of EUS was observed among the included studies, and further studies are needed to identify the factors responsible for this variability. Furthermore, our results show that EUS has poor accuracy in distinguishing superficial tumors (T1a versus T1b) and assessing the extent of lymph node involvement, with relevant consequences on treatment and prognosis.

We included six randomised clinical trials with a total of 492 participants. All 6 trials compared day-surgery laparoscopic cholecystectomy with overnight stay surgery. The number of participants in the trials varied from 28 to 150. The percentage of women in the trials varied between 74% and 84%. The average age of the participants varied between 40 and 47 years. Only one trial reported the number of deaths in the first month after surgery (short-term mortality). It reported no deaths in either group and we inferred that there were no short-term deaths in the remaining trials. Long-term mortality was not reported in any of the trials. There was no significant difference in the rate of major complications (involving death, need for transfusion, infection, wound breakdown, needing reoperation or a combination thereof) between the two groups. There was no significant difference in quality of life between the two groups nor in the outcomes of pain, time to return to activity and return to work. There was also no difference in hospital readmission rate, in the proportion of people requiring hospital readmissions and in the proportion of failed discharges between the two groups. We could not reach any firm conclusions about the relative benefits and harms of day-surgery versus overnight stay surgery laparoscopic cholecystectomy because of the small number of participants in the trials and because some trials were of poor methodological quality.

We found eight clinical trials involving 2488 adults with long-lasting (more than 3 months) nerve pain after shingles attacks, pain from damaged nerves caused by diabetes, HIV-related nerve damage, or surgery. Most studies recruited participants with moderate or severe pain. All studies tested capsaicin against a cream with much lower concentration of capsaicin (a 'placebo'). Studies lasted 8 to 16 weeks. We found that high-concentration capsaicin was clearly better than placebo for relieving pain in people with postherpetic neuralgia (nerve pain after shingles), HIV-neuropathy (damage to peripheral nerves caused by HIV), and painful diabetic neuropathy (nerve pain caused by diabetes). For each condition, about 1 in 10 more people experienced a 'good response' to high-concentration capsaicin than to placebo. This is a moderate-sized result, but the overall quality of the evidence was only moderate or very low, so we cannot be completely sure of it. However, for those who do get good pain relief with capsaicin, they often also have additional benefits such as improved sleep, reduced fatigue, less depression, and better quality of life. There are some side effects, mostly mild or moderate ones such as skin irritation, but these were no more common with capsaicin than with placebo. The most important message from this review is that high-concentration capsaicin may give good pain relief for people whose nerve pain has not responded to other treatments.

This review includes three large randomised controlled trials with a total of 6343 participants who had carotid endarterectomy. The trials were conducted in the USA, Europe and Australia between 1998 and 2008. All three studies used antiplatelet drugs to reduce the risk of clotting. Two used anticoagulants to prevent clotting, and one used neither. The methods of measuring carotid narrowing were different, as were the ways that the trials defined stroke. We took the most up-to-date published information and re-analysed the data from the trials, but not all trials reported all the information we needed so we obtained the individual participant data from the trialists. This allowed us to re-assess the carotid narrowing and outcomes from all three trials, and to redefine outcome events where necessary, to make them comparable. There were no overall differences in benefits or harms between the treatment groups in the pooled analysis. When we looked at the results from participants with different degrees of carotid narrowing, we found that surgery reduced the risk of stroke in people with moderate narrowing (50% to 69%) and was highly beneficial for people with severe narrowing (70% to 99%), but there was no benefit for people with near-occlusion. The quality of the evidence was moderate for participants with moderate narrowing and high for those with near-occlusion. Overall, the results continue to show that carotid endarterectomy can be effective in reducing the risk of stroke in people with carotid stenosis, but there are still some uncertainties.

We searched multiple electronic databases and conference proceedings up to May 2016. We found only one randomized controlled trial (a type of scientific experiment that compares people who receive one treatment to those who receive another) and 10 non-randomized studies that assessed the effects of the two surgery types on postoperative complications in people having a stoma placed for any indication. The participants of the included studies had an average age of 58 years and 62% were men.
