The indications for enterostomy placement and the baseline characteristics of the participants (age, other health conditions, severity of the underlying disease) varied between studies. The results between the two surgery types were inconclusive for the occurrence of parastomal hernia (a bulging of the bowel through a weakness in the abdominal wall), bowel obstruction (caused by a blockage of the small intestine), narrowing of the stoma, and skin irritation around the stoma. One study also reported results for the occurrence of prolapse (the protrusion of the bowel through the anus), which was not different between the two operations. None of the included studies measured other complications related to the stoma, or death due to a stoma. Quality of life was measured in all studies, but this was not one of our key outcomes. There is still no evidence supporting the use of one surgical technique over the other as the overall quality of the evidence was low to very low. Future research may change these findings.

We ran an electronic search up to September 2015 for randomised controlled trials comparing supportive therapy with standard care or other psychological therapies for schizophrenia. We identified 24 studies with 2126 participants who received either supportive therapy or standard care or another type of therapy. Most of the studies were relatively old (the first was from 1980) and most were very small (fewer than 100 people). Only four were less than 10 years old. We found that supportive therapy was similar to standard care in terms of relapse, hospitalisation and general functioning. Other psychological therapies were better than supportive therapy in terms of hospitalisation, overall clinical improvement and satisfaction of treatment. Based on very low quality evidence, it seems that other psychological therapies may be more effective than supportive therapy. However, there were not enough data to make any definite conclusions. There were no clear differences between supportive therapy and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). There were too few studies that compared supportive therapy with other types of therapy to draw any conclusions. Overall, the evidence for supportive therapy is weak. More high quality trials are needed before we can say whether supportive therapy is effective for schizophrenia.

We found 11 studies with 949 people with cancer pain, with mostly moderate or severe pain to start with. These studies lasted at least one week and compared different NSAIDs either with each other, with other medicines, or with no medicine. However, few people completed the studies and information was often missing, so we cannot be sure of the results. We did not find any studies that compared NSAIDs together or compared one NSAID with another. The studies were often not clear about how pain was assessed, and used many different ways to measure how well the medicines worked. We thought the quality of the evidence was very low, so we could draw no conclusions. For all medicines combined, 1 in 10 people had their pain reduced from moderate or severe to no worse than mild pain after one or two weeks. Side effects were common, but most were mild. There were 2 serious side effects and 22 deaths, but these were not clearly related to the pain treatment. People dropped out of the studies mostly because they felt the medicines were not effective enough or had side effects.

The review authors identified two randomised controlled trials with a total of 287 participants. Both studies were published in 2006. One trial included 253 participants comparing the low-molecular-weight heparin tinzaparin with a placebo, and the other trial included 34 participants comparing another low-molecular-weight heparin, dalteparin, also with a placebo. We could not combine any data from these studies because they were so different in terms of design, treatments, and the ways that the outcomes were assessed. Key results The study with 253 participants found that pain severity was lower in the tinzaparin group than in the placebo group at day two, day three, and day four. The time until the next crisis occurred was longer in the tinzaparin group than in the placebo group, i.e. there was an improvement in the control of pain. There were two reports of bleeding in the tinzaparin group and none in the placebo group. The study with 34 participants found that pain was reduced after one day in the dalteparin group more than in the placebo group. Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was very low. There were problems with both studies in relation to their designs. Also, we should note that the results of the larger study were based on a small number of participants and so we cannot be certain about them.

The authors searched the medical literature and found 36 studies which looked at the effect of anticoagulants when taken as a treatment for people who had had a carotid artery dissection. They found 11 studies that investigated the effects of antiplatelet drugs. However, none of the studies were randomised controlled trials. The authors also found one trial that randomly allocated participants to receive either an anticoagulant or an antiplatelet drug. The results from the studies that used participants who were not randomly allocated to treatments showed that there was no clear difference in the risk of death, ischaemic stroke, death or disability, symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage, or major extracranial haemorrhage between the anticoagulant group and the antiplatelet group. The results of the one trial that did use random allocation found no difference between the two groups in the risk of ischaemic stroke, death, or any serious adverse events. There were no significant differences in the risk of symptomatic bleeding between the two groups. The authors concluded that there was insufficient evidence to say whether anticoagulants are better than antiplatelet drugs, or vice versa, in the treatment of carotid artery dissections.

This review of 26 randomised controlled trials (2066 participants) set out to determine if there was evidence that dressings containing silver promote wound healing or prevent wound infection. The studies were too different to be able to pool their findings. Those treating burns wounds provided inconsistent evidence on infection, and no evidence on healing. There was no consistent evidence of benefit in other types of wound either. One study in diabetic foot ulcers did find that a silver-containing hydrofibre dressing improved healing time.

We identified 12 randomised controlled trials with 3571 participants comparing two types of antibiotic treatments for pneumonia occurring in people who are on breathing machines in intensive care units: single antibiotic versus multiple antibiotics. The particular antibiotics examined by each study varied. Four studies compared single antibiotic therapy to multiple antibiotic therapy. Eight studies compared different types of multiple antibiotic therapies to each other. One of the studies was funded by a pharmaceutical company, four had authors with ties to pharmaceutical companies and three had authors with no declared interests. Studies did not routinely test the bacteria from patients' cultures to determine which antibiotics they were resistant to, although one study did and found that none of the isolates were resistant to any of the antibiotics used in the study. Multiple antibiotic therapy did not result in lower death rates, shorter hospital stays or fewer side effects when compared to single antibiotics. Because studies did not identify patients at increased risk for bacteria that are resistant to multiple antibiotics, these results may not be generalizable to all patient groups. However, this is the largest review comparing single antibiotic therapy to multiple antibiotics for pneumonia occurring in people on breathing machines and contributes further evidence to the safety of using effective single antibiotics for the empiric treatment of pneumonia occurring in people on breathing machines in intensive care units. Due to a lack of studies, we could not evaluate the best antibiotic choice for pneumonia occurring in people on breathing machines, but carbapenems may result in better clinical cure than other tested antibiotics.

The review found 29 studies from around the world examining the impact of drug policies on drug utilization and health service use. Most of the studies focused on elderly people or low-income populations, who rely on publicly funded medication. The findings suggest that: - Restrictions on certain types of drugs can lead to reduced drug use and lower drug costs without affecting other health services (6 studies). - Removing restrictions on certain drugs can increase their appropriate use and reduce overall drug costs (4 studies). - There is little evidence on the impact of policies on health outcomes (2 studies). - There is no evidence on the impact of policies on health equity, despite the fact that some groups, such as the elderly and low-income populations, may have less ability to pay for medicines out-of-pocket.

This review is up-to-date to November 2015. We found 19 studies that were suitable to include in the review and which involved 4232 participants. The studies evaluated a variety of different types of one-to-one oral health advice, given at different times and places. We did not combine the results of any of the studies because of differences in the way that they were conducted. Instead we grouped the studies according to four comparisons: - Any form of one-to-one oral health advice versus no oral health advice. - Personalised one-to-one oral health advice versus routine one-to-one oral health advice. - Self-help oral health advice versus professional oral health advice. - Enhanced one-to-one oral health advice versus one-to-one oral health advice. For each comparison we described the results for Gingivitis (inflammation of the gums), Plaque (a sticky substance that forms on teeth) and Dental caries (tooth decay). There was some evidence that any form of one-to-one oral health advice gave better results for reducing plaque and dental caries than no oral health advice. However, there was no evidence that any of the other comparisons made a difference to the level of gingivitis, plaque or dental caries. Overall, there was not enough high-quality evidence to recommend any specific type of one-to-one oral health advice as being better than another.

Five small randomised controlled trials were included. The trials were all undertaken in Thailand. Patients were aged over 25 years with adhesive capsulitis. Oral steroids were used at various dosages and for varying lengths of time. One study also explored the use of intraarticular corticosteroid injection. None of the other studies investigated this option. One trial reported that oral steroids were better than placebo in improving pain, range of movement and function in the short term. Two further trials did not show a significant benefit of oral steroids over placebo or no treatment. However, these two trials did suggest that there was an early improvement in pain with oral steroids. One trial reported that taking oral steroids resulted in significantly more people reporting success (48% absolute improvement) at two weeks, but this benefit was not maintained at six weeks. Range of movement also showed a significant improvement with oral steroids at two weeks but not at six weeks. A second trial reported a similar early improvement in pain with oral steroids compared to no treatment but this was not significant. The third trial reported that oral steroids provided a more rapid initial improvement in pain compared to no treatment but there was little difference by five months. Overall, the evidence suggests that oral steroids are more effective than placebo or no treatment in improving pain, range of movement and function in the short term (up to six weeks) in patients with adhesive capsulitis. But the evidence is not strong. This review does not evaluate the role of intraarticular corticosteroid injection or manipulation under anaesthesia.

We identified three randomised controlled trials with a total of 50 participants. All three trials compared rTMS with sham TMS. The trials were too different from each other in terms of the type of TMS, the frequency of the sessions and the duration of the study to pool the results. None of the trials provided detailed data on the ALS Functional Rating Scale-revised (ALSFRS-R) scores at six months follow-up. One trial contained data in a suitable form for quantitative analysis of our secondary outcomes. No difference was seen between rTMS and sham rTMS using ALSFRS-R scores and manual muscle testing (MMT) scores at 12 months follow-up in this trial. Furthermore, none of the trials reported any adverse events associated with the use of rTMS. However, in view of the small number of participants and the methodological limitations of the trials, rTMS cannot be judged as completely safe. Given the small number of people with ALS who participated in these three studies, we cannot draw any conclusions about the benefits or harms of TMS in ALS.

This review includes 10 studies involving a total of 1049 participants. Each study compared a different treatment regime with another, so no single treatment was compared throughout the review. All studies used a cross-over design, which means that participants received two or more treatments, being randomised to receive them in a different order. This type of study is less reliable than a between-patient study design, because it is difficult for people to 'unlearn' the effects of a previous intervention when comparing two treatments given sequentially. However, some information cannot be obtained by comparing one treatment with another within the same person. We found that very potent topical steroids (such as clobetasol propionate 0.05%) are highly effective in controlling disease control and reducing death rates, but may cause side-effects on large areas of skin. Mildly potent topical steroids (such as triamcinolone acetonide 0.1%) are also effective and safer, and are recommended for moderate disease. Oral prednisolone is effective and safer than higher doses. There appears to be no added value for combining oral prednisolone with azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil, or with plasma exchange, or using a combination of tetracycline and nicotinamide. Very potent topical steroids such as clobetasol propionate are effective and safe for treating bullous pemphigus, but cause side-effects on large areas of skin. Mildly potent topical steroids such as triamcinolone acetonide are also effective and safer, and are recommended for moderate disease. Oral prednisolone is effective and safer than higher doses.

We reviewed 75 randomized trials with 7957 participants who had irritable bowel syndrome. We found that some herbal formulations may improve the symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome, but the evidence is far from conclusive. Methodologies of most of the trials were poor, and confirmatory studies are lacking. Therefore, the results should be interpreted with caution.

In this review, we examined the effectiveness, safety, and user acceptance of pills taken at the time of sex for birth control in women who have sex only a few times a month. We did computer searches to find randomized trials of the use of pills taken at the time of sex for birth control. We wrote to researchers to find other trials. The studies could compare the pill to another birth control method or to a placebo (or 'dummy'). The women in the studies took the pills at the time of sex, usually right before. The studies lasted for at least three months. We found 22 trials with a total of 12,400 women. The studies used levonorgestrel (LNG) in 11 studies, other hormones in 7 studies, and different doses of LNG in 4 studies. The women liked the pills okay even though many had abnormal bleeding. The pills worked well for birth control. The studies did not show if the pills were better with more frequent sex or higher doses. The evidence was rated as moderate because the reports were old, had method problems, and were not alike. Also, the large number of women, the low pregnancy rate, and the consistent results across studies helped to rate the evidence as moderate.

We searched the literature up to May 2015 and found 15 studies that were suitable for inclusion in the review. These studies had enrolled a total of 561 patients, all of whom received orthodontic treatment. The age range of patients was commonly restricted to teenagers or young adults although the participants of two studies were older (12 to 54 years). Both male and female patients participated in the studies. The main focus of the studies was to compare the use of surgery to reinforce anchorage with other ways of reinforcing anchorage. Reinforcement of anchorage is required when there is a need to maintain the position of one tooth while the rest of the teeth are moved. This is often necessary when treating the upper jaw with braces. The studies evaluated the effectiveness of surgical versus non-surgical methods of anchorage control, specifically focusing on whether the use of surgery resulted in greater movement of the upper molar teeth towards the back of the mouth (mesial distal movement). There was strong evidence that surgical anchorage was more effective in the reinforcement of anchorage by 1.68 mm. However, this result should be interpreted with some caution due to differences in the way that the studies were run. There was no evidence of a difference in overall duration of treatment between surgical and conventional anchorage. Information on how the patients felt about their treatment such as pain and acceptability was limited and the studies did not report on side effects of the treatments. There was no evidence to suggest that any type of surgical anchorage was better than another. The evidence presented here is of moderate quality due to some methodological issues in some of the studies.

Fifty studies including 45,285 participants were included. Thirty-eight studies compared statins with placebo or no treatment and included 37,274 participants. Three studies compared two different statin regimens and included 5547 participants. Most study participants had stage 3 to 5 CKD. Follow-up periods ranged from one to five years. All-cause mortality was 15%, cardiovascular death 11%, heart attack 6% and stroke 5%. The benefits of statins were similar in people with and those without increased protein in their urine. Statins did not appear to protect against stroke. Potential harms from statin therapy were not well reported and were uncertain in our analyses that had few events: elevated creatine kinase (an enzyme involved in muscle metabolism), liver function abnormalities, withdrawal due to adverse events, and cancer. The evidence shows that statins are beneficial in people with CKD, but there is some uncertainty about whether they should be used to treat people with milder forms of CKD.

This review aimed to evaluate the effectiveness of any medication used to relieve pain caused by limb length discrepancy. We searched several databases for clinical trials evaluating the use of pain medications in children with limb length discrepancies. We identified nine trials enrolling 379 children with either cerebral palsy or osteogenesis imperfecta with a range of ages from 2 to 19 years. Most of the trials were very small with only one trial having more than 100 participants. This review did not find any high quality trials that looked at commonly used pain medications, so we are unable to draw any conclusions about the benefits and harms of these medications in this population.

We searched many databases for clinical trials comparing latrepirdine to placebo (a dummy treatment). We found seven trials, of which six could be included in our analyses; one trial could not be included because it did not report any data. The trials included a total of 1697 people with Alzheimer’s disease. On average, the participants were around 70 years of age and had been experiencing memory problems for about two years. They were randomised to receive either latrepirdine or placebo for 6 months or 1 year. We found some evidence that latrepirdine improved behaviour, as measured by the Neuropsychiatric Inventory (NPI), although this evidence was not consistent across all studies. There was no evidence that latrepirdine had any effect on cognition (thinking) or function (daily activities) in people with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer’s disease. There was some evidence that latrepirdine and placebo were comparable in terms of side effects, serious side effects, and the number of people who dropped out of the studies. Overall, we thought the evidence on cognition and function was of low quality, but we thought there was some high quality evidence that latrepirdine may have a small beneficial effect on behaviour.

This review includes seven randomised controlled trials with a total of 735 participants. Three trials involved people with narrowing of the internal carotid artery (the main artery supplying blood to the brain) who did not have symptoms of stroke or TIA. Four trials involved people who had an acute ischaemic stroke with cerebral small vessel disease. We found that regular exercise may reduce the risk of recurrent stroke in people with narrowing of the internal carotid artery and reduce the size of any future strokes. However, we also found that regular exercise may increase the chance of being dead or disabled after an acute stroke if you are having clot-busting drugs. The quality of the evidence was low for all outcomes meaning that we cannot draw firm conclusions from this review. More research is needed.

We found six randomised controlled trials, enrolling 204 preterm babies. We found that protein supplementation of mother's own milk (HMB) with extra protein increases the rate of gain in weight, length, and head circumference of preterm babies during their initial hospitalisation. It did not increase the risk of feeding intolerance, necrotising enterocolitis, or change the level of serum albumin. However, it was associated with longer hospital stays. Currently, protein supplementation of HMB is usually done as a component of multi-nutrient supplements, so future studies should compare different levels of protein in these supplements, and be designed to examine the effects on duration of hospital stay and safety as well as on longer-term growth, body fatness, heart problems, and brain development. The overall quality of the current evidence for protein supplementation of HMB in preterm babies was low to very low. All of the studies had some limitations in terms of how they were designed, and there were not enough studies to allow us to be sure of the results.

We found 45 studies that evaluated the impact of PEMs on professional practice. Almost all (44/45) compared PEM to no intervention. One study compared a paper-based PEM to the same document disseminated on CD-ROM. We found that PEMs may have a small beneficial effect on professional practice outcomes when measured immediately after receipt of the PEM. We could not find evidence that PEMs lead to changes in how healthcare professionals treat patients. We do not know whether PEMs are more effective than other types of knowledge transfer activities, or whether they are more effective when combined with other activities. Future research should test the long-term impact of PEMs and their relative effectiveness compared to other knowledge transfer activities.

This review found 23 randomised controlled trials which evaluated the effects of behavioural interventions aimed at reducing the spread of sexually transmitted infections (STIs) among young people aged under 25 years. Most studies were conducted in the USA and involved young women. Many of the programmes provided information about STIs and taught safer sex skills (e.g. communication) and around half included the provision of resources such as free sexual health services. Few included information about human papillomavirus (HPV) or cervical cancer. The evidence shows that some programmes can increase condom use but there was no effect on abstinence from sexual activity. There were also few effects on actual STI outcomes. It is uncertain whether the interventions had any longer term effects because most studies only followed participants for a short time. Future research should better reflect the global nature of the problem by including studies from outside the USA. Research should also pay more attention to HPV and how it causes cervical cancer.

The Information Specialist of Cochrane Schizophrenia searched their specialised register for relevant trials up to February 2015. We identified twenty randomised controlled trials (studies where people were allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) that met our inclusion criteria. These included a total of 2337 participants. The participants received either brief psychoeducation alone, usual care alone or another type of treatment. We divided the interventions into three categories: i) brief psychoeducation versus usual care; ii) brief psychoeducation versus cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT); and iii) brief psychoeducation added to usual care versus usual care. We found that participants who received brief psychoeducation were less likely to stop taking their medication in the short term than those receiving usual care, and were less likely to relapse back into schizophrenia in the medium term. However, there was no difference in effectiveness between the two groups in the long term. A few studies suggested that brief psychoeducation may lead to an improvement in overall functioning and a reduction in symptoms in the short and medium term. There was no clear effect on quality of life, nor on death rates. Our findings suggest that brief psychoeducation is more effective than usual care in improving medication compliance and reducing the risk of relapse. However, the quality of the evidence for these outcomes was low or very low, based on a small number of studies. More research is required to confirm whether brief psychoeducation is effective.

We found 11 studies including 9839 people with COPD. Most were men with moderately severe symptoms. Not many included people had recent flare ups of their COPD. Four studies did not state how long it took to collect the data on outcomes after people started taking the medication. The evidence shows that people who received LAMA+LABA had 18% fewer exacerbations (flare ups) compared to those who received LABA+ICS, and this finding was consistent across most of the studies. They also had 8% better lung function, and were 43% less likely to experience pneumonia. In terms of quality of life, people receiving LAMA+LABA were 25% more likely to improve by 4 or more points on a quality of life questionnaire, compared to those taking LABA+ICS. We could not draw any conclusions about the effects of LAMA+LABA on mortality (death rate), as there were very few deaths reported. Overall, the evidence supporting these findings was either low or moderate, meaning we cannot be completely confident in our results. This is because most of the studies were sponsored by pharmaceutical companies with a vested interest in the results of the studies. Also, the studies included people with different levels of symptom severity, and the studies were of different lengths. This means that the results may not apply to everyone with COPD.

We included three studies with a total of 91 children aged between six months and four years. Two studies took place in the USA and one in Spain. The length of the studies was seven to 16 months; all three studied croup in children who presented to the emergency department. Different concentrations of helium and oxygen were used. One study compared heliox with 30% humidified oxygen and two studies compared heliox with 100% oxygen. In one study of children with mild croup, we found no clear difference in improvement in croup symptoms between those who received heliox and those who received 30% humidified oxygen. However, this study was small and at the end of the treatment period (20 minutes), which may not be long enough to see an effect. In one study of children with moderate croup, those who received heliox improved slightly more than those who received no treatment after 60 minutes, but not at 120 minutes. In the other study of children with moderate to severe croup, those who received heliox had similar improvement in croup symptoms to those who received 100% oxygen and one to two doses of adrenaline (epinephrine). Overall, the evidence for all outcomes was of low quality. This was due to the small number of participants in the studies as well as concerns about aspects of the study designs. As the studies compared different treatments, we cannot combine their results. It is unclear whether giving heliox is better than standard treatment for children with croup. More research is needed to gather more evidence.

This review examined the ability of'red flags' to identify patients with vertebral fractures in those with low back pain. Red flags are signs or symptoms that indicate the possibility of a serious cause of low back pain such as a fractured vertebra, a tumor, an infection, or an abnormal growth. This review found many different types of red flags described in the literature but few had been tested for their ability to identify vertebral fractures. Those that had been tested had poor accuracy. However, some combinations of red flags may help identify vertebral fractures.

This review aimed to compare the effectiveness of different treatments for urethral stricture disease but only two small studies were found. There were insufficient data to determine which treatment is best for urethral stricture disease in terms of balancing effectiveness, side effects and costs. Well designed, adequately powered multi-centre trials are needed to answer relevant clinical questions regarding treatment of men with urethral strictures.

We found six studies including 1211 adults and children with chronic asthma. We found that both methods were equally effective in avoiding exacerbations and serious health problems. However, people taking daily ICS had slightly better lung function, symptoms and inhaler use. In contrast, children on intermittent ICS seemed to grow taller. We believe that more research is needed to properly answer the question whether intermittent ICS is as good as daily ICS in terms of long-term outcomes.

Seventeen studies involving 1639 adults with CKD were included. Most participants had stage 1 to 3 CKD but three studies included people on dialysis and four included people who had received a kidney transplant. Nine studies involved dietary counselling and eight involved dietary programmes using recommended dietary patterns such as a healthy or Mediterranean diet. The duration of diet intervention ranged from three months to two years. We could not be sure whether dietary interventions improved survival or prevented heart disease because few studies recorded these outcomes and the quality of evidence was very low. In very low quality evidence, dietary interventions may prevent one person in every 3000 treated for one year avoiding end-stage kidney disease. Quality of life was higher with dietary interventions compared to controls and adverse events were not regularly reported. We found some evidence that dietary interventions lower blood pressure and improve kidney function tests. A Mediterranean diet lower serum cholesterol. The quality of evidence was low to very low for all outcomes meaning that additional studies are needed to be sure of the results.

The review authors searched the medical literature and found only one small study (of 156 infants aged between seven weeks and 24 months) that compared the use of a mist in a tent to help relieve the symptoms of bronchiolitis. This study also compared giving the participants nebulised salbutamol to those who had nebulised saline. The results showed a significant decrease in the respiratory distress score for the nebulised salbutamol group but no significant decrease in the respiratory distress score for either the mist in a tent group or the nebulised saline group. There were no reports of any harm due to the treatments. As there is only one study, which included a very small number of participants, we cannot draw any firm conclusions about the effectiveness of mist therapy for bronchiolitis in young children.

The evidence is current to July 2015. We found four studies with 1154 participants who had a diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease. Two of these studies were large randomised controlled trials and involved people who were already taking a cholinesterase inhibitor (a type of dementia medication). The other two studies involved people who were not taking any medications for their dementia. We did not find any studies involving people with vascular dementia. When we combined results, we did not find any evidence that statins improved cognition (thinking ability), global function or activities of daily living. There was also no difference in side effects between the statin and control group. There is currently no evidence for use of statins in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease or vascular dementia. More research is needed to determine if statins are beneficial in these conditions.

This review included four trials with a total of 149 participants. Two studies looked at the effects of a night splint for people with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A, one examined the effects of prednisone for people with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, and one evaluated the effects of surgery for people with Duchenne muscular dystrophy. The evidence is current to August 2014. Night splints were no more effective than no night splints in improving ankle range of motion for people with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A. Prednisone was more effective in improving muscle strength and walking speed than placebo; however, there was no improvement in ankle range of motion. Surgery did not improve functional ability (measured by stair climbing speed) and was associated with an increased risk of Achilles tendon tightness and foot pain. There is no evidence of significant benefit from any intervention for increasing ankle range of motion in these conditions. More research is needed.

The review includes 25 studies involving 5218 women who chose their own positions during labour. The majority of women walked around and used birthing pools, while others preferred to remain in bed. The length of time taken to give birth to the baby was significantly shorter for women who walked and assumed an upright position compared to those who remained in bed. The difference in time taken for the birth was almost two hours, from an average of 10 hours and 48 minutes in a recumbent position to eight hours and 26 minutes when upright. Fewer women had a caesarean section, fewer needed pain relief with epidurals, and their babies were less likely to be admitted to the neonatal nursery, when they assumed an upright position. However, more studies are needed to confirm these findings.

We searched for both published and unpublished studies comparing treatment versus no treatment, or comparing different treatments for TOS. The evidence is current to August 2014. We found one study that compared natural progress with an active intervention, but the rest of the studies we found only compared different treatments. Of these, only two studies met our criteria for being included in the review. These two studies randomly allocated a total of 92 people to treatment groups. One study looked at the effects of injecting botulinum toxin into the muscles of the neck to paralyze them, while the other studied the effects of removing the first rib through the armpit. One study was of high quality, but the other had some serious flaws in its design. The studies were for shoulder pain and disability, sense of fatigue, sensation, and muscle strength in the affected arm. The studies used quite different outcomes and scales, and so could not be combined. The study of botulinum toxin injections reported no improvement in pain or disability, but did report improved sensation after six months. The study of resection of the first rib reported greater pain relief with this procedure than with neurolysis. There were no complications in either group. We conclude that there is no evidence from randomized controlled trials for the use of any of the treatments commonly used for TOS. There is a need for agreed outcome measures, and for randomized controlled trials that compare the outcome of interventions with no treatment and with each other.

This review includes 21 randomised controlled trials with a total of 884 participants who had been diagnosed with carpal tunnel syndrome for at least two weeks but were not more than 12 weeks past the onset of symptoms. Participants were randomly allocated to receive one of the following interventions: a hand brace, electrotherapy, oral medication, injection, manipulation, yoga, ergonomic changes to their work station or education/advice only. The main comparisons were between hand brace and no brace, oral steroids and placebo, ultrasound and sham ultrasound, and between different types of oral medication. Oral steroids were found to be effective in reducing night time waking up because of tingling and numbness, as well as improving overall symptoms and function. Ultrasound, yoga, and carpal bone mobilisation were also found to be effective in improving overall symptoms and function. A hand brace was found to be effective in improving function but not symptoms. There were no adverse effects reported in the trials. Further research is needed to determine which of these treatments is the most effective in the long term.

Two randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that involved a total of 708 participants with CRVO-ME were identified. The first, SCORE, compared the efficacy of triamcinolone acetonide injected into the vitreous body of the eye with no intervention. The second, GENEVA, compared the efficacy of a dexamethasone implant implanted through the conjunctiva covering the white part of the eye with a sham procedure. Not all planned outcomes were reported in either study, and there were concerns about the completeness of outcome data in the SCORE study. The GENEVA study included participants with both branch retinal vein occlusion and central retinal vein occlusion, but did not report results for the CRVO-ME population separately. Overall, we found insufficient evidence to determine whether IVS is of benefit for individuals with CRVO-ME. In the SCORE study, the number of participants who dropped out was high, especially in the observation group. Furthermore, the fact that the final visual acuity in the observation group was worse than at baseline may have led to differential loss to follow-up. The GENEVA study found that there was little or no change in visual acuity at 6 months. Adverse events occurred more frequently in participants receiving IVS than in those in the observation group. Elevated intraocular pressure, progression of cataract, and retinal neovascularisation were the most common complications. We judged the quality of the evidence to be low. This was because of study limitations, imprecision of treatment estimates, and selective outcome reporting.

The authors found five studies that demonstrated a beneficial effect of nasal decongestants and steroids on nasal breathing in children with enlarged tonsils and adenoids. However, the studies were small and of short duration, and side effects were not well discussed. Further studies are needed to determine the long-term benefits of this treatment and its safety.

The search identified one small study involving 24 women with severe pre-eclampsia. The women were randomly allocated to receive either an epidural or a placebo. The epidural was given with a 10 mg initial dose and 5 mg per hour thereafter. The women received the same medications for their blood pressure and fits as the women in the placebo group. The study was funded by a foundation. The quality of the evidence in the included study was low. There was no information on the important outcomes of this review. The included study provided no clear difference in the Apgar scores of the babies in the two groups. There was a reduction in diastolic arterial pressure in the women who received the epidural. However, there was no significant difference in the mean arterial pressure and systolic arterial pressure between the two groups. More high-quality evidence from randomised controlled trials evaluating the effectiveness, safety or cost of using epidural therapy for treating severe pre-eclampsia in non-labouring women is needed.

We found 16 eligible randomised clinical trials, which recruited 927 participants who received glucocorticosteroids and 934 who received placebo or no intervention. The participants were between 25 and 70 years old, had different stages of alcoholic liver disease, and 65% were men. The evidence is current to January 2018. We are uncertain whether giving glucocorticosteroids has an important effect on increasing or decreasing the risk of dying from any cause, serious side effects, liver-related dying, liver decompensation (i.e. deterioration of liver function), and number of participants with complications. We are also uncertain whether glucocorticosteroids have an important effect on health-related quality of life. The certainty of evidence was very low for all outcomes, mainly because the studies were small and at high risk of bias, and the results varied across the studies. Therefore, we need new, large, well-conducted and well-reported randomised clinical trials to know more precisely how glucocorticosteroids affect patients with alcoholic liver disease.

This review of four studies with a total of 245 participants examined the efficacy of driver rehabilitation in people with stroke. The four studies used different methods for their interventions and looked at different aspects of driving ability. One study used a driving simulator to assess the effect of a simulated driving task on driving ability. Two studies assessed the effect of remedial exercises on specific skills required for driving, such as recognizing traffic signs. The fourth study looked at the benefit of practising driving in a car with a licensed driver by his or her side. None of the studies looked at the impact of driver rehabilitation on actual driving behaviour, for example, number of cars on the road, number of accidents. There were some positive effects of the various interventions, but more research is needed before any firm conclusions can be drawn.

We looked for studies that compared a course of oral or injected steroids lasting less than eight days with a course lasting eight days or longer in people with COPD who were having an exacerbation. We included only studies where it was decided randomly who would receive each length of treatment. We found eight relevant studies with a total of 582 participants. Of these, five studies involved injections and three involved tablets. The older studies used different drugs, and the newer studies used prednisolone tablets, which is more commonly used nowadays. The studies were generally well designed, but one study had concerns about how the people taking part were allocated to the treatments. The studies all included people with severe or very severe COPD. The main outcome was whether the treatment failed, i.e. whether the person's symptoms worsened again before the end of the study. We found that there was no clear difference in effectiveness between shorter and longer duration of systemic corticosteroids, in terms of risk of treatment failure, relapse (a new event) or time to the next exacerbation. There was also no difference in the likelihood of an adverse event between short-duration and longer-duration treatment. Neither length of hospital stay nor lung function at the end of treatment differed between short-duration and longer-duration treatment. We are confident that five days of oral corticosteroids is likely to be sufficient for treatment of adults with COPD during an attack, and we think it is unlikely that shorter courses of systemic corticosteroids (of around five days) lead to worse outcomes than are seen with longer (10 to 14 days) courses. We graded most available evidence as moderate in quality because of imprecision; further research may have an important impact on our confidence in the estimates of effect or may change the estimates. The studies in this review did not include people with mild or moderate COPD; further studies comparing short-duration systemic corticosteroid versus conventional longer-duration systemic corticosteroid for treatment of adults with acute exacerbations of COPD are required.

The Cochrane Review authors searched scientific databases for randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) in order to find studies that looked at the effects of giving a liberal or restrictive blood transfusion policy to people with MDS or Aplastic Anemia. We found one completed study (13 participants) and three ongoing studies. The completed study compared a liberal with a restrictive policy, but did not show a difference between the policies in terms of the risk of death or the number of blood transfusions given. It did not report whether there were any differences in how often participants had to be admitted to hospital, or in their quality of life. There was insufficient evidence to determine whether either policy was better than the other for improving symptoms or reducing toxicity from transfusion. We rated the quality of the evidence using four levels: very low, low, moderate, or high. Very low-quality evidence means that we are very uncertain about the results. High-quality evidence means that we are very confident in the results. The evidence from the one included study was of very low quality.

We searched for all known research evaluating the use of games for the education of health professionals. We found only two studies that met our inclusion criteria. One study showed that using a game as a reinforcement technique improved knowledge of infection control. The other study comparing a game with traditional case-based learning found no difference in knowledge, but the game group reported more enjoyment. We are unable to make any conclusions about the effectiveness of using games as a method of educating health professionals from the evidence available in this review.

In this review we found eight studies which met our inclusion criteria. We concluded that lithium and antidepressants were equally effective in preventing relapse from depression but the effects of one drug versus the other could not be determined. It is a limitation of the review that two of the studies also included participants with bipolar disorder, who may have different response characteristics to those with unipolar disorder. We recommend large-scale, long-term randomised trials in unselected groups of subjects with unipolar affective disorder are needed.

We identified two randomised controlled trials with a total of 130 patients. The patients received either amifostine or a placebo (dummy infusion) immediately before and during administration of radioactive iodine. Amifostine did not significantly reduce side effects of radioactive iodine treatment on the salivary glands. No effects on overall survival, morbidity, quality of life and costs were evaluated in these two small trials. Larger trials would be needed to draw final conclusions on these issues.

This review examined the effectiveness of parent-mediated interventions for improving the language and communication of children with Down syndrome. We searched widely for studies published up to July 2016 and found three studies involving a total of 45 children with Down syndrome, between 29 months and six years old. These studies investigated the use of parent-child interaction therapy, parent-child interaction therapy with an added clinician component, and the Hanen Parent-Child Interaction Therapy. They all took place in North America and were conducted with the children being between two to six years of age. The length of the treatments varied from 12 weeks to six months. Two studies compared parent-mediated interventions versus usual care (TAU), and the third compared a parent-mediated plus clinician-mediated intervention versus a clinician-mediated intervention alone. In two studies, there were eight children in the intervention group and nine in the control group; in the third study, there were 10 children in the intervention group and 11 in the control group. This review found that there is not enough evidence to draw conclusions about the effectiveness of parent-mediated interventions for improving the language and communication of children with Down syndrome. Although the studies reported some positive effects of the interventions, these were inconsistent and were not maintained over time. The studies differed in terms of the type of intervention, treatment duration, age range of the children, and the ways in which the outcomes were measured. Therefore, it was not possible to pool the results together. There were also a number of potential biases in the way that the studies were designed and reported. Overall, we thought that the quality of the evidence was very low, so we cannot be confident in the results, and further research is likely to have an important impact on our confidence in the results.

The evidence is current to September 2014. We found only two studies that met our inclusion criteria. The first study (67 participants) compared a cardiac rehabilitation programme consisting of exercise training and education to a no-exercise control group. The second study (81 participants) compared an exercise training programme with usual care. We found that, compared with no exercise, exercise-based rehabilitation may increase exercise capacity. There was insufficient evidence to judge the effect of exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation on mortality and serious complications. These studies did not report quality of life, costs and the need for reoperation. There was insufficient evidence at 12 months follow-up to judge the effect of exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation on the ability to return to work. The quality of the evidence was moderate for exercise capacity and very low for all remaining outcomes. The main limitations were imprecision and risk of bias.

The review authors searched the literature and identified five relevant studies, with a total of 1130 participants. Two studies evaluated meditation, the others evaluated multi-disciplinary palliative care interventions that involved a chaplain or spiritual counsellor as a member of the intervention team. The studies evaluating meditation found no overall significant difference between those receiving meditation or usual care on quality of life or well-being. However, when meditation was combined with massage in the medium term it buffered against a reduction in quality of life. In the palliative care intervention studies there was no significant difference in quality of life or well-being between the trial arms. Coping with the disease was not evaluated in the studies. The quality of the studies was limited by incomplete reporting of design features. The authors concluded that they found inconclusive evidence that interventions with spiritual or religious components for adults in the terminal phase of a disease may or may not enhance well-being. They noted that all five studies had been carried out in the USA, and that all five had methodological problems. They suggested that there is a need for more rigorous evaluation of such interventions.

We found six studies with a total of 137 participants. The mean age of participants in four studies was over 70 years and the duration of the disease ranged from two to 23 years. Three studies examined the effects of tDCS applied to the brain regions involved in movement control. Three studies examined the combination of tDCS and movement therapy versus sham tDCS and movement therapy. The duration of tDCS ranged from five days to three weeks. We found very low quality evidence that tDCS does not improve impairment, reduces off time or the time spent with dyskinesias, or improves health-related quality of life and disability. Very low quality evidence also showed no difference in side effects or dropouts between those receiving tDCS and those receiving sham tDCS. Finally, we found very low quality evidence that there is no difference in the improvement of gait speed between those receiving tDCS plus movement therapy and those receiving sham tDCS plus movement therapy. The evidence is current to January 2015.

We searched for clinical trials where people were randomly allocated to receive either sympathetic blockade or another treatment. We looked at pain intensity as an outcome measure. We wanted to investigate the short-term (i.e. 48 hours to six weeks) and long-term (i.e. six weeks to one year) effect of sympathetic blockade on pain. The evidence is current to May 2015. We included 12 studies in this updated review (the previous version had 11 studies). We added four new studies to the review. All 12 studies were small, all were at some risk of bias, and all were conducted in adults. Only two studies compared sympathetic blockade to a placebo (a 'dummy' treatment), and neither showed any reduction in pain after short-term follow-up. One study showed a benefit at one-year follow-up, but this was a low quality study. When sympathetic blockade was compared to other active treatments, most studies found no clear difference in pain reduction, and none found a benefit at short-term follow-up. Three studies compared different types of sympathetic block and found no clear difference in pain reduction. Six studies reported complications, all with minor effects. Overall, the quality of evidence was low to very low, meaning that we cannot be sure of the results. There is still no strong evidence for or against sympathetic blockade in CRPS.

We included 14 studies with 2488 participants in this review. This is an update of a previous Cochrane Review published in 2015. We included four new studies in this update. All of the included studies assessed whether adding antiviral medication to corticosteroids had a beneficial effect on improving facial paralysis and reducing long-term effects of Bell's palsy compared to corticosteroids alone or a placebo. Outcomes were measured by whether participants achieved complete recovery, and by whether they developed long-term effects of Bell's palsy (motor synkinesis or crocodile tears). We also collected information on adverse events. The evidence is current to May 2019. The combination of antivirals and corticosteroids may have little or no effect on rates of incomplete recovery in people with Bell's palsy compared to corticosteroids alone. However, this result was imprecise and allowed for the possibility of no effect. Corticosteroids alone were probably more effective than antivirals alone and antivirals plus corticosteroids were more effective than placebo or no treatment. There was no clear benefit from antivirals alone over placebo. The combination of antivirals and corticosteroids probably reduced the late effects of Bell's palsy compared with corticosteroids alone. Studies also showed fewer episodes of late effects in corticosteroid-treated participants than antiviral-treated participants. We did not find any clear difference in adverse events from the use of antivirals compared with either placebo or corticosteroids, but the evidence is too uncertain for us to draw conclusions. The overall certainty of the evidence ranged from very low to moderate. Most of the included studies were small and poorly reported, which means that their results may be less reliable.

We searched scientific databases for clinical trials comparing LHRH analogues with chemotherapy (or placebo) as an add-on treatment in women with relapsed epithelial ovarian cancer. The evidence is current to July 2016. We found two randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) with 97 women that matched our search. One trial looked at the effect of the LHRH agonist leuprorelin versus the chemotherapeutic agent treosulfan, and one trial examined a LHRH agonist (decapeptyl) versus a placebo. Since both studies had different control groups, we could not combine the results from these studies. Quality of life was not reported in either study. Overall survival and progression-free survival (the length of time that the tumour does not get worse) at six and 12 months may be similar with leuprorelin or treosulfan, but this is uncertain. Side effects such as alopecia (hair loss), nausea and vomiting, neurological effects and low levels of white blood cells were reported, but we couldn't draw any conclusions about which treatment was better in terms of side effects because of the small number of participants and limited data. In the study by Currie and colleagues, the progression-free survival was longer with decapeptyl (16 weeks) than with placebo (11.2 weeks), but other outcomes were not reported. It is worth noting that information on side effects were not reported for the placebo group in this study. We couldn't reach any conclusions on the role of LHRH agonists as an add-on treatment in women with relapsed epithelial ovarian cancer. The overall quality of evidence for all outcomes (overall survival, progression-free survival, quality of life and side effects) is very low.

The review authors searched the literature and identified 17 completed and ongoing randomised controlled trials that met inclusion criteria of the review. These included a total of 1262 infants. Most of these infants were born at near term (37 weeks' gestation). Inhaled nitric oxide improved oxygenation in about half of the infants studied. It reduced the need for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), a highly invasive and risky treatment, with a number needed to treat for an additional beneficial outcome (NNTB) of 5.3. Death was not affected. There was no apparent difference in effect with inhaled nitric oxide whether infants had echocardiographically confirmed persistent pulmonary hypertension or not. In infants with congenital diaphragmatic hernia, outcomes were not improved with inhaled nitric oxide. In infants who received inhaled nitric oxide at less severe criteria, there were no differences in clinical outcomes with inhaled nitric oxide compared to infants who were enrolled but received treatment only if their condition deteriorated. Fewer of the babies who received inhaled nitric oxide early satisfied later treatment criteria, showing that earlier inhaled nitric oxide reduced progression of the disease but did not further decrease mortality nor the need for ECMO. Inhaled nitric oxide is safe; there are no reports of important adverse effects. The major limitation of this review is that very few studies used a placebo.

This updated review found moderate evidence that lumbar supports are not more effective than no intervention or training in preventing low-back pain, and conflicting evidence whether they are effective supplements to other preventive interventions. It remains unclear whether lumbar supports are more effective than no or other interventions for treating low-back pain. Future research should address some important issues such as how long lumbar supports have to be worn and who benefits from them.

We searched the literature on 5 February 2016 and identified 57 relevant studies, which included 16,784 catheters and 11 types of impregnations. There were 338 participants in the most recently included study. The minimum follow-up period was one day and the maximum was 12 months. We could not assess the overall numbers of participants enrolled in the included studies. The majority of the studies enrolled adults aged 18 or older. We found that impregnated catheters significantly reduced the risk of CRBSI by 38% and catheter colonization by 33%. However, they did not seem to reduce the risk of sepsis or death. The benefits of using impregnated catheters seemed to be greater in studies that recruited participants from intensive care units. There were no significant differences in the rates of complications like thrombosis and bleeding between the impregnated and non-impregnated groups. We graded the overall quality of the evidence as high for CRBSI and moderate for catheter colonization and clinically diagnosed sepsis. The main limitations in the evidence were the variations in the types of impregnants used, the types of participants enrolled and the way each outcome was reported. We are confident that impregnated CVCs are better than non-impregnated CVCs in preventing CRBSI and catheter colonization. However, we are more cautious about their potential to prevent sepsis and mortality.

We searched the literature up to 5 May 2017. We found 15 studies that met our inclusion criteria. Fourteen of these were randomised controlled trials (RCT; a type of experiment in which people are randomly allocated to treatment groups) and one was a controlled before-and-after study. The settings of the included studies were mainly Bangladesh, India, and Peru. One study had a low risk of bias and the rest had high risk of bias. The interventions included zinc supplementation in pregnant women (three studies), micronutrient or macronutrient supplementation in children (eight studies), nutrition education for pregnant women (two studies), and nutrition systems strengthening interventions targeting children (two studies). Six interventions were adapted to the urban context and seven targeted household, community, or'service delivery' via systems strengthening. The interventions focused on improving the use of nutritious foods by pregnant women and young children to improve their growth and development, but none measured the impact of the interventions on health inequalities. Key results Zinc supplementation of pregnant women had no clear effect on low birth weight (three RCTs) and there was insufficient evidence on the effect on length (stature) of the baby at birth (low- to moderate-certainty evidence). Supplementation of children with micronutrients or macronutrients had no clear effect on height-for-age (HFA) (eight RCTs) and the effect on length was inconclusive (very low- to moderate-certainty evidence). Education of pregnant women had a positive effect on birth weight (two RCTs), with low-certainty evidence. Nutrition systems strengthening interventions had an inconclusive result on HFA (very low- to low-certainty evidence) and a positive effect on length at 18 months, with low-certainty evidence. Quality of the evidence The overall certainty of the evidence was very low to moderate. This was due to the small number of studies, the fact that many were conducted in different settings, and because many of the studies had some methodological weakness.

We identified 12 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that compared hydrocellular foam dressings with other dressings in people with venous leg ulcers. In general, the studies were not well conducted, and most did not report sources of funding. Hydrocellular foam dressings were similar to polyurethane foam dressings in terms of ulcer healing, but better than hydrocolloid dressings, paraffin gauze dressings, hydrocapillary dressings, and knitted viscose dressings. However, these findings were based on relatively small numbers of participants. Adverse events were reported by half of the RCTs, and there were no clear differences between hydrocellular foam dressings and the other dressings investigated in terms of the number of participants experiencing adverse events. Most of the RCTs (10 out of 12) were not blinded, which may have affected the results obtained. This review of 12 RCTs (1023 participants) suggests that hydrocellular foam dressings are no better than other types of dressings in the treatment of venous leg ulcers. More research is needed to increase our confidence in the results.

The researchers identified two randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that met the inclusion criteria of the review. One RCT (n = 159 patients) compared etoricoxib (60 to 120 mg per day) to a placebo (fake medication) in patients with inactive or mildly active Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis. The other (n = 222 patients) compared celecoxib (200 mg twice daily) to placebo in patients with inactive ulcerative colitis. The two RCTs were not combined because the patients were different and the treatments were for different lengths of time. There was no difference in the rate of flare-ups of IBD between etoricoxib and placebo. This was reported as 17% (14/82) in the etoricoxib group compared to 19% (15/77) in the placebo group. There was no difference in flare-ups of ulcerative colitis between celecoxib and placebo. This was reported as 4% (5/112) in the celecoxib group compared to 6% (7/110) in the placebo group. Side effects were similar in celecoxib and placebo groups. No one in either group died or experienced serious side effects. The most common side effect was stomach upset. The overall quality of evidence was low. There were not enough data to be certain that there is no difference in flare-ups of IBD between celecoxib or etoricoxib and placebo. It should also be noted that the two included studies were small and the patients were only followed for a short time.

In September 2017 we searched for randomised controlled trials looking at the effects of topical antimicrobial treatments used for treating infected diabetic foot ulcers. We found 22 relevant studies with a total of 2310 participants. One study did not report the number of participants. The majority of the studies had small numbers of participants (from 4 to 317) and short follow-up times (up to one year). A range of different topical antimicrobial treatments were assessed, including: silver-containing dressings; iodide dressings; super-oxidised dressings; zinc hyaluronate; silver sulphadiazine; tretinoin; pexiganan cream; and chloramine. We grouped the studies according to the type of treatment being compared. We found: 1) more evidence that wounds healed when treated with an antimicrobial dressing than with a non-antimicrobial dressing; 2) no clear evidence that there was a difference between the effects of using a topical treatment versus a systemic antibiotic; 3) no clear evidence that there was a difference between the effects of using one topical treatment compared with another; and 4) no clear evidence that there was a difference between the effects of using a topical treatment compared with growth factors. Overall, the quality of the evidence was low to very low for all comparisons. This was because of problems with the way that the studies were conducted and the large amount of uncertainty surrounding the results.

The review authors searched the medical literature for studies assessing the impact of zinc supplementation on pneumonia in children under 5 years of age. The search is current to May 2016. We identified six studies that met the inclusion criteria for the review. These studies were published between 2004 and 2014 and were conducted in Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Pakistan, Peru and Vietnam. They enrolled a total of 5193 children aged six months to 5 years from urban and rural settings. The results of these studies showed that zinc supplementation reduces the occurrence of pneumonia in children under five by 13%. The effect was larger when we restricted the outcome to pneumonia diagnosed using more specific criteria (41% reduction). The quality of the evidence was low. Key factors that might have lowered the quality of the evidence included inadequate reporting of study methods and the fact that half of the studies were sponsored by entities with potential conflicts of interest.

The authors searched for trials which investigated the effect of vitamin A supplements on acute lower respiratory tract infections (LRTI) in children under the age of 5 years. The review includes 10 studies with 33,179 participants. The results show that vitamin A does not prevent acute LRTIs in all children. Its impact is limited to those who are malnourished or have acute and chronic under nutrition. In healthy well-nourished children, vitamin A did not prevent acute LRTIs. Moreover, it was associated with an increased risk of pneumonia in two trials. The beneficial effects of vitamin A appear to be limited to populations with acute and chronic under nutrition. The results suggest that vitamin A should not be given to all children to prevent acute LRTIs.

The evidence from these twenty randomised controlled trials shows that theophylline improves lung function in people with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). It is most effective at doses between 600-750 mg per day, but higher doses are associated with more adverse effects. Theophylline should not be used as first line treatment for COPD, because other drugs are more convenient and more effective in terms of improving exercise ability. However, it may have a role as an add-on drug to other bronchodilators. The studies reviewed were of good methodological quality and the overall reporting was thorough.

We included 10 clinical trials with a total of 439 children aged 1 year to 18 years. Two hundred and forty-nine children received oral immunotherapy and 190 were in the control group. The control groups in three studies received a dummy treatment (placebo) and in seven studies avoided eating eggs for two years before the test. In the largest trial, both groups included children who were already eating egg. The duration of treatment was from three months to two years. Most children (82%) in the oral immunotherapy group could ingest a partial serving of egg (1 g to 7.5 g) compared to 10% of the control group. Half of the children receiving oral immunotherapy were able to eat a full serving of egg (12 g) compared to 10% of the control group. Almost all the children (95%) who received oral immunotherapy had mild-to-severe allergic reactions, and 8% had serious reactions that required adrenaline injection. The most common side effects were abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and lip and mouth irritation. The studies were not of high quality; they were small and used different methods for giving oral immunotherapy. Therefore, we cannot be confident in the results.

We searched scientific databases from their inception to 1 April 2015 and found four trials involving a total of 579 adults who had had three or more episodes of syncope within the previous year. Two of the four studies followed up participants for at least 12 months and two for at least six months. The evidence is current to January 2016. The trials compared ILR with a conventional diagnostic workup, which includes electrophysiology testing (EPS), tilt table testing and extensive blood tests. We did not find any difference in long-term mortality between the groups but the quality of the evidence was very low. There was no evidence of differences in quality of life, which was reported in different ways in two studies, making it impossible to pool the results. There was a trend towards fewer syncope relapses at one year in the ILR group but this was not significant. The costs of ILRs were higher than the costs of a conventional diagnostic approach. However, the costs per arrhythmia diagnosed were lower using the ILR and the costs per diagnosis were also lower with ILR. The ability of ILRs to increase the rate of diagnoses was shown in the four studies, with more diagnoses made in the ILR group than in the control group. Overall, we felt that the evidence was moderate as to whether the use of an ILR could increase the rate of diagnoses, but was very low for the other outcomes examined. More trials would be needed to enable firmer conclusions to be drawn.

This review includes four studies that randomly assigned a total of 275 patients with hydrocele to either aspiration and sclerotherapy or surgery. Aspiration and sclerotherapy failed to show a better result than surgery in terms of overall success, recurrence, febrile episodes, and adverse events. However, it had the advantage of being less painful, having fewer postoperative complications, shorter hospital stay, and lower cost. The quality of the evidence in this review is low to moderate. There are various types of materials that can be used for sclerotherapy, including hypertonic saline, povidone-iodine, bupivacaine, sodium morrhuate, and others. There are many different concentrations of these solutions and the ideal concentration is unknown. Injection volume also varies from a few millilitres to 20 mL or more. For these reasons, there is a great need for well-designed randomised controlled trials to compare different types of sclerosing agents, concentrations and volumes. These trials should have a sufficient number of participants and follow-up period. They should measure the outcomes mentioned above using standardised and validated measures.

The aim of this review was to determine which treatment is most effective in managing this condition. One study was found that compared nebulised rhDNase with placebo (normal saline). There were no differences seen in the clinical outcomes between the two groups. This study does not provide enough evidence to conclude which treatment is best for tracheomalacia. More research needs to be done in order to determine the best method of treatment.

This review included 21 studies with 2658 participants. Most participants had somatisation disorder or undifferentiated somatoform disorder. The duration of symptoms was at least several years. All studies assessed the effectiveness of some form of psychological therapy. We found no studies that included physical therapy. Types of psychological therapy included cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), behaviour therapy, third-wave CBT (mindfulness), psychodynamic therapies, and integrative therapy. Fifteen studies compared the studied psychological therapy with usual care or a waiting list. Five studies compared the intervention to enhanced or structured care. One study compared CBT with behaviour therapy. The number of sessions ranged from one to 13, and duration of treatment from one day to nine months. Follow-up assessment lasted from two weeks to 24 months. Most studies took place in outpatient settings. All studies were conducted and reported on in English. There were no clear benefits of psychological therapy on functional impairment or health care costs. Psychological therapies were superior to usual care or waiting list in terms of symptom severity, but effect sizes were small. The studies were heterogeneous, and quality of the evidence was low to moderate. The interventions generally did not report any harms, although most studies did not include 'adverse events' as an outcome measure. It is likely that there are people with somatoform disorders or MUPS who do not wish to receive psychological treatment. The number of such individuals and their characteristics are unknown. The results of this review therefore need to be interpreted with caution when applied to everyday practice.

We searched databases until May 2015 for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that investigated whether regional anaesthesia can prevent PPP development after any type of surgery. We included 63 RCTs in the review, but we only included 39 of these (with 3027 participants) in our main analysis. Keyhole surgery, open surgery, minimally invasive surgery and combination of regional and general anaesthesia were included as long as the participants received regional anaesthesia. We excluded patients with active cancer, known nerve damage or previous history of PPP. We grouped studies by type of surgery and anaesthesia used. We found that there is moderate-quality evidence that regional anaesthesia may reduce the risk of developing PPP three to 12 months after breast cancer surgery (18 studies, 1297 participants) and three to 18 months after thoracotomy (seven studies, 499 participants). Similarly, there is moderate-quality evidence that intravenous infusion of local anaesthetics may reduce the risk of developing PPP three to six months after breast cancer surgery (two studies, 97 participants). We found that there is moderate-quality evidence that regional anaesthesia may reduce the risk of developing PPP three to 12 months after caesarean section (four studies, 551 participants), and low-quality evidence that regional anaesthesia may reduce the risk of developing PPP three to 12 months after breast cancer surgery (16 studies, 1923 participants). For surgeries such as limb amputation, hernia repair, cardiac surgery and laparotomy, we could not pool the evidence. The quality of the evidence for the prevention of PPP was moderate for thoracotomy and caesarean section, low for breast cancer surgery and iliac crest bone graft harvesting, and unclear for other operations. The quality of the evidence was reduced by small numbers of studies and participants, missing data and poor study reporting. Regional anaesthesia may not always be feasible or safe. We cannot draw firm conclusions about which regional anaesthesia technique is the most effective for preventing PPP. We recommend that further research is carried out.

This review summarises the results of 28 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with a total of 1742 participants. We examined the efficacy and acceptability of pharmacological treatments in adult patients with borderline personality disorder. The drugs tested were first-generation antipsychotics (flupenthixol decanoate, haloperidol, thiothixene), second-generation antipsychotics (aripirazole, olanzapine, ziprasidone), mood stabilisers (carbamazepine, valproate semisodium, lamotrigine, topiramate), antidepressants (amitriptyline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, phenelzine sulfate, mianserin), and dietary supplementation (omega-3 fatty acid). Recent studies focused on second-generation antipsychotics and mood stabilisers. For individual drug groups, the evidence was sparse and of low quality. It was suggestive of a positive effect for second-generation antipsychotics and mood stabilisers, but the results require confirmation because most were based on single studies. Total BPD severity was not improved by any drug, and there were no significant improvements in the core BPD symptoms of chronic feelings of emptiness, identity disturbance and abandonment. Acceptability of treatment with all drugs was good, and adverse events were rare, except for olanzapine which caused significant weight gain, sedation and changes in blood test parameters. These findings are constrained by several limitations of the RCT evidence which limit their applicability to everyday clinical settings.

We found seven relevant trials, which included a total of 349 adults who had a short-term indwelling urinary catheter. However, nearly a third of participants did not complete the trials. Most study participants were older than 65 years of age and had urinary retention problems.
