Four trials looked at whether using a washout solution (saline or acidic) every day reduced the chance of a urinary tract infection when compared to no washout. We are uncertain if this has an important effect on the rate of urinary tract infections or how long the catheter stayed in because the results are imprecise. Three trials looked at different types of washout solution. We are uncertain if type of washout solution has an important effect on the rate of urinary tract infection or length of time each catheter was in because the results are imprecise. One trial compared stronger and weaker acidic solutions, but only 14 of the planned 50 participants completed this part of the trial. We are uncertain if different compositions of acidic solutions has an important effect on the rate of urinary tract infection or length of time each catheter was in because only 14 participants completed the trial. Four studies reported on possible harms caused by washouts, such as blood in the washout solution, changes in blood pressure and bladder spasms. There were very few small trials that met the review inclusion criteria. The high risk of bias of the included studies resulted in the evidence being graded as low or very low quality. Overall, we are uncertain about whether washouts are beneficial or harmful.

We searched several important databases such as CENTRAL, MEDLINE and PsycINFO for studies on this topic. The evidence is current to April 2014. We found 30 relevant studies. These studies included a total of 18,682 survivors of childhood cancer. The average age in the studies varied from a few months to 25 years. We found that the percentage of survivors who experienced severe fatigue was very different in the individual studies, ranging from 0% to 62%, with an average of 29%. This means that one out of every three survivors experienced severe fatigue. We also looked at subgroups of survivors based on the type of cancer they had, their age at the time of fatigue assessment, and compared them with a control group of siblings or people from the general population. We found that survivors of haematological cancer (blood cancers), like leukaemia, more often experienced fatigue than survivors of other types of cancer. We also found that survivors aged 16 and older more often experienced fatigue than younger survivors. We found no clear pattern in the type of cancer treatment related to fatigue. We looked at various factors that might be linked to fatigue, such as age at diagnosis, gender, psycho-social factors, and physical factors. We found that survivors with lower levels of education had higher levels of fatigue. We also found that survivors with depression were more tired than those without depression. We were not able to combine any of these findings to gain a better understanding of the overall picture, because each study measured the factors in its own way. One study followed a large group of survivors over time (more than two and a half years), and found that 31% of survivors experienced persistent severe fatigue. We cannot draw any conclusions from this single study, but it does suggest that survivors may experience long-term problems with fatigue. Overall, we found a lot of variation in the studies in terms of what they measured, how they measured it, and the results they found. This makes it difficult to get a good overview of the overall picture, but our review does provide a summary of the current state of knowledge on this topic.

The review authors identified 36 randomised controlled trials with 6914 critically ill adults who received mechanical ventilation for at least 48 hours. The trials were conducted mainly in Europe and the USA with one from Australia, one from Canada and one from Asia. The trials varied in the types of antibiotics used, duration of ventilation and level of critical illness. We concluded that giving a combination of topical and systemic antibiotics reduced both respiratory tract infections and total mortality whereas using topical antibiotics alone reduced respiratory tract infections but not mortality.

The review authors searched the literature and found five randomised controlled trials which met the inclusion criteria. All studies examined the effect of education on nursing staff in either nursing homes or nursing home units. One study looked at the impact of education on people living in group dwellings. The methods of four studies were flawed and the overall quality of all five was low. The five studies yielded inconsistent results. Thus, there is no convincing evidence that education reduces the use of physical restraints in nursing homes or nursing home units, but further research is needed.

We found four randomised controlled trials comparing glucocorticoid supplementation versus placebo in a total of 416 women undergoing ovarian hyperstimulation for IVF or ICSI. Two of the studies had data in a format that we could not use in our analyses, so results are based on data from two trials with a total of 310 women. Not all of these women had completed the treatment cycle, however, so results are based on data from 212 women. The evidence was of low or very low quality for all outcomes, mainly because of the small number of women in the studies and the fact that there were few live births and pregnancies, making it difficult to draw conclusions with certainty. Giving glucocorticoids as an add-on to stimulation had no apparent effect on live birth rate (low-quality evidence), but may increase the chance of becoming pregnant (low-quality evidence). There was insufficient evidence to determine whether there was any difference between the groups in multiple-pregnancy rate (very low-quality evidence) or miscarriage rate (very low-quality evidence). Neither of the studies reported OHSS or side effects. More research is needed to better understand the possible role of glucocorticoids in assisted reproduction.

We included eight randomised controlled trials with 291 people with cystic fibrosis aged between five and 23 years of age. Seven of the trials compared standard-dose rhGH (approximately 0.3 mg/kg/week) to no treatment and one trial compared placebo, standard-dose rhGH (0.3 mg/kg/week) and high-dose rhGH (0.5 mg/kg/week). Six trials lasted for one year and two for six months. We found that rhGH treatment may improve some of the lung function outcomes but there was no difference between standard and high-dose levels. The trials show evidence of improvement in the growth parameters (height, weight and lean body mass) with rhGH therapy, but only for standard-dose compared to no treatment. There is some evidence indicating a change in the level of fasting blood glucose with rhGH therapy, however, it did not cross the clinical threshold for diagnosis of diabetes in the trials of short duration. There is low- to very low-quality evidence for reduction in pulmonary exacerbations with no further significant adverse effects, but this is limited by the short duration of trials and the small number of participants. One small trial provided very low-quality evidence on improvement in quality of life. There is low-quality evidence for improvement in exercise capacity. None of the trials have systematically compared the expense of therapy on overall healthcare costs. We conclude that rhGH therapy is effective in improving growth in individuals with CF, but its effect on clinical outcomes such as survival remains unknown. Future long-term studies are needed before its use is routine in the CF population. We assessed the quality of the evidence for different outcomes ranging from very low to high. This was mainly due to imprecision in the results due to the small number of participants included in the analysis and the short duration of the trials.

We found 26 non-randomized studies with 1,695 participants. We grouped these studies into three categories: (1) removal from exposure compared to continued exposure, (2) reduction of exposure compared to continued exposure, and (3) removal from exposure compared to reduction of exposure. For each category we performed analyses including all studies and separate analyses including only the subset of patients who were exposed to low molecular weight agents. For all three groups of studies we found that removal from exposure may improve symptoms and lung function more than reduction of exposure. However, removal from exposure may also increase the risk of unemployment. Furthermore, removal from exposure had larger positive effects on lung function when we studied patients exposed to low molecular weight agents. Based on our results we would expect that of 1000 workers who continue to be exposed to an occupational agent without any intervention, 56 would report absence of asthma symptoms at follow-up. This number may increase to 219 if they reduce their exposure to the agent and up to 271 if they remove themselves from exposure altogether. Of 1000 workers who continue to be exposed to an occupational agent without any intervention, 500 would have some impairment in lung function at follow-up. This number may increase slightly if they reduce their exposure to the agent but may decrease to 384 if they remove themselves from exposure. We cannot make any conclusions about the effect of interventions on non-specific bronchial hyperreactivity because not enough data were reported. The overall quality of the evidence in this review is very low.

We found six studies including 1758 adults who had acid reflux (but not severe erosions on endoscopy) or mild inflammation of the esophagus. We did not include studies of people with severe disease. One study was older (average age 73 years) but the other five studies were of fitter, younger volunteers. All studies looked at people who were taking proton pump inhibitor drugs and wanted to know whether it was better to continue or discontinue them. One study asked participants to stop taking their proton pump inhibitor completely and the other five studies asked participants to take their proton pump inhibitor only when they felt symptoms coming back. We found that people who only took their proton pump inhibitor when they felt symptoms were more likely to have poor control of their acid reflux symptoms, but they took fewer pills and reported lower satisfaction. There was no information about side effects or costs. We rated the overall quality of evidence as low for lack of symptom control and as moderate for reduction in pill burden. The low quality of evidence means that we are very uncertain about the results.

The Information Specialist of Cochrane Schizophrenia ran an electronic search of their specialised register up to 30 March 2015 for trials that randomised people with schizophrenia to receive either a social skills programme or standard care or to a control. We identified 13 trials that met our inclusion criteria. All 13 trials randomised participants who had been diagnosed with schizophrenia according to the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Text Revision (DSM-IV-TR) (American Psychiatric Association 2000) or ICD-10 (World Health Organization 1992). The trials originated from seven countries: China (9 trials), USA (1), Sweden (1), Italy (1), Taiwan (1), Spain (1) and Iran (1). We found that social skills training may lead to an improvement in social functioning when compared to standard care, however, the evidence is weak. When social skills training was compared to participation in a discussion group, there was no difference in patient outcomes. The evidence available is very low quality, meaning we are very uncertain about the results. To fully investigate whether social skills training can improve the social functioning of people with schizophrenia, we need further high quality trials. Seven of the 13 trials were funded by the National Government, two by a charitable institution, one by a university research centre and one by a pharmaceutical company. One trial did not report funding source. The remaining trials did not provide information on funding sources.

The review authors searched the literature up to 29 March 2016 and identified one study (225 participants, 145 with tophi at start of the trial) comparing pegloticase with placebo. Participants were assigned to receive biweekly pegloticase infusion (8 mg), monthly pegloticase infusion (8 mg), or placebo (two weekely). The results showed that the chance of having no tophi after six months was about five times higher with biweekly pegloticase than with placebo, and almost three times higher with monthly pegloticase than with placebo. In those with tophi at the start of the trial, 21 out of 52 people given biweekly pegloticase had all their tophi resolved, compared with 2 out of 27 given placebo; 11 out of 52 people given monthly pegloticase had all their tophi resolved, compared with 2 out of 27 given placebo. More people on both types of pegloticase infusion experienced side effects than those on placebo, mainly because of gout flares. Given the lack of studies, we could not assess other treatments for tophi such as surgery. We found moderate-quality evidence that biweekly pegloticase infusion reduces tophi, but at the cost of increasing gout flares and other side effects.

This review examined whether giving newborns with very low birth weight (VLBW) <1500 g) antibodies by mouth could prevent necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). The search identified five studies on oral immunoglobulins for the prevention of NEC of which three met the inclusion criteria. In this review of the three eligible trials (including 2095 newborns with very low birth weight), the oral administration of IgG or a combination of IgG and IgA did not result in a significant reduction in the incidence of NEC, need for surgery or death.

The authors concluded that there is now good evidence that postoperative chemotherapy should be considered routine treatment in women with early stage endometrial cancer. It reduces the risk of dying from the disease and the risk of developing a second cancer in the pelvis. It could also reduce the need for radiotherapy in some patients.

We found 35 studies that met our inclusion criteria. We included studies in six languages. Nineteen of the studies were conducted in low- and middle-income countries and sixteen in high-income countries. Some of the studies asked people who had used the programmes what they thought of them, and some asked people what they would think if a particular programme were available. The programmes examined covered a range of topics, but most gave people information or tried to help them change behaviour. What people said fell into a number of broad areas: •	They liked receiving messages and felt supported and connected, but... •	...they had problems with accessing the programmes because of equipment, skills, or costs. •	They had concerns about privacy and confidentiality, especially for sensitive topics like family planning, abortion, or HIV. To make programmes better, people suggested that messages should be tailored to the individual, and that the messages should be in a format that suited the person, including when and how often they received them. They also said that messages should be free of charge. Overall, people's views on these types of programmes were mixed. On one hand, they liked receiving messages and felt supported and connected; on the other hand, they faced problems with access, privacy, and confidentiality. Programme planners should take these points into account when designing and implementing these types of programmes. Trial authors should also report on how they addressed these issues in their trial and should report whether any participants had problems accessing the programme.

The aim of this review was to assess the efficacy and safety of different pharmacological interventions for the management of opioid withdrawal. In the present review, 23 studies (2467 participants) have been included. All the studies focused on adults who were primarily dependent on heroin. The comparison between the different drugs or drug combinations indicated that all medications used in the included studies were equally effective in facilitating completion of naloxone-assisted opioid detoxification. The type of medication and the treatment programme had an impact on the intensity of the withdrawal symptoms and on the rate of dropout from treatment. The use of methadone maintenance compared with other pharmacological interventions was associated with fewer dropouts and higher rates of completion of treatment. However, the differences between the effects of the various medications were small.

The review found that use of antagonists to precipitate withdrawal from methadone maintenance is safe and effective. This approach reduces the amount of time that people spend in withdrawal and enables clinical staff to establish doses of naltrexone that will keep them abstinent from opioids. Use of heavy sedation or anaesthesia to manage withdrawal from methadone maintenance does not make withdrawal easier or faster. However, it increases the risk of serious harm to the user and is expensive.

The aim of this review was to evaluate the effects of molindone for schizophrenia compared to placebo or other medications. The results are limited by the small number of studies, small total sample, and by poor study design. This makes drawing clear conclusions difficult. However, the side effect profile of molindone is similar to that of other older typical antipsychotic drugs, apart from the fact that it causes more weight loss. There is currently no evidence to suggest that it has an atypical antipsychotic drug side effect profile.

The studies we found were of poor quality with inadequate treatments and lack of long-term results. We found some evidence that triple-combination cream containing hydroquinone, tretinoin, and fluocinolone acetonide used for four to eight weeks was better than hydroquinone used alone, and also better than the dual combinations of tretinoin and hydroquinone, tretinoin and fluocinolone, and hydroquinone and fluocinolone. However, these results may have been affected by the poorer quality of the studies. There was some evidence that azelaic acid (20%) was more effective than 2% hydroquinone and by the same margin 4% hydroquinone, although this may have been due to chance. Tretinoin used for four to 12 weeks was only better than placebo in terms of self-assessment by the participants in one out of two studies, but significantly better than placebo by other measures. Skin-lightening complex, Thiospot, was more effective than placebo. Most adverse effects of treatments were mild and transient, consisting of skin irritation, itching, burning, and stinging.

We searched the literature in July 2013 and identified 12 randomised controlled trials that compared 5-FU to control in people undergoing trabeculectomy for glaucoma or ocular hypertension. We categorised three groups of participants: those who had never had surgery before (primary trabeculectomy); those at high risk of failure because of previous failed surgery or other complications; and those having combined cataract and glaucoma surgery. We further divided these groups according to whether they received 5-FU just during the operation (intraoperative 5-FU) or immediately after (postoperative 5-FU) and whether they received a low dose or a regular dose of postoperative 5-FU. Our results show that there is an overall benefit to using 5-FU in reducing the risk of surgical failure in the first year after surgery, but that this benefit is much greater in those at high risk of failure and those having primary trabeculectomy. However, 5-FU does increase the risk of several complications. Doctors and patients may therefore choose to avoid its use. None of the trials asked the participants about their views on the use of 5-FU. We graded the quality of the evidence as moderate for the overall benefit of 5-FU in reducing risk of surgical failure; low for the use of combined surgery with 5-FU and very low for the use of low-dose postoperative 5-FU.

We found 55 studies with 16,154 participants who were using ICS for at least two years. We found that ICS reduced the number of exacerbations (flare-ups) and improved quality of life but had little effect on lung function. However, there are risks of side effects such as thrush, hoarseness and increased risk of pneumonia. Not everyone who takes ICS will experience these side effects but for some it may be troublesome. It is important to weigh up the potential benefit of ICS in terms of reducing flare-ups and improving quality of life against the possibility of these side effects. This review also found that there was wide variation in the results from different studies. Future studies need to take account of whether patients have previously responded to ICS and also whether they have any of the side effects associated with ICS.

We included 80 randomised controlled trials with 5820 participants. We found that NSAIDs were better at relieving period pain than placebo (a pretend treatment) or paracetamol (a common over-the-counter painkiller), but caused more side effects. There was no evidence that COX-2-specific inhibitors (a type of NSAID) were better than traditional NSAIDs. None of the NSAIDs tested were clearly better than each other, although more research is needed as most comparisons were made with very small numbers of women. Half of the studies were commercially funded. NSAIDs reduced period pain by an average of 42% (range 36% to 48%), while placebo reduced pain by an average of 16% (13% to 19%). If 18 out of 100 women taking placebo have good pain relief, then between 45 and 53 out of 100 taking NSAIDs will have good pain relief. Women who took NSAIDs experienced side effects on average 17% of the time (15% to 20%), compared with nine out of 100 taking placebo. If 10 out of 100 women taking placebo experience side effects, then 11 to 14 out of 100 women taking NSAIDs will do so. The overall quality of the evidence was low. This was because most studies did not report how they were conducted in enough detail.

The authors searched the literature until May 2013 and identified seven randomized controlled trials that compared the effects of high versus low levels of positive end-expiratory pressure on outcomes in adults receiving mechanical ventilation. These studies included a total of 2565 participants. Five of the studies (2417 participants) compared high versus low levels of positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) with the same tidal volume in both groups, and in the remaining two studies (148 participants), the tidal volume was different between high- and low-PEEP groups. The main problem encountered in this review was the fact that the studies differed in terms of the levels of PEEP applied and the target population studied. Two of the studies focused on patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome, and the remaining five studies included a broader spectrum of patients who required mechanical ventilation. We found no clear evidence that high levels of PEEP, as compared with low levels, reduced the risk of death before hospital discharge. High levels of PEEP showed no significant difference in the risk of barotrauma but did improve oxygenation. The number of ventilator-free days showed no significant difference between the two groups. Available data were insufficient to allow determination of length of stay in the intensive care unit (ICU). The subgroup of patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome showed a decrease in mortality in the ICU, although it must be noted that in two of the three included studies, the authors used a protective ventilatory strategy involving a low tidal volume and high levels of PEEP.

We searched the literature in April 2014 and found 23 new randomized controlled trials (RCTs) to add to the 19 included in our previous review. We were able to include 42 RCTs with 11,399 patients in this update. The patients included in the trials had trauma, major surgery, sepsis, burns or cancer. We found that all types of HES increase the risk of AKI and the need for RRT. The risk of AKI using the RIFLE-F failure criteria was also increased with HES. However, we found that the risk of AKI according to the RIFLE-R criteria was reduced with HES, but this disappeared when we excluded the urine output component of the RIFLE-R criterion. The RIFLE-I injury criterion was increased with HES. No differences were seen between septic and non-septic patients, high molecular weight versus low molecular weight HES or between different doses of HES. Quality of the evidenceOverall, the methodological quality of the studies was good.

This review shows that use of Rheum officinale may temporarily improve some measures of kidney function in patients with chronic kidney disease. However, the number of studies is small, the quality of these studies is variable, and the results need to be interpreted cautiously. There are no reports of serious side effects in patients with chronic kidney disease who used Rheum officinale, but this may simply reflect the fact that it was used at relatively low doses for short periods of time. More rigorous clinical trials are needed before any recommendations can be made about using Rheum officinale to treat patients with chronic kidney disease.

We searched differing databases of published research for all papers relating to the diagnostic accuracy of IQCODE in July 2011 and April 2015. We identified 72 eligible papers, from which we extracted information to form the basis of this review. We included 13 of these papers, which included a total of 2745 participants, 1413 (51%) of whom had been diagnosed with dementia. The papers were of mixed quality, with several weaknesses identified that could undermine the validity of our findings. The main weakness was the variation in the way that studies were carried out, particularly in terms of how many participants were recruited and how those taking part were selected. This means that some caution is required when interpreting our findings. The review suggests that IQCODE can be used to identify patients in the general hospital setting who may have dementia and would benefit from further assessment. It is not as good at identifying those who do not have dementia.

This review included three studies with 91 participants. All three studies looked at different ways to treat spasticity in people who had had a stroke at least one year ago and still had some ability to move their affected arm. They evaluated: modified constraint-induced movement therapy (mCIMT); task practice therapy with cyclic functional electrical stimulation (FES); and occupational, manual therapy with dynamic elbow extension splinting. We found very little evidence for the effectiveness of these therapies in improving either spasticity or active function in people with upper limb spasticity due to stroke. No study looked at the effect of these therapies on passive function (caring for the affected limb) or on the carer's experience of providing care. Overall, we found little evidence for the effectiveness of outpatient MD rehabilitation in improving active function and impairments following BoNT for upper limb spasticity in adults with chronic stroke. More research is needed to establish the effects of BoNT combined with MD rehabilitation in improving spasticity and function in adults and children with spasticity as a result of brain injury.

This review aimed to assess the beneficial and harmful effects of antiviral drugs for preventing an HBV recurrence in the liver graft of patients who have undergone liver transplantation for HBV-related cirrhosis. Four small randomised clinical trials, enrolling 136 participants, were analysed. The treatments compared were standard therapy (lamivudine) versus immunoglobulin; lamivudine plus interferon versus lamivudine; and lamivudine plus adefovir dipivoxil versus lamivudine plus interferon. None of these studies showed statistically significant differences in any of the comparisons and outcomes. More large randomised clinical trials are needed to determine the best treatment for preventing HBV recurrence.

This review aimed to evaluate the benefits and harms of different treatments in LADA, but found only 15 small studies (1019 participants) which made it difficult to draw firm conclusions. The duration of follow-up of these studies ranged from three months to 10 years. There was no standard way of diagnosing LADA in the studies. Two studies showed that sulphonylureas led to earlier insulin dependence and poorer long-term blood sugar control compared to insulin. One study showed that adding vitamin D to insulin may help to preserve pancreatic beta cells. Another treatment, GAD65 encapsulated in a slow release form (Alumina) tried to reactivate the body's own immune system to produce insulin. In one study, this treatment maintained C-peptide levels after five years. There was some concern about the reliability of the evidence due to poor reporting of study methods and inconsistency in the results between studies.

We identified 70 randomised controlled trials (RCTs; 44,958 participants). The RCTs compared the effectiveness of social norms campaigns to control the misuse of alcohol among students with various comparison conditions. The social norms interventions were delivered face-to-face (81%), in writing (13%) or through both face-to-face and written materials (6%). They were implemented in 11 countries. Trials had a mean follow-up period of six months and measured whether students' alcohol use had changed after the intervention. We summarised the results in terms of the degree to which participation in the social norms intervention led to a decrease in alcohol-related problems, binge drinking, quantity and frequency of drinking and blood alcohol concentration (BAC). We found that social norms interventions reduced alcohol-related problems and BAC at four or more months, but these effects were small. No effects were found for reducing binge drinking, quantity and frequency of drinking and no differences between social norms interventions and controls were found for peak BAC. The quality of the evidence was assessed as moderate for most outcomes, ranging from low for BAC to moderate for alcohol-related problems, binge drinking, quantity, frequency of drinking and peak BAC. The results of this Cochrane systematic review suggest that social norms interventions have little or no effect on reducing students' misuse of alcohol.

We identified three studies with a total of 492 adults who had undergone 530 THAs. All studies investigated the role of occupational therapy interventions. One study (81 participants) compared the provision of hip precautions, equipment and functional restrictions with no provision of these. We found that there was no difference in function (measured using the Harris Hip Score) or health-related quality of life (measured using the Short Form-12) between groups at 12 months follow-up. However, we cannot say this with any degree of certainty due to the very low quality of the evidence. There were no reports of hip dislocations or adverse events in either group in the first 12 months after surgery. The second study (265 participants) compared the provision of hip precautions with and without the prescription of postoperative equipment and restrictions on functional activities. Again, we found no difference between the two groups in terms of perceived satisfaction in the rate of recovery, or the risk of hip dislocation, but we are uncertain about this due to the very low quality of the evidence. Pain, function, health-related quality of life, re-operations or total adverse events were not measured in this study. The third study (146 participants) looked at whether an enhanced postoperative education and rehabilitation service improved function compared to conventional rehabilitation strategies. We found no difference between the two groups in terms of function (measured using an unreported outcome), but we are uncertain about this due to the very low quality of the evidence. Pain, health-related quality of life, global assessment of treatment success, hip dislocation, re-operations or total adverse events were not measured in this study. Overall, the evidence for the use of occupational therapy interventions other than standard care to improve function and prevent dislocation after THA is not strong. More research is needed.

The authors found three small studies (with a total of 271 participants) that met the inclusion criteria for the review. Two studies compared the LNG-IUD with no treatment and one compared it with GnRH agonists. The results showed that the LNG-IUD may help to reduce the risk of painful periods by about 78% when used as treatment after surgery for endometriosis. This finding was based on moderate strength evidence. A larger study would be needed to confirm whether this really works.

We searched for studies evaluating the effects of changes to the environment where people choose their food and drinks on what they select or consume. We looked for studies that compared choices made by people exposed to an environmental change with those of people not exposed to an environmental change. We did not include studies which compared people who had chosen to be in a control group with those who had chosen to participate in the intervention group. We also did not include studies which only looked at how attractive or appealing the food and drinks were to people, nor those which only looked at how much people liked what they chose. We found 24 studies which met our inclusion criteria. Most were conducted in laboratories, with adults taking part, and with a focus on reducing the choice of unhealthy foods and drinks. We grouped the studies into two categories: those which changed the number of options available and those which changed the positioning of foods and drinks. Fewer options: When we combined the results of three studies, we found that having fewer options to choose from may result in a large reduction in the selection of those foods/drinks, and a moderate reduction in how much of them was consumed. However, the certainty of this evidence was low. Proximity of foods and drinks: When we combined the results of 15 studies, we found that placing foods and drinks farther away may result in a moderate reduction in both their selection and consumption. This effect was greater the farther away the food/drink was placed; if only the targeted food/drink was available; if participants had fewer financial resources; and if the study was at high risk of bias. The certainty of this evidence was low. Changing either the number of available options or their positioning may reduce selection and consumption of unhealthy foods and drinks. To enable more certain and generalisable conclusions about these effects, further research is needed in real-world settings, studying a wider range of foods and drinks, and measuring effects over longer time periods.

The purpose of this review was to evaluate the effectiveness and safety of PEP in preventing HIV infection in healthcare workers following percutaneous exposure to HIV. Occupational PEP is commonly prescribed, but its value has never been assessed in a randomized controlled trial. One study was found that examined the circumstances under which PEP was prescribed and whether or not it was taken by healthcare workers following exposure to HIV. Based on these results, we conclude that a four-week regimen of PEP should be started as soon as possible after exposure, depending on the risk of seroconversion. Multi-drug combinations are recommended, but there are no studies to support this recommendation. Adverse effects of PEP are common, especially with regimens that include the drug indinavir. However, only a small proportion of healthcare workers stop taking their assigned medication.

This review shows that cognitive-behavioural approaches have some effect on reducing pain and increasing a person's ability to cope with it. However, the results were modest and temporary. This suggests that while such approaches may be helpful to some patients, they will not solve the problem of chronic pain. More research is needed to understand which parts of the treatment help who, and why.

We found 15 initiatives that included more than 260,000 adults. None were in lower-middle-income or low-income countries. All except one had a pre-post study design (observing the same group of people both before and after an intervention was implemented). Only half of the initiatives provided information on the average amount of salt that people consumed at the start and end of the intervention. On average, salt intake decreased in six initiatives and remained the same in four. In five initiatives, the average salt intake appeared to have increased, but in two of these the starting level of salt intake was several years ago, so it is uncertain whether the current level of salt intake has actually increased. Seven of the 10 initiatives with information on average salt intake used more than one way to reduce salt intake (e.g. altering the composition of foods, changing how food is served in restaurants). Six of the 10 initiatives reported that men experienced a greater reduction in salt intake than women. The initiatives did not report on other groups that we considered important to examine (e.g. young people, older people, Indigenous peoples). No initiatives reported any harm caused to participants. Overall, the quality of evidence was low. There were concerns about aspects of the design of all of the studies. The differences between the findings of some of the studies suggest that some interventions may be more successful than others.

This review looked at whether drug treatment helps to reduce the severity of sleep bruxism. We included seven studies with 86 adults in total. Six different types of medications were examined: amitriptyline, bromocriptine, clonidine, levodopa, propranolol and tryptophan. All medications were compared with an inactive substance that has no treatment value (placebo). The type of study design was cross-over, which means that each person in the trial receives two or more treatments, one or more of which are active. This type of design can lead to carry-over effects, where the effects of the first treatment are still present when the second treatment is taken, making it difficult to separate the effects of each treatment. The main outcome was an objective measure of jaw muscle activity during sleep. Other outcomes included other measures of sleep quality, dream content, side effects and the amount of time spent in different stages of sleep. The results were not very reliable because of the small numbers of people in the studies and because the studies used different ways to measure the main outcome. For most comparisons, the results were imprecise and could represent anything from a large effect of the medication to no effect or even a slightly harmful effect. This was especially true for amitriptyline, propranolol and tryptophan. The results for clonidine were uncertain for all outcomes except for a reduction in the amount of rapid eye movement (REM) sleep and an increase in the second stage of sleep. Levodopa had no beneficial effects for either of the two outcomes assessed. Side effects were common with all medications, including amitriptyline (drowsiness, dry mouth and insomnia), propranolol (dry mouth and nausea), clonidine (dizziness, drowsiness, dry mouth and constipation) and levodopa (nausea, vomiting and abdominal pain). Because of the small number of studies, which did not have a large enough sample size to provide meaningful results, we cannot recommend any particular treatment for sleep bruxism. Larger studies are needed to determine which treatments work best for whom.

We found 10 studies with 1015 participants comparing immune-enhancing formulas containing omega-3 fatty acids and/or antioxidants versus standard feedings or no supplementation. These studies were often small and of poor methodological quality, which could affect accuracy of results. The evidence is current to September 2016. There was no difference in the number of deaths from any cause for participants receiving immune-enhancing formulas with omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants compared to those not receiving these components. It is unclear whether there is any benefit to reducing the duration of mechanical ventilation or length of stay in the intensive care unit, as well as improving blood oxygen levels in people who receive this treatment. Finally, it is uncertain whether there are more side effects in the people who receive immune-enhancing formulas with omega-3 fatty acids and antioxidants, including heart problems, digestive issues, and any other adverse events. Overall, the evidence for these outcomes was of low or very low quality.

This review examined whether the way researchers ask about side effects influences the information that is provided by patients. We found 33 studies that asked participants about side effects in different ways. These studies were of mixed quality and looked at a wide range of side effects. Side effects were reported less often when participants were asked general questions rather than specific ones. Some side effects were only reported when participants were asked specifically about them. This means that a more general question may'miss' some side effects that are important to patients. It is not known what types of side effects are missed with a general question, whether they are relatively minor or serious. An interview was better than a questionnaire for detecting some quite serious side effects, but not all studies agree with this finding. Overall, the results support concerns that the way researchers ask about side effects affects what information is provided by patients. This information is important because it may affect the care provided to patients and their well-being. Future studies could improve the way they report side effects and other information, such as how long side effects were considered, who asked the questions, and how the information was analysed.

Ten studies were potentially eligible for this review and one study met our inclusion criteria. The study suggested that supervision at night may reduce the risk of death due to SUDEP. More research is needed to find out if other treatments like seizure detectors, safety pillows, antidepressants, early surgical evaluation, education programs, and drugs that prevent seizures are effective in reducing the number of people dying from SUDEP. The quality of the evidence was very low and the study had its own problems with how it was conducted. Future studies will need to be larger and better conducted to help us understand which treatments might reduce the number of people dying from SUDEP.

We looked at the research published up to 12 October 2015. We found seven studies with a total of 208 people with cystic fibrosis aged between seven and 63 years of age. Most were stable, but one group had just been in hospital because of a flare up of their cystic fibrosis. The studies lasted from just a few days to two years. We did not find any studies comparing autogenic drainage to exercise. The studies compared autogenic drainage to other treatments such as chest physiotherapy, vibrations to the chest or a combination of different treatments. The studies did not show any difference in lung function after autogenic drainage compared to other treatments. Three studies looked at quality of life and found no clear improvement with autogenic drainage. Only one study mentioned side effects and reported a drop in oxygen levels during one of the breathing techniques, but not during autogenic drainage. We could not combine many of the studies due to the different designs. Even though six of the studies used a cross-over design (where participants received each treatment for a set period of time and then swapped over to the next treatment), we could not combine them due to the different treatments used. We could only include one study with a parallel design (where participants received either one or the other treatment throughout the study). The quality of the evidence was generally low or very low. This is because it was possible for the participants to know which treatment they were receiving and this could affect how they performed during the study. We also had concerns about some of the studies not reporting all of their results and some of the studies only including a small number of participants.

This review examined the efficacy of psychological therapies for generalised anxiety disorder (GAD). We included 25 randomised controlled trials (RCTs), with 13 RCTs (850 participants) contributing to the main analysis. There was strong evidence that cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) is an effective short-term treatment for GAD, leading to a greater reduction in anxiety symptoms than treatment as usual or wait list control. However, there was insufficient evidence to determine whether CBT is more effective than other psychological therapies for GAD.

We searched for evidence on 15 May 2017 and identified 12 randomised controlled trials, which included a total of 1856 women who had experienced two or more spontaneous miscarriages. Eight of these trials compared progestogen to a placebo and the other four compared progestogen to no treatment. The trials were conducted in India, Jordan, UK and USA. Most of the women had had three or more previous miscarriages and were within six months of their last miscarriage when they started treatment. Progestogens were given either by mouth (tablets), intramuscular injection or vaginal pessary. The results suggest that progestogen supplementation may reduce the risk of miscarriage in subsequent pregnancies. However, we should interpret this result with caution as the overall quality of the evidence for this outcome was only moderate. A minor increase in live births was also noted, but there was considerable inconsistency between the individual studies for this outcome. The evidence for the effect on preterm birth rate was very low, and no clear differences were seen for other adverse outcomes including neonatal death, fetal abnormal development or stillbirth. Overall, the review found that progestogen may help to prevent miscarriage in women with unexplained recurrent miscarriage. More research is needed to determine if this finding is correct and to discover how progestogen works to prevent miscarriage.

We found 14 randomised controlled trials (studies in which participants are assigned to one of two or more treatment groups using a random method) with 1298 participants. Nine of these studies (704 participants) examined the effects of CM and five studies (594 participants) looked at MIB. Most of the studies had relatively small numbers of participants and some were at risk of bias. The overall quality of the evidence was rated as low to moderate. Few of the studies looked at the health of the baby and most did not look at how the psychosocial intervention affected the mother's use of drugs during pregnancy. The limited evidence from the included trials suggested that psychosocial interventions do not improve the health of babies born to mothers who use drugs. This is likely due to the fact that most of the studies did not examine the health of the babies properly. No adverse effects of psychosocial interventions were reported. The findings of our review suggest that psychosocial interventions are unlikely to help improve the health of babies born to mothers who use drugs, but more research is needed to be sure.

The evidence is current to January 2017. We found 31 studies with more than 27,000 people who received coronary angiography or percutaneous coronary intervention through the radial artery in the Netherlands, France, United Kingdom, Italy, Spain, Sweden, Denmark, Germany, Poland, and the United States of America. Receiving coronary angiography or percutaneous coronary intervention through the radial artery did not increase the risk of any complication, except for excessive bleeding and complications at the access site. Furthermore, the use of the radial approach was associated with a lower risk of death. The quality of the evidence was moderate for net adverse clinical events, cardiac death, and procedural success, and high for myocardial infarction and all-cause mortality. The quality of evidence for bleeding and access site complications was low because of inconsistent results among the included studies.

We looked for studies published up to July 2017. We found two studies that met our inclusion criteria. One took place in hospitals and involved a palliative care team. It compared those who received a palliative care assessment with those who did not. The other study took place in nursing homes and evaluated the use of a decision aid for families to assist them in making decisions about food and fluid intake. We do not feel that either study was well enough conducted or reported to allow us to draw reliable conclusions about the effectiveness of palliative care interventions for people with advanced dementia; however, there are six ongoing studies that we expect to include in future updates of this review. Currently, there is a lack of knowledge about how best to provide palliative care for people with advanced dementia.

This review found that there are at least three ways to sequence CT and RT and that these different ways do not appear to affect the time until women die from their breast cancer or the time before the cancer returns. Because of the small number of studies, some of the results remain imprecise.

The included randomised controlled trials (RCTs) were conducted in the community setting, with interventions mainly delivered by health professionals, and had a short- to medium-term follow-up (up to 24 weeks). The review authors did not detect any relevant source of bias in six of the included studies, but the remaining three studies did not provide sufficient information to allow judgement of risk of bias. The evidence is current to January 2015. We found that chromium supplements lead to a small decrease in body weight of 1.1 kg when compared to placebo, but this result should be interpreted with caution because of the overall low quality of the included studies and the fact that it may not be replicable in longer trials. We found no clear evidence that chromium supplements are more effective than placebo in reducing body weight or body fat when measured at six months. The currently available evidence does not allow firm conclusions regarding the use of chromium supplements as a means of treating obesity. The quality of the evidence was low, and future studies should aim to provide higher-quality evidence.

We identified 11 randomised controlled trials with a total of 886 participants. Each trial evaluated a different comparison but on different types of wounds. None of the wounds had a significant risk of bias related to how they had been randomised or whether the people assessing the outcomes knew which treatment the participants had received. However, the overall quality of the evidence presented was low to moderate at best. For most comparisons there was no clear evidence of a difference in wound healing. Where some evidence for possible treatment effects was reported, it was from single studies with small numbers of participants and was classed as moderate or low quality evidence. This means it is likely or very likely that further research will have an important impact on our confidence in the estimate of effect, and may change the estimate. Overall, there is no clear evidence on which antiseptic or antibiotic preparation is the most effective for wounds healing by secondary intention.

This review includes five randomised controlled studies with a total of 1049 women. They evaluated five different technique modifications during either amniocentesis (three studies) or chorionic villus sampling (two studies). Each modification was investigated in a single small study. None of the interventions resulted in improved outcomes. The overall quality of the included studies was low. Therefore, there is no evidence to support any specific technique.

The evidence is current to January 2018. We found 10 studies. Six of these studies were conducted in upper-middle-income countries (China, Mexico, and South Africa), one in a lower-middle-income country (Bangladesh), and three in a high-income country (Canada). Seven studies examined wheat flour fortified with folic acid alone or with other nutrients. Three studies included maize flour fortified with folic acid alone or with other nutrients. The length of time that women were followed during the studies ranged from two weeks to 36 months; two other studies measured blood levels of folate in women before and after flour fortification began and continued for up to seven years afterwards. Most studies presented information at the group level only. We did not find any studies that reported on the effects of folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour on neural tube defects. One study showed that women's chances of having a baby with a neural tube defect was significantly lower if they consumed wheat flour fortified with folic acid and other B vitamins compared to unfortified flour. Folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour may increase blood levels of folate in pregnant women, adults, and women of reproductive age. However, the quality of this evidence was very low. There was no evidence of an effect on anaemia in children who consumed folic acid-fortified wheat flour. We could not reach any conclusions about the effects of folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour on other health outcomes because the number of studies was small and the quality of evidence was low. None of the studies reported whether there were any adverse events related to folic acid fortification of wheat or maize flour. Our findings are limited because most studies were of poor quality, and the overall quality of evidence was low or very low.

The review found two studies including 1,124,483 newborns of which 210 had cystic fibrosis. The studies had different methods and measured slightly different things. We could not calculate the overall results of the trial as we had hoped, but we were able to analyse the results from one trial. Newborn screening led to better nutrition. Although initial lung infections were less severe among screen participants, other factors affected their lung health. These factors included whether or not they had the severe form of the disease and whether or not they were infected with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, a type of bacteria that can cause lung damage. We conclude that screening leads to better nutrition and that although the lungs of screen participants were healthier initially, other factors had an impact on their lung health. We recommend further research to determine how often individuals should be tested for the presence of Pseudomonas aeruginosa and what additional treatment they should receive if infected.

We identified all randomized trials which compared two blood pressure targets in adults with at least moderately elevated blood pressure and with confirmed diabetes mellitus. The review includes five trials with 7314 participants. The duration of treatment was 3.7 to 6 years. One large trial compared a blood pressure target of less than 120/60 mmHg with a target of less than 140/85 mmHg. Four trials compared targets of less than 80/75 mmHg with a target of less than 90/85 mmHg. Using more drugs, the 'lower' target was achieved in most of the participants. The number of deaths was similar in the two groups, as was the occurrence of heart attack and congestive heart failure. The occurrence of stroke was significantly reduced, by 17%, in the group assigned to the 'lower' target. Serious adverse events increased substantially in the 'lower' target group, by 115 per 1000 participants. When we analyzed the four trials that compared 'lower' diastolic blood pressure targets (less than 80 mmHg) with standard targets (less than 90 mmHg), we found similar results in terms of death, heart attack, and congestive heart failure. The occurrence of stroke was slightly lower in the 'lower' target group by 32 per 1000 participants. Total serious adverse events were not reported. We cannot recommend lowering blood pressure targets in diabetic patients to less than 120/60 mmHg or to less than 80/75 mmHg based on the evidence available at this moment.

This review of 14 randomised controlled trials found that non-removable casts are better than removable casts or dressings alone in healing diabetic foot ulcers. The review also found that surgery to lengthen the tendon at the back of the ankle, when used with a non-removable cast, can lead to more foot ulcers healing and remaining healed after two years than a non-removable cast alone.

We found five studies that included a total of 235 participants. Two studies examined the effect of cognitive training, two of compensatory strategy training and one each of meditation and physical activity. All five studies focused on women with a history of breast cancer. The median age of participants in four studies was 65 years and seven years in one study. We found that cognitive training improved objective measures of cognition including processing speed, executive functions, cognitive flexibility, language, delayed- and immediate- memory. It also improved subjective reports of cognitive function and mental well-being. Compensatory strategy training improved objective measures of delayed-, immediate- and verbal memory, as well as self-reported cognitive function and spiritual quality of life. However, it did not improve physical well-being at either immediately after the intervention or two months later. In fact, there was some suggestion that mental well-being was lower in those who received compensatory strategy training compared to the control group at the end of the intervention. Meditation and physical activity had no measurable effects on cognitive function. However, these results should be treated with caution because of concerns about the quality of the evidence. To fully investigate whether there is benefit in such interventions, we need larger studies which involve more people, take place at multiple locations and include information about how often participants use the strategies they learn. Future studies should also consider whether the effects of the interventions generalise to everyday functioning (i.e. impact on activities of daily living). Additionally, it would be useful to conduct research with cancer patient groups other than women with breast cancer.

The Information Specialist of Cochrane Schizophrenia ran an electronic search of their specialised register up to 30 March 2015 for trials that randomised people with schizophrenia to receive either chlorpromazine or piperacetazine. The search returned 12 records, which referred to six unique trials. Five trials met the review inclusion criteria and provided usable data. All five trials were from the 1970s and randomly allocated 343 people to receive either chlorpromazine or piperacetazine. Poor reporting made it difficult to obtain reliable information from the trials. Only short-term information (less than 12 weeks) was available. The trials did not report on negative symptoms, economic costs or final outcome. Very little information was available for analysis. The evidence is very low quality, so we cannot draw firm conclusions. Chlorpromazine and piperacetazine may have similar clinical effectiveness, but more high quality research is needed before a conclusion can be drawn.

The review authors searched the literature and identified and screened 211 articles on the subject. Five randomised controlled studies with a total of 207 participants were included in the final analysis. The participants were randomly allocated to either a stent or an operation. The results showed that there was no difference in the number of people who died within 30 days after the intervention (2.9% with a stent, 2.9% with an operation). There was also no difference in the overall complication rate (41% with a stent, 46% with an operation), but there was a higher technical success rate for relieving the blockage with an operation (87% versus 79%). Stent placement required less time to do and less blood was lost (50 ml with a stent, 350 ml with an operation) and the stay in hospital was shorter (11 days with a stent, 17 days with an operation).

We found nine randomised controlled trials comparing albumin, hydroxyethyl starch (HES), or mannitol with no treatment or placebo that involved a total of 1867 women. The main outcomes were rates of OHSS and pregnancy. We did not include studies where the women knew what treatment they were getting. Not all studies reported all outcomes. The main limitations of the evidence were imprecision, poor reporting of study methods, and failure to blind assessors to the intervention. There was evidence that human albumin reduces the risk of moderate and severe OHSS from a range of 12% to 20% down to a range of 6% to 12%. However, there was also evidence that it may reduce pregnancy rates from a range of 35% to 45% down to a range of 25% to 35%. There was strong evidence that HES reduces the risk of moderate and severe OHSS from a range of 15% to 30% down to a range of 5% to 10%. There was no evidence that it affects pregnancy rates. There was weak evidence that mannitol reduces OHSS from a range of 45% to 60% down to a range of 20% to 35%. There was no evidence that it affects pregnancy rates. None of the studies reported live births. Adverse events appeared to be uncommon, but were too poorly reported to reach any firm conclusions. There was insufficient evidence to support routine use of human albumin, HES or mannitol to prevent OHSS in women at high risk. Any large trials would need to be designed so as not to affect the rate of pregnancy.

The review found ten randomised controlled trials, with a total of 484 patients randomised to treatment with either sclerosing agent or a placebo. The agents used were hypertonic saline, sodium tetradecyl sulfate, polidocanol and heparsal (a solution of 20% salt water mixed with heparin). Not enough evidence was found to prove that any of the agents used were better than the others, but all were better than normal saline. Hypertonic saline caused more pain, while heparsal caused less pain than the other agents. There were no major complications in the majority of cases, but there were some minor side effects such as itching, burning and crusting around the injection site, and in rare cases, ulceration around the injection site. More research is needed to determine the best agent and dosage to use.

This review found seven small studies that included 241 patients. We were interested in studies that would guide us in choosing the best treatment for correcting high potassium levels. We were interested in studies that looked at drugs that lower potassium levels quickly, such as insulin, beta-agonists (medications that stimulate the heart), or medications that remove excess potassium from the blood. We also looked for studies that examined the side effects of these treatments and their ability to improve heart function. We found that inhaled salbutamol (a drug that lowers potassium quickly) is safe and effective. However, its effect on the heart remains unknown. We also found that insulin-dextrose is effective at lowering potassium levels rapidly, but its role in treating abnormal heart rhythms caused by high potassium is unclear. We were unable to make any conclusions about the use of other treatments such as calcium salts, potassium binding resins or IV fluids containing bicarbonate or aminophylline. These studies were limited by the fact that none of the medications were compared to a placebo (a 'dummy' medication that looks like the study drug but does not actually contain anything that could affect the level of potassium). In addition, most of the studies were very small, making it less likely that we will see an effect if one exists. More research is needed to determine the best treatment for abnormalities in potassium levels.

We searched the literature up to 25 October 2017. We found 39 relevant studies, enrolling 4216 participants. Four thousand three hundred and ninety-two participants were included in our meta-analyses. Most studies had a parallel-group, randomised controlled trial design. The number of participants enrolled and the duration of the study varied widely. All studies compared antimicrobial lock solutions to standard locking solutions (usually heparin) for CVC used for haemodialysis. We did not include studies that investigated CVC for other applications such as chemotherapy administration. We divided antimicrobial lock solutions into two subgroups: those containing antibiotics and those that did not contain antibiotics but had another type of antimicrobial activity. We found that antibiotic lock solutions and the combination of both types of lock solutions decreased CRI compared to standard lock solutions, whereas non-antibiotic lock solutions reduced CRI only when the CVC was placed under the skin (tunnelled CVC). The effect on thrombosis incidence is uncertain for all antimicrobial lock solutions. Our confidence in the evidence is low or very low; therefore, better-designed studies are needed to confirm the benefits and harms of using antimicrobial lock solutions.

We searched the scientific databases from their inception to 5 January 2015. We included 15 studies with 1098 participants. Six of these studies compared pre-emptive treatment to placebo or to treating the participant only if they developed CMV disease (standard care). Eight studies examined pre-emptive treatment compared to antiviral prophylaxis and one study compared oral to intravenous administration of pre-emptive treatment. We found that pre-emptive treatment was superior to placebo or standard care in reducing the risk of CMV disease. However, we found that there was uncertainty as to whether pre-emptive therapy was better than antiviral prophylaxis to prevent CMV disease. Donor-derived CMV infection was significantly lower with pre-emptive therapy compared to prophylaxis, however there were no differences in all-cause mortality, graft loss, acute rejection and other infections. The quality of the evidence was variable; most studies were small and four used more than two different strategies.

The review authors searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) which compared the effects of sweet potato on blood sugar control in people with diabetes mellitus. Three RCTs were found, all conducted by the same researcher and involving a total of 140 participants. These were compared to placebo (a dummy treatment) for three to five months. The results showed a small but significant improvement in glycaemic control as measured by glycosylated haemoglobin A1c (HbA1C), when taking sweet potato preparation. However, there were several weaknesses in study design and reporting which means that further trials are needed before any recommendations can be made about using sweet potatoes for diabetes mellitus.

The review included 19 studies with 440 people with cystic fibrosis aged between six and 63 years of age. Most studies were very small - ranging from just seven to 65 people. Thirteen studies only looked at the immediate effects of treatment and four studies looked at the long-term effects of treatment (from one to three years). More people preferred the active cycle of breathing technique over autogenic drainage, airway oscillating devices, and high frequency chest compression. However, there was no difference in lung function, sputum output or quality of life when the active cycle of breathing technique was compared to any of these other techniques. When the active cycle of breathing technique was combined with standard chest physiotherapy, there was no difference in lung function and number of pulmonary exacerbations. Overall, there was not enough evidence to say if the active cycle of breathing technique was better than any other airway clearance technique. We recommend that future studies measure the long-term effects of this treatment and compare it to other treatments.

The main outcome measures were ongoing pregnancy rate (the number of pregnancies that are still present at 12 weeks' gestation), clinical pregnancy rate (the presence of a fetus with a heartbeat on ultrasound), and miscarriage rate (miscarriage before 24 weeks of gestation). We also recorded the mean age of the women and the duration of infertility. This review found eight randomised controlled trials comparing brief co-incubation with the standard overnight insemination protocol. There were a total of 733 women who fulfilled the inclusion criteria of this review. The limited data from these trials showed that brief co-incubation of sperm and oocytes improved the ongoing pregnancy and clinical pregnancy rates for infertile women undergoing IVF cycles, but did not affect the miscarriage rate. The overall quality of the evidence was low.
This review of trials found that the use of longer inspiratory times was associated with an increase in air leaks and deaths.

This review examines the effectiveness of task force training for people with schizophrenia or other similar mental illnesses. We found 33 relevant studies with a total of 5110 participants. However, all these studies had methodological problems, which makes their results less reliable. There were also differences in the way that task force training was conducted, making it difficult to compare results across studies. These problems mean that we cannot draw any firm conclusions from this review.

We found 38 trials that tried out different ways to keep people in trials until the end. We grouped these according to six strategies: incentives, communication strategies, new questionnaire format, participant case management, behavioural interventions and methodological interventions. The majority of trials assessed ways to improve completion of questionnaires. Trials were done in many different places and studied a variety of diseases. More people returned questionnaires when they were offered money compared with no offer of money. A GBP20 voucher was better than a GBP10 voucher. We could not tell if shorter questionnaires were better than longer ones or if questions about specific diseases were better than general questions. Recorded delivery of questionnaires seemed better than telephone reminders and a package of letters seems better than standard procedures. An open trial design seemed better than a blind trial design for one fracture prevention trial. There was no clear evidence that adding a non-monetary incentive, an offer of a non-monetary incentive, 'enhanced' letters, letters delivered by priority post, additional reminders or changes to questionnaire order either increased or decreased trial questionnaire response. There was also no evidence that a telephone survey was either more or less effective than a monetary incentive and a questionnaire. What we cannot tell from the trials we have found is whether offering charity donations, sending reminders to trial sites and when a questionnaire is sent helps keep people in trials. Case management and behavioural strategies used for trial retention may also warrant further evaluation. Most of the trials we found tried to keep people in trials until the end by asking them questions by post or electronically. Few trials kept people coming back to the trial site for trial follow-up.

We found eight randomized controlled trials that compared the two types of airways. These studies involved 829 adults who were undergoing general anaesthesia and needed assisted ventilation. Both men and women were included, as were people of all ages. However, the majority of participants had planned surgery on their abdomen, and one study involved children aged from one month to 10 years. We do not think that the results would differ for adults only. The evidence is current to September 2013. Most studies were of high quality, although one did not state how it was funded and one was funded by a pharmaceutical company. Key results We found no clear difference between the two airways in terms of the number of people who experienced poor ventilation. This is because there were no reports of inadequate ventilation and only four reports of inadequate oxygenation. Therefore, we are uncertain about the effects of either of the airways in terms of oxygenation and ventilation. The ProSeal LMA made a better seal than the Classic LMA, which may mean that it is more suitable for assisted ventilation. The Classic LMA took approximately 10 seconds less to insert than the ProSeal LMA, but this small difference is unlikely to be important. There was no difference in any complication that resulted in harm to the participant. Quality of the evidence Evidence was of low quality. The main reasons for this were that some aspects of the studies could not be hidden from the person inserting the airway and there were not enough data to be sure whether the results were accurate.

This review is current to May 2018. We found 19 randomised controlled trials (clinical studies where people are randomly put into one of two or more treatment groups) of children aged between four and 18 years from eight different countries. Fifteen of these studies were new since the previous version of this review. The average age of the children at the time of recruitment was between 6.3 and 13.1 years, and most were girls. Most studies recruited children with abdominal pain and changes in bowel movements, but five studies specifically looked at children with irritable bowel syndrome. The studies tested the effects of probiotics, fibre supplements, and reducing the amount of fermentable carbohydrate intake for up to six months. We found that probiotics may help to reduce pain and increase the likelihood of improvement in pain, while fibre supplements did not. However, there were several issues with the evidence for both probiotics and fibre which means that we cannot be completely sure of our results. There was not enough evidence to draw conclusions about the effects on other aspects of the children's lives, such as their school performance, social or psychological functioning, or quality of daily life. None of the studies reported any serious side effects. Therefore, probiotics may be a useful add-on treatment for some children with RAP, but more research is needed to be sure that they work and to establish whether they are safe in the long term. It is not clear if fibre supplements are helpful, and more research is needed before we can recommend them.

We searched for relevant studies in January 2015. We found 22 studies with almost 5000 participants. All were randomised trials, and 19 of the 22 studies had useable results for the review. Five studies tested whether the interventions reduced discrimination, 13 tested whether they reduced prejudice and 11 tested both. Only half of the studies tested an intervention that was primarily aimed at reducing discrimination rather than prejudice. The studies were very different, in terms of the groups they focused on (13 with the general public; seven with specific groups such as healthcare professionals or employers; two with young people), the type of mass media used and the duration of the intervention. The evidence suggests that mass media interventions may be effective in reducing prejudice, but we could not find evidence that they reduced discrimination. There was very little information about other outcomes, including costs and adverse effects. Overall, the evidence was low- to very low-quality. We recommend that future research should aim to improve the quality of evidence by using better methods, reporting data more consistently and being less likely to recruit students as participants. Future research should also seek to establish which types of mass media are most effective, and should aim to fill gaps in the evidence, including lack of evidence about mass media other than films and television programmes.

We included 85 studies in this review. These explored the views and experiences of 12,915 women and 1,799 healthcare providers in 41 countries. Of these: • 46 studies explored the views and experiences of healthy pregnant or postnatal women; • 17 studies explored the views and experiences of healthcare providers; and • 22 studies explored both the views of women and the views of healthcare providers.We found 52 findings in total and grouped them into three main themes: • how the local context affects use of and provision of ANC (11 findings); • how ANC services are designed and delivered (24 findings); and • what matters to women and staff (17 findings). What we foundThe findings can be used to explain why some women do and do not use ANC, and why some healthcare providers are able to provide the kind of care that might encourage women to do so. Overall, our findings suggest that whether or not women use ANC depends on their personal circumstances, as well as on how they perceive their pregnancy and the local ANC services available to them. Our findings also suggest that the way in which ANC is experienced by women depends on the quality of the care they receive, which in turn depends on the resources available to the healthcare providers who deliver the care. What we found can be summarised in two lines of argument: For women: women's initial or continued use of ANC depends on a perception that doing so will be a positive experience. This is a result of the provision of good-quality local services that are not dependent on the payment of informal fees and that include continuity of care that is authentically personalised, kind, caring, supportive, culturally sensitive, flexible, and respectful of women’s need for privacy, and that allow staff to take the time needed to provide relevant support, information and clinical safety for the woman and the baby, as and when they need it. For healthcare providers: the capacity of healthcare providers to deliver the kind of high-quality, relationship-based, locally accessible ANC that is likely to facilitate access by women depends on the provision of sufficient resources and staffing as well as the time to provide flexible personalised, private appointments that are not overloaded with organisational tasks. Such provision also depends on organisational norms and values that overtly value kind, caring staff who make effective, culturally-appropriate links with local communities, who respect women’s belief that pregnancy is usually a normal life event, but who can recognise and respond to complications when they arise. Healthcare providers also need sufficient training and education to do their job well, as well as an adequate salary, so that they do not need to demand extra informal funds from women and families, to supplement their income, or to fund essential supplies.

The review found 16 trials, involving a total of 3361 participants, which investigated the effect of calcium antagonists on outcome after SAH.
