Nimodipine did not cause any serious side effects and reduced the chance of a poor outcome; the effect of other calcium antagonists could not be estimated because there were too few trials. However, calcium antagonists did not always show a beneficial effect and some even seemed to do harm. Another drug, magnesium sulphate, showed promise as an additional treatment with nimodipine but more research is needed.

We searched several important medical databases such as CENTRAL, MEDLINE and EMBASE until May 2012. We found three randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with 739 eligible patients. All RCTs identified used an age of one year as the cut-off point for pre-treatment risk stratification. Two RCTs compared myeloablative therapy followed by autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation to conventional chemotherapy, and one RCT compared myeloablative therapy followed by autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation to no further treatment. Myeloablative therapy with or without autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation gives a better event-free survival and overall survival than conventional chemotherapy or no further treatment. However, the impact on event-free survival is smaller than on overall survival. The toxic effects observed were mostly haematological, endocrine and gastrointestinal. The incidence of serious infections was similar in both treatment arms. Renal effects, interstitial pneumonitis and veno-occlusive disease seemed to occur more frequently after myeloablative therapy with or without autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation, but the majority of cases were manageable and reversible. No information on quality of life was reported. Overall, the evidence for myeloablative therapy with or without autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation in newly diagnosed high-risk neuroblastoma is promising. However, further studies are needed to identify the most optimal treatment strategy. The inclusion of patients with intermediate-risk disease in the high-risk groups in the past may have resulted in an overestimation of the impact on event-free survival and overall survival.

We searched the major databases for randomised clinical trials (where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) of adults (aged 18 years or older) with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma. The evidence is current to September 2018. We found eight randomised clinical trials (632 participants). The average age ranged from 16 years to 78 years. The percentage of men ranged from 60% to 75% and the percentage of people with advanced cancer (stage III) ranged from 22% to 85%. Participants were followed up for an average of one year (two months to three years). Most of the trials had problems in their methods, which makes us very uncertain about the reliability of their results. We found that TACE followed by 3-DCRT may lead to better survival and more tumour shrinkage after treatment than TACE alone. One study suggested that quality of life was better in the TACE followed by 3-DCRT group, but the information provided was not sufficient to make any conclusions. No trials reported whether participants died as a result of complications from treatment. Some trial results on side effects were not consistent between the trials and we could not draw definite conclusions. Overall, we found that TACE followed by 3-DCRT may improve survival and tumour response rates, but at the cost of increased toxicity. The overall certainty of the evidence was low to very low and should be interpreted with caution. Future clinical trials with better methodology and reporting are needed to confirm whether the addition of radiotherapy to TACE is beneficial to people with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma.

To date, no randomised controlled trials have been conducted to determine if an increase in energy intake compared with standard energy intake is beneficial for improving the clinical course of BPD in preterm infants. Future studies should aim to determine if increasing energy intake compared with standard energy intake reduces death, improves lung function, promotes normal growth and development, and has any side effects that are harmful to the child.

Thirteen studies with 2341 participants (involving 2360 procedures) were included in this review. The majority of these studies evaluated subclavian vein cannulation (nine studies, 2030 participants, 2049 procedures); the remaining four studies evaluated femoral vein cannulation (four studies, 311 participants, 311 procedures). Two-dimensional ultrasound use reduced the risk of inadvertently hitting the artery (by three percentage points) and the risk of developing a haematoma (by 74 percentage points) at the subclavian site. No clear evidence of a difference was seen in total or other complications (together, accidental arterial puncture and haematoma formation), overall (together, accidental arterial puncture, haematoma formation, total complications and number of attempts until success), number of attempts until success or time taken to insert the catheter. For the femoral vein, no evidence of a difference was found in accidental arterial puncture or other complications (together). However, ultrasound use resulted in better success on the first attempt (18% absolute improvement) and a small increase in overall success. No data on mortality or participant-reported outcomes were available. The quality of evidence was very low for most outcomes. In summary, on the basis of the available data, we conclude that two-dimensional ultrasound provides small gains in safety (subclavian vein: accidental arterial puncture, haematoma formation) and quality (femoral vein: success on the first attempt, overall success) when compared with an anatomical landmark technique for central vein cannulation. Data on insertion by inexperienced or experienced users, or on patients at high risk for complications, are lacking. The results for Doppler ultrasound techniques versus anatomical landmark techniques are uncertain.

This review looked at the effect of physostigmine in patients with Alzheimer's disease. Fifteen studies were found which used four different methods of giving physostigmine. These were intravenous infusion, conventional tablets, controlled release tablets and a physostigmine skin patch. Intravenous infusion  No usable results could be extracted from the trials using this mode of administration. Oral form  The results from trials using the conventional form of the drug showed no benefit of physostigmine compared with placebo.  Controlled release  The results from two of the four studies of the controlled-release physostigmine apply only to a group of patients identified as responders in a pre-randomization titration period. The best dose physostigmine was associated with improvement on the ADAS-Cog score compared with placebo at 6, 12 weeks. However, there were statistically significantly more patients from the physostigmine group suffering from adverse effects such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, anorexia, dizziness, stomach pain, flatulence or sweating at 6 weeks, and at 12 weeks. When all patients were randomized, regardless of their response to titration, fixed dose physostigmine (mean 33 mg/day) was associated with a statistically significantly higher number of patients withdrawing, withdrawing due to adverse events and suffering adverse events such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, anorexia, dizziness, stomach pain, dyspepsia, sweating, asthenia, dyspnoea or abnormal dreaming, but with no benefit on cognition compared with placebo at 24 weeks. Verum patch  The double dose (deliverying mean dose 12 mg/day) was associated with statistically significantly higher numbers suffering vomiting, nausea, or abdominal cramps, and the lower dose (delivering mean dose 5.7mg/day) was associated with statistically significantly higher numbers suffering gastrointestinal complaints compared with placebo at 24 weeks. There was no difference between physostigmine (higher and lower dose) and placebo for numbers improved (Clinical Global Impression of Change) at 24 weeks. The evidence of effectiveness of physostigmine for the symptomatic treatment of Alzheimer's disease is limited. Even in a controlled release formulation designed to overcome the short half-life, physostigmine showed no convincing benefit and side effects remained common leading to a high rate of withdrawal.

The evidence is current to July 2015. We found 13 trials including a total of 16,112 participants living in Europe and North America with CVD who received either a fibrate or placebo for at least five years. On average, the participants were aged between 64 and 74 years and 73% were men. Three studies did not report sources of funding. One study was funded by a pharmaceutical company, four studies received government funding, and four studies received both government and pharmaceutical company funding. Two studies did not report funding sources. The last study reported receiving no funding. Four studies reported that there were conflicts of interest. The review showed that fibrates are moderately effective in reducing the combined outcome of non-fatal stroke, heart attack, and death due to vascular diseases. This effect was evident when we included all types of fibrates, but not when we excluded clofibrate as these trials have now been discontinued due to safety concerns. Furthermore, when we looked at each type of outcome separately, we found that although fibrates were not effective in reducing all-cause deaths, death due to vascular diseases, or stroke, they were effective in reducing heart attacks. There was little or no difference in the number of adverse events in the groups receiving fibrates compared to those receiving placebo. When we performed analyses stratifying by age, gender, and diabetes mellitus status, we found that the beneficial effect of fibrates was similar in both men and women, and in people with and without diabetes mellitus. Overall, we judged the quality of evidence to be moderate.

The authors searched the literature up to 30 November 2014. We found 12 randomised controlled studies involving 3285 women. In all cases, women received routine uterotonic medications as recommended by the institution where the study took place. We did not pool the results from the different studies as we considered the overall quality of the studies to be low to moderate. However, we were able to pool the results from six of the 12 studies which showed that total blood loss was significantly less with the use of tranexamic acid. This was the case in both women having a caesarean section and those having a vaginal birth. The risk of having a blood loss of more than 1000 mL was significantly lower in women who had a caesarean section and received tranexamic acid (43% compared with 19% in the control group) but not in women having a vaginal birth. The mean blood loss from the time of delivery until two hours postpartum was lower in women taking tranexamic acid. Additional medical interventions and blood transfusions were less common in women receiving tranexamic acid. Side effects such as nausea, vomiting and dizziness were reported more frequently in the group of women who received tranexamic acid. The effect of tranexamic acid on death, severe complications and problems with blood clots is unclear. Tranexamic acid may decrease blood loss and prevent excessive bleeding after both caesarean sections and vaginal births in women who are not considered to be at risk of excessive bleeding. However, since the majority of the studies took place in developed countries, its effect in developing countries should be evaluated.

The evidence is current to April 2013. We included 12 studies with 2494 participants. We identified no completed studies in adults. In children, we found that amantadine was effective in preventing influenza A, but the quality of the evidence was low and the safety of the drug was not well established. One small study showed that rimantadine was beneficial in reducing temperature by day three of treatment, but there was no significant difference in other symptoms. In elderly people, neither amantadine nor rimantadine were beneficial in preventing or treating influenza A. There were no reports of side effects due to treatment with amantadine or rimantadine. Given the low to very low level of the methodological quality of the included studies and the limited benefits observed, amantadine and rimantadine do not seem useful in preventing, treating and shortening the duration of influenza A in children and the elderly.

We found nine randomised controlled trials comparing endometrial injury to no injury or a sham procedure. They included a total of 1512 women with unexplained infertility who were undergoing intrauterine insemination (IUI) or trying to conceive via sexual intercourse. Of the nine trials, eight compared endometrial injury versus no intervention or a sham procedure and one compared two different methods of endometrial injury. The evidence is current to April 2015. We are uncertain whether endometrial injury improves live birth/ongoing pregnancy rates as the quality of the evidence has been assessed as very low. When we restricted the analysis to only studies at low risk of bias the effect was imprecise and the evidence remained of very low quality. Endometrial injury may improve clinical pregnancy rates however the evidence is of low quality. No studies reported any complications related to the procedure other than mild pain in one study. The average pain experienced by participants undergoing endometrial injury was 6 out of 10 on a zero-10 scale. Only one study reported this outcome. We are uncertain whether timing of endometrial injury affects the chances of pregnancy as the quality of the evidence has been assessed as very low. There was no evidence of a difference in ongoing pregnancy rate or clinical pregnancy rate when endometrial injury was performed in the cycle prior to IUI compared to the same cycle as the IUI. Neither of these studies reported the primary outcome of pain during the procedure. In all three comparisons there was no evidence of an effect on miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy or multiple pregnancy. No studies reported bleeding secondary to the procedure. The quality of the evidence for all outcomes was low or very low. The main reasons for this were poor reporting of study methods and a lack of precision in the results.

We searched for relevant studies in March 2016. We found four studies that met our inclusion criteria. They all examined professional oral care versus usual oral care. One study was stopped early because it found no clear differences between professional oral care and usual oral care. One study each of 141 and 507 participants reported on death due to pneumonia and all-cause death, respectively. The remaining two studies reported on nursing home-acquired pneumonia. We found no studies that investigated professional oral care versus no oral care. We found low-quality evidence that professional oral care may reduce the risk of dying as a result of lung infection in nursing home residents when compared to usual care. However, we found no clear evidence regarding any other benefits or harms of professional oral care. There were some potential problems with how the studies were carried out. Overall, there is a need for further research to determine the best way to care for people's teeth and mouths in nursing homes in order to prevent lung infections and improve their overall health.

The researchers identified five studies which recruited a total of 149 patients. These randomised patients to receive either active treatment or a placebo (fake medicine). Four of these studies used placebos which looked like the active treatment. The medications used to treat patients with lymphocytic colitis included bismuth subsalicylate, budesonide, mesalazine and beclometasone dipropionate. Budesonide was more effective than placebo in inducing remission. Clinical remission was induced in 9 out of 10 patients given budesonide and in 4 out of 10 patients given placebo. Mesalazine with or without cholestyramine and beclometasone dipropionate resulted in remission in the majority of patients who received them, but not significantly more than the number who received placebo. Side effects associated with the use of budesonide included nausea, vomiting, neck pain, abdominal pain, excessive sweating and headache. Nausea and skin rash were reported in the mesalazine study. Side effects associated with the use of beclometasone dipropionate included nausea, sleepiness and change of mood. No side effects were reported in the bismuth subsalicylate study. The study which assessed mesalazine versus mesalazine plus cholestyramine and the study which assessed beclometasone dipropionate versus mesalazine were judged to be at high risk of bias due to lack of blinding. The study which compared bismuth subsalicylate versus placebo was judged as low quality due to the very small number of patients included and limited data. The other three studies were judged to be at low risk of bias. Overall, there is some evidence that budesonide, mesalazine with or without cholestyramine and beclometasone dipropionate may all be effective treatments for lymphocytic colitis. However, confirmation of this effect will only come from larger studies.

In this review, we have reviewed the literature on the effects of self-monitoring of blood glucose on glycaemic control, well-being and health-related quality of life in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus. We found twelve randomised controlled trials which fulfilled our inclusion criteria. We found that self-monitoring of blood glucose had a small beneficial effect on glycaemic control in the first year after initiation, but that this effect subsided after 12 months. We also found that self-monitoring did not affect patient satisfaction, general well-being or general health-related quality of life. There was little information on the impact of self-monitoring on diabetic complications or on costs. Future research should aim to identify which patients benefit from self-monitoring of blood glucose and under what conditions.

This review investigated the effectiveness of psychological treatments for adults with antisocial personality disorder (AsPD). We found 11 relevant randomised controlled trials, but none of these were designed specifically for people with AsPD; all had been conducted for substance misuse problems. Only three interventions - contingency management, cognitive behaviour therapy and the 'Driving Whilst Intoxicated program' - showed some positive effects. However, for each of these, the main effects were on substance misuse rather than on aggression, reconviction or social functioning. Overall, there is no clear evidence that psychological treatments are helpful for people with AsPD.

The evidence is current to April 2015. We found 11 studies. Six studies involved participants with an upper airway infection with runny nose, sore throat, stuffy nose, sneezing, cough and sometimes a fever (the common cold) and five studies involved an infection with pus (purulent rhinitis) with similar symptoms plus headache, facial pain, nasal discharge and a productive cough. Some studies included only children and some only adults; some studies included men only and some included women. The choice of antibiotic differed between studies as did the dose and duration of treatment. In this review participation in both types of study was generally low with around half of the participants reporting at least one adverse effect. The most common adverse effects were nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, headache and dizziness. The evidence shows that antibiotics are ineffective in reducing the severity or duration of the common cold in adults and children. There is also no evidence that they are effective in reducing the chance of a persistent infection with pus (acute purulent rhinitis). There is evidence that antibiotics cause adverse effects in adults when used for the common cold and in people of all ages when used for acute purulent rhinitis. The quality of the evidence for the main outcomes is low to moderate. The numbers of participants in the studies were small, some of the studies reported poorly and some were conducted many years ago.

We found five randomised controlled trials (RCTs), which included 162 people. Four RCTs evaluated oral antibiotics (two with penicillin and two with azithromycin), and one evaluated tonsillectomy (the surgical removal of the tonsils). Two comparisons (erythromycin compared to no treatment, and rifampicin compared to placebo) did not measure any of the outcomes of interest. The other three studies assessed the effects of oral antibiotics on guttate or chronic plaque psoriasis. However, the three studies were very different in terms of the way they were conducted and the way each study measured the outcomes. We therefore judged the quality of the evidence to be very low, meaning that we are very uncertain about the results presented. There is a need for further well-designed clinical trials to assess the benefits and harms of antistreptococcal treatments for guttate and chronic plaque psoriasis, particularly in children and young adults. There was insufficient evidence to determine whether patients who received antibiotics experienced more side effects than those given placebo (fake medicine) or no treatment. It is possible that taking antibiotics may result in side effects such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, headache and rash. Tonsillectomy may result in complications such as bleeding from the nose, throat or stomach, and infection. More research is needed to find effective treatments for psoriasis.

The evidence was current to February 2016. We found 11 studies testing vitamin D₃ and one study testing vitamin D₂. In total, 933 adults with MS participated. Half received vitamin D, and half received placebo. No study assessed vitamin D₂ compared with placebo. Vitamin D₃ had no effect on relapses, brain lesions seen on brain scans, or disability. Some people taking vitamin D reported higher quality of life than those taking placebo, but some studies reported no effect of vitamin D on quality of life. Some studies reported that people taking vitamin D were less tired than those taking placebo, but others found no effect of vitamin D on fatigue. Most people in the studies experienced no side effects, and there were few serious side effects. The overall quality of the evidence was poor. This means we are very uncertain about the results.

This is an update of a previous review published in 2012. We searched the literature in May 2015 and identified seven new studies. We excluded six and one is still ongoing. This review now includes 62 studies with 4241 participants. The population in all the studies was participants with cancer pain. Oral morphine was compared with another preparation in 37 studies. There were 18 studies comparing oral morphine with placebo, and seven studies comparing different routes of administration (how the medicine is given – e.g. mouth, skin patch). The main measure of the effectiveness of a drug was the amount of pain relief it provided. We also looked at whether side effects forced people to stop taking their medication. Side effects were generally mild to moderate in severity, and the most common were constipation, drowsiness, dry mouth, feeling sick and drowsiness. No more than 1 person in 10 stopped taking morphine because of side effects. The quality of the evidence in this review is generally low. Most of the studies were old, and were designed primarily to show that different preparations of morphine were equally effective rather than to find the best preparation. They were also very small, with most having fewer than 100 participants. Even though morphine has been available for many years, there are relatively few studies that have been done to assess the effectiveness of morphine for cancer pain. This review shows that oral morphine is very effective in controlling cancer pain. It is widely used and tolerated, but like all strong painkillers, can cause side effects.

We identified 14 randomised controlled trials with 1260 participants with 1361 trigger fingers. The average age of the participants in the included studies ranged from 16 to 88 years; 70% of them were women. The studies reported nine types of comparisons: open surgery versus steroid injections (two studies); percutaneous surgery versus steroid injection (five studies); open surgery versus steroid injection plus ultrasound-guided hyaluronic acid injection (one study); percutaneous surgery plus steroid injection versus steroid injection (one study); percutaneous surgery versus open surgery (five studies); endoscopic surgery versus open surgery (one study); and three studies comparing types of incision for open surgery. The majority of studies had significant methodological flaws and were considered at high or unclear risk of bias. The primary comparison was open surgery versus steroid injections, because open surgery is the oldest and the most widely used treatment method and is considered as standard surgery, whereas steroid injection is the least invasive control treatment method and is often used as first-line treatment in clinical practice. There was low-quality evidence that open surgery provided better results with less recurrence of trigger finger (at six to 12 months), although it caused more pain in the first week after treatment. It was uncertain whether open surgery was better than steroid injection in improving the resolution of trigger finger, due to few events occurring in the studies. It was also uncertain whether open surgery increased the risk of adverse events (infection, tendon injury, flare, cutaneous discomfort and fat necrosis) and neurovascular injury. None of the studies reported whether participants had improved hand function or higher levels of participant satisfaction. There was no information on complications arising after the procedure. There was insufficient evidence to determine which technique is better for improving the release of the affected finger.

The three relevant trials included in this updated review had a total of 931 participants. All patients had newly diagnosed anaplastic oligodendroglioma. Most were young to middle-aged adults, with a male to female ratio of approximately 1.5:1. All three studies assessed overall survival, but only two assessed progression-free survival. The primary outcome was reported as overall survival in two trials and as progression-free survival in the NOA-04 trial. Overall survival was significantly longer with combined treatment in two trials, with a median overall survival of 3.5 years in the combined therapy group compared with 2.6 years in the radiotherapy alone group in the EORTC trial. This result was not apparent when the original Cochrane review was published in 2008. In addition, we identified a subgroup of patients who have specific genetic features that make them more sensitive to chemotherapy with temozolomide and lomustine and vincristine (PCV). These are patients whose tumour has a deletion of both the short arm of both chromosome 1 and the short arm of chromosome 19 (1p/19q codeletion), and/or a mutation in isocitrate dehydrogenase-1 or -2 (IDH-1 or IDH-2). These factors were associated with improved overall survival in two of the three trials and with progression-free survival in the third. They did not, however, affect the effect of the addition of chemotherapy to radiotherapy on overall survival. There were significant toxic effects associated with the use of lomustine in two trials, mainly low blood counts (anaemia, neutropenia and thrombocytopenia). There were no significant differences in quality of life between the different treatment groups in any of the three trials. We conclude that, although there is evidence that the addition of chemotherapy to radiotherapy may prolong overall survival, it is still not clear which patients will derive most benefit from this approach. Identification of patients with specific genetic features who are likely to be sensitive to chemotherapy with temozolomide and lomustine and vincristine (PCV) is an important step forward, but further studies are required to better understand the role of these drugs in the treatment of anaplastic oligodendrogliomas.

We searched for studies including children with OSA aged 12 years or younger. We found five studies with a total of 240 children with mild to moderate OSA. Not all studies reported on all outcomes. Two studies compared the drug montelukast against a placebo, and three studies compared intranasal steroids against a placebo. The length of treatment was between six weeks and four months. We are uncertain whether the apnoea-hypopnoea index (AHI; number of breathing interruptions per hour of sleep) is different between children receiving intranasal corticosteroids compared to placebo. However, children who received montelukast had a lower AHI than those who received a placebo. We are also uncertain whether the oxygen saturation (level of oxygen in the blood) is different between children receiving intranasal corticosteroids compared to placebo, but children who received montelukast had higher oxygen saturation levels than those who received a placebo. Adverse events were rare and of minor nature (e.g. nasal bleeding) and were equally distributed between the different treatment groups. No study investigated whether the number of children who avoided surgery for OSA was different. Overall, the evidence for the use of intranasal corticosteroids as treatment for OSA in children is not strong, while there is moderate- to high-certainty evidence for the efficacy of montelukast. However, it remains unclear if the observed treatment effects are important for the individual child. Future studies should investigate how long the treatment effects last and whether there are any long-term side effects. They should also assess whether children with OSA do better in school, have improved concentration, and have fewer accidents.

We searched several important medical databases such as CENTRAL, MEDLINE, EMBASE and LILACS, conference proceedings and trial registers until May 2013. We found six randomised controlled trials (studies in which participants are assigned to one of two or more treatment groups using a random method) and four cohort studies (studies in which a group of people who have had similar characteristics at the start of the study are followed over time, to examine different treatments received and subsequent outcomes). We included 13 studies with 1291 children with cancer aged under 19 years old who had a CVC and were treated in hospitals. Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), antithrombin (AT) supplementation, low-dose warfarin, cryoprecipitate and/or fresh frozen plasma (FFP) supplementation were given to 491 children, and 800 children did not receive any medication. In five studies, participants were assigned to receive a systemic treatment or no intervention, and in one study, participants were assigned to receive two different treatments. The age range of the participants was from three months up to 18 years. We found no significant effects of systemic treatments compared with no intervention in preventing (a)symptomatic VTE. One study showed that the combination of LMWH and AT was superior to AT alone in reducing the occurrence of symptomatic VTE. All studies assessed the occurrence of major and/or minor bleeding, and none found a significant difference between study groups. None of the studies reported thrombocytopenia, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT), heparin-induced thrombocytopenia with thrombosis (HITT), death as a result of VTE, removal of CVC due to VTE, CVC-related infection or post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS). Our results were limited by the small number of participants, especially in the studies that looked at the effect of systemic treatments. Given the small number of participants, there was a very low chance of finding a true effect if one existed.

The review authors searched the literature and identified three randomised controlled trials with a total of 287 participants. All three trials studied participants who had thoracic or thoracoabdominal aneurysms. The first two trials studied the use of CSFD during the operation and the third studied the use of CSFD for 48 hours after the operation. The first trial found no benefit from using CSFD. The second trial found a benefit with combined use of CSFD and intrathecal papaverine (a potent vasodilator) but this study was stopped early as more participants than planned had completed the trial. The third trial demonstrated a benefit from CSFD. The results of this trial would be applicable only to operations performed by the same team of surgeons. The use of CSFD may be beneficial in preventing paralysis after aneurysm surgery but more studies are required to be sure of this.

We included 13 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with a total of 662 participants in this review. Seven RCTs compared NB-UVB with oral PUVA, four RCTs compared NB-UVB with bath PUVA, and two RCTs compared NB-UVB with topical PUVA. One RCT compared NB-UVB with selective BB-UVB. Oral PUVA was clearly superior to NB-UVB in achieving clearance in three RCTs of participants with CPP, but not in an RCT of participants with PPP. This means that more people who received oral PUVA achieved clearness of their psoriasis than those who received NB-UVB. However, this result should be interpreted cautiously because of the risk of overestimating the true effect. On the other hand, there was no significant difference between NB-UVB and bath PUVA in an RCT of participants with CPP and in an RCT of participants with GP. In an RCT of participants with PPP, there was no significant difference between the NB-UVB and topical PUVA group in terms of clearance rate. Re-NB-UVB and re-PUVA were similarly effective for treating participants with CPP or GP. There was no significant difference in withdrawals due to adverse events between the NB-UVB and selective BB-UVB groups in an RCT of participants with CPP. No studies reported on our primary outcomes for NB-UVB compared with conventional BB-UVB. Currently, there is no high-quality evidence to suggest that NB-UVB is better than any other phototherapy. However, NB-UVB might be more convenient to use than oral or bath PUVA, since no drug needs to be taken orally or added to the water.

This review aimed to assess the effectiveness of biofeedback and anal sphincter exercises for the treatment of faecal incontinence. Twenty one eligible studies were identified with a total of 1525 participants. There was not enough evidence as to whether there was a difference in outcome between any method of biofeedback or exercises. There is some evidence that biofeedback and electrical stimulation may enhance the outcome of treatment compared to electrical stimulation alone or exercises alone. Exercises appear to be less effective than an implanted sacral nerve stimulator. While there is a suggestion that some elements of biofeedback therapy and sphincter exercises may have a therapeutic effect, this is not certain. Larger well-designed trials are needed to enable safe conclusions.

This review found 39 relevant trials but they were generally small and of mixed quality. Whether a catheter was used at all, whether it was attached to a suprapubic tap or left in via the urethra, and for how long, all seemed to have implications for the chance of subsequent urinary retention and infection. However, the results were not conclusive as so many different approaches were studied in such a small number of patients.

The Cochrane Schizophrenia Group carried out this review of the effects of aripiprazole in mania, which includes the effects of aripiprazole on causing harm as a side effect. Ten studies with 3340 participants were included in the review. The participants were aged between 6 and 77 years old. All studies randomised participants to receive either aripiprazole or placebo or another drug. Participants received aripiprazole for a period of 3 to 16 weeks. The review found that aripiprazole was better than placebo in reducing manic symptoms, but that it did cause some side effects such as nausea, vomiting and constipation. When compared with other drugs, aripiprazole was similar to lithium and haloperidol in reducing manic symptoms, but caused more side effects. More people receiving aripiprazole experienced movement disorders than those taking placebo, and more people taking haloperidol experienced movement disorders than those taking aripiprazole. For participants taking aripiprazole, the number experiencing adverse events was higher than for those taking placebo, and similar to those taking lithium. Quality of the evidence Overall, the evidence for the effectiveness of aripiprazole was moderate, meaning that further research is likely to change our confidence in the result. The evidence for the harms caused by aripiprazole was high, meaning that we are very confident in the result.

We searched several important medical databases such as CENTRAL, MEDLINE and EMBASE for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and controlled clinical trials (CCTs) comparing different types of lock treatments with each other or with no lock treatment in children with cancer who had a CVC and developed a CVC-related infection. We also looked at non-randomised studies such as cohort studies. We found four cohort studies and five cohort studies including a total of 126 children with cancer who had a CVC and developed a CVC-related infection. One study was published in two parts. The median age of the children was 6.7 years and 62% were boys. The most common diagnosis was leukaemia. The studies assessed different lock treatments; two studies used urokinase lock treatment, one study used ethanol lock treatment and two studies used a combination of urokinase and ethanol lock treatments. Two studies compared urokinase lock treatment with and without antibiotics and one study compared ethanol lock treatment with antibiotics alone. The duration of the treatment lasted from 10 days to six months. There were no significant differences in the number of children cured, the number of CVC-related infections, the number of days until the first negative blood culture, the number of CVCs removed prematurely, the occurrence of sepsis or the occurrence of adverse events (except for one study which did not report on adverse events) between the different groups. In three of the five publications of cohort studies assessing this outcome, the CVC malfunctioned after lock treatment. No RCTs or CCTs evaluating antibiotic lock treatment were identified. The quality of the studies was low and all studies had some limitations.

We found 15 randomised controlled trials (a type of scientific experiment in which people are randomly allocated to one or more treatment groups) that examined the effects of providing educational interventions to patients with acute whiplash-associated disorders. The studies were limited by their small number of participants, some of whom dropped out of the studies. The evidence is current to April 2012. We grouped the included studies according to the type of advice given: (1) activation - advice to 'get active' and return to normal activities; (2) pain and stress coping skills - advice to cope with pain and stress; and (3) self-care strategies - advice to manage symptoms and promote recovery. For each group we combined the results of similar studies together and rated the quality of the evidence as high, medium, low, or very low. We found that an educational video advising patients to 'get active' and return to normal activities may help to reduce pain in the short term but there was no difference between groups at longer term. A pamphlet advising patients on'self care' did not show any difference in pain or function with usual care at short- or intermediate-term follow-up. The various other treatments showed no benefit or were no better than other treatments. Therefore, the overall effectiveness of educational interventions for acute whiplash-associated disorders is poor.

We searched for studies evaluating ways of informing and supporting people at risk of vCJD or CJD, but we did not find any studies that met our criteria. We then looked at other published information and found 49 studies and pieces of literature that met our population, intervention and outcome criteria, although they were not suitable for the Cochrane review. We analysed these using a method called thematic synthesis. Our findings suggest that good communication with people at risk might include: • co-ordinating information and support over a long period of time • tailoring the information and support to the needs of the person • making sure that people at risk have equal access to healthcare services and support. Our findings also suggest that bad communication with people at risk could lead to problems, such as discrimination when trying to access healthcare services. Overall, there is not enough evidence to know what the best way is to communicate with people at risk of CJD or vCJD, so our findings from the thematic synthesis are used to make recommendations for future research and to inform policy and practice decisions.

The search was updated to April 2015. We found one study with 276 participants who received either an opioid (oxycodone prolonged release) or a placebo. The duration of the trial was 12 weeks. The treatment reduced RLS symptoms more in the opioid group than in the placebo group. More patients in the opioid group than in the placebo group reported a decrease of at least 50% of their RLS symptoms, and more achieved complete remission. Quality of life and sleep also improved more in the opioid group than in the placebo group. However, there were more side effects in the opioid group than in the placebo group, mainly nausea, vomiting, constipation, dizziness, and dry mouth. There was no difference in morning drowsiness, trouble staying awake during the day, or naps during the day. The main limitation of this review was that there was only one small study with a high dropout rate.

The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 2013 and identified 15 relevant studies. These studies were generally of reasonable methodological quality, although there were some weaknesses. The studies showed that external fixation maintains the fractured bone in place better than plaster casting, and that it is less likely to end in a poor position (malunion). However, the external fixator needs to be attached to the bone using pins inserted through the skin into the bones of the forearm. This can cause complications, particularly pin-site infections. In about one in five people treated with an external fixator, at least one of these complications occurred. However, most of these were relatively minor and did not affect the final result. There was no clear difference in the numbers of people who developed what are known as 'complications of poor blood flow' (e.g. coldness, tingling, pain, colour change) after having an external fixator or a plaster cast. These were usually temporary and self-limiting. Based on the available evidence, the review concluded that external fixation is more reliable in maintaining the fractured bone in place, and should be considered for people with difficult or potentially unstable fractures.

We searched the literature up to 22 September 2016 and identified five studies (reported on 1127 participants) which met the inclusion criteria of the review. These five studies compared early (less than 30 days post-transplant) with late (more than 30 days post-transplant) removal of ureteric stents. They all looked at the incidence of urological complications (MUC) and urinary tract infections (UTI) as well as other factors such as length of hospital stay and cost. We found that the incidence of UTIs may be reduced by removing the stent earlier (i.e. within 30 days of surgery), but this was only evident when a percutaneous nephrostomy stent (inserted through the skin into the kidney) was used and not when a ureteric stent was inserted from inside the bladder to the kidney. However, we found no evidence that the overall incidence of MUC was affected by the timing of stent removal. There was some evidence that stents removed earlier resulted in a shorter hospital stay, but there was insufficient data regarding any effect on the donor kidney function or overall survival.

This review of existing studies was carried out by the Cochrane Oral Health Group, and the evidence is current up to 19 November 2015. We found five studies that fitted children with either crowns or fillings and assessed how the treatments performed over time. Three studies compared crowns with fillings and two of these used the Hall Technique to fit the crowns, whilst the other two used the conventional method. One of the studies with crowns fitted using the Hall Technique also compared the treatment to fillings but without using the Hall Technique. A further study compared two types of crowns: metal crowns versus aesthetic stainless steel crowns with white veneers. None of the studies considered what children and parents preferred. We found some evidence that crowns were better than fillings in reducing the risk of the tooth eventually needing further treatment or falling out (known as major failure). This was true whether the crown was fitted using the conventional method or the Hall Technique. However, this result should be interpreted with caution due to issues with the way in which the studies were conducted. We found some evidence that crowns were better than fillings in reducing the risk of pain in the long term. We also found that children experienced less discomfort when crowns were fitted using the Hall Technique than when fillings were used. We are very uncertain about whether there is a difference in the risk of bleeding around the crown when using crowns instead of fillings. There is no evidence comparing the Hall Technique with the conventional method for fitting crowns. There is very little evidence for the comparison between crowns and non-restorative treatment and no evidence for the comparison between different types of crowns. The evidence presented here is mostly of moderate quality. This means we are reasonably confident in the results but there are some concerns about the design of some of the studies.

We looked at the research published up to 12 November 2016. We found 28 studies including 788 children and adults with cystic fibrosis. The studies lasted for a maximum of three months, with 10 studies lasting longer than one month. We did not include six studies where the participants were younger than 18 years of age and four studies where the participants were already receiving ventilation through a machine (mechanical ventilation). The studies compared PEP to other techniques such as autogenic drainage (where patients clear mucus from their lungs by taking slow breaths out and in through a mask), high-frequency chest wall oscillation (HFCWO), vest therapy (which uses vibrating pads placed on the back of the body to help loosen mucus), combined PEP and exercise, and combined PEP and autogenic drainage. We could not combine many of the studies in the review because the studies used different types of PEP (with or without a mask, or with different frequency settings) and different comparison techniques. The studies used different ways to measure how well the lungs were functioning, and reported on different outcomes in different ways, making comparisons difficult. Overall, PEP was better than vest therapy at reducing the likelihood of a flare-up of disease (respiratory exacerbation); there were fewer exacerbations over a one-year period in those using PEP with a mask compared to those using vest therapy. In those using PEP with a mouthpiece, there were no differences in the number of exacerbations. Patient preference was reported in 10 studies and in all of these studies with an intervention period of at least one month, this was in favour of PEP. Other outcomes were not reported in enough detail to be analysed. Few studies reported on side effects and those that did were generally of low certainty. We do not think that any of the treatments are superior to each other for short-term use (up to three months). For long-term use, the evidence is less clear. Future trials should measure the amount of mucus cleared from the lungs, and should measure the impact of each treatment on lung function, quality of life, and individual preference. They should also measure any side effects associated with each treatment and report these clearly. We assessed the overall quality of the evidence to be low to moderate for most outcomes. This was due to issues with the way in which the studies were conducted, and the fact that the results were not similar across studies.

We found four studies that included a total of 1485 adult participants with moderately to severely active Crohn's disease. Participants were randomly assigned to receive certolizumab pegol (CZP) or a placebo (a fake medication). Certolizumab pegol is an anti-TNF medicine that is administered by injection. The studies showed that after eight weeks of treatment with CZP, 27% of participants achieved remission compared to 20% of those who received a placebo. Additionally, 40% of participants receiving CZP experienced an improvement in their symptoms compared to 31% of people receiving a placebo. Side effects were similar in both groups, but this could not be determined with certainty. We judged the overall quality of evidence to be moderate, meaning that our confidence in the results is limited. This review does not apply to children with Crohn's disease.

We found 112 studies that assessed the effects of drug treatments in people with heart failure and impaired kidney function. Fifty-six of these studies included only individuals with heart failure and impaired kidney function. The remaining 56 studies included individuals with heart failure, but we could not extract information on the individuals with heart failure and impaired kidney function. The number of participants ranged from 16 to 2916 and follow-up lasted between three months and five years. We did not pool any of the data. In people with heart failure and impaired kidney function, the effects of drugs used to treat heart failure such as adenosine A1-receptor antagonists, dopamine, nesiritide, or serelaxin were uncertain because of a lack of reliable evidence. In people with chronic heart failure, we found that angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors or angiotensin receptor blockers, aldosterone antagonists, or vasopressin receptor antagonists had uncertain effects on death (any cause), hospitalisation for heart failure, worsening heart failure or kidney function, high potassium levels, low blood pressure or quality of life. Treatment with beta-blockers may reduce the risk of death (any cause) and hospitalisation for heart failure. Aldosterone antagonists may increase the risk of high potassium levels. We found very little reliable evidence on the side effects of treatment. There were problems with the design and reporting of many of the studies, which means that we cannot be sure of the reliability of their results. Overall, we found that there is not enough evidence to say if treatments for heart failure are safe and effective in people with heart failure and impaired kidney function.

This review of three randomised controlled studies including 1945 women, using external or internal monitoring of uterine contractions, found no clear difference in outcomes for mother or baby with either method. There were no deaths in either group. It is not possible to recommend which method is better for monitoring progress in labour when oxytocin is used to induce or augment labour.

The authors of this review evaluated the effect of physical exercise on people with chronic venous insufficiency (CVI). They identified two randomised controlled trials (RCTs) that involved a total of 54 people with CVI. The studies were similar in design; however, they differed in their methods. We could not combine the results from the two studies because the measurements were too different. One study found no difference in improvement in the symptoms of leg pain, oedema and cramping between the exercise and control groups after three months. The other study reported a significant improvement in these aspects with exercise after six weeks. One study reported an increase in some heart function tests in the exercise group compared with the control group at the end of the treatment period. Muscle strength was higher in the exercise group than in the control group at the end of the six-week treatment period. Neither of the included studies reported any adverse events or whether the participants had any venous leg ulcers. The sample size of the included studies was small and there were concerns about how reliable the results were. We therefore considered the overall quality of evidence to be very low. There is currently no strong evidence supporting the use of physical exercise for people with chronic venous insufficiency.

This review summarises the evidence from 76 randomised controlled trials of hyaluronic acid (HA) injections into the joint space (intra-articular or IA) for people with knee osteoarthritis (OA). There are several different HA products available, so we also present separate results for the different products where possible. We compared each HA product against another HA product, a placebo (a substance containing no active ingredient), an active drug or another type of treatment. We categorised the outcomes as pain, function, patient global assessment, physical examination, self-evaluation scales and adverse events. We found that viscosupplements are an effective treatment for knee OA with beneficial effects on pain, function and patient global assessment. These effects were seen at different times depending upon the product but were most evident 5 to 13 weeks after injection. Some products seemed to work better than others but there were relatively few direct head-to-head trials of different HA products so caution is required when making conclusions about the relative value of different products. Overall, the HA class performs well in this setting.

The authors searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs; experiments in which people are randomly allocated to one of two or more treatment groups) that assessed the effect of treatments for women with excessive bleeding after childbirth. The review includes 10 RCTs involving 4052 women. Four RCTs compared misoprostol plus oxytocin versus oxytocin alone; two RCTs compared misoprostol versus oxytocin; one compared giving misoprostol versus oxytocin in women who had also been given oxytocin after birth; one compared giving estrogen versus misoprostol; one compared tranexamic acid versus placebo; and one compared the use of a pessary (a vaginal pessary is a device placed in the vagina to apply pressure to the womb (uterus) to stop the bleeding) versus no intervention. Giving misoprostol in addition to oxytocin was no better than oxytocin alone in reducing maternal deaths, admissions to intensive care units or need for surgery to remove the womb. Misoprostol was associated with an increased risk of vomiting and shivering. In summary, oxytocin is the drug of choice in the management of postpartum haemorrhage, and there is no evidence to support the routine use of misoprostol in addition to oxytocin. The review highlights gaps in knowledge about the management of postpartum haemorrhage and in particular how to manage women who do not respond to initial treatment.

The authors reviewed the effects of cysteine and N-acetylcysteine supplementation of parenterally nutritioned infants, especially preterm infants, with low plasma levels of cysteine. The review found five small trials of cysteine supplementation of cysteine-free PN and one large trial of N-acetylcysteine supplementation of cysteine-containing PN. Routine cysteine supplementation had positive effects on growth and protein anabolism, but there are no data from randomized controlled trials on side effects. N-acetylcysteine supplementation did not have any beneficial effects.

We searched the literature up to 30 November 2016. We found 77 randomised clinical trials (RCTs); 41 RCTs provided information for one or more measures (outcomes). The trials lasted between one month and 24 months. Most of the participants had NAFLD but some had NASH. Half of the trials included only participants with NAFLD and half included participants with NASH. Half of the trials included only participants with type 2 diabetes and half included participants with no diabetes. The source of funding was not provided in 39 trials. Twenty-six trials were partially- or fully-funded by pharmaceutical companies that would benefit, based on the results of the trial. Twelve trials did not receive any additional funding or were funded by parties with no vested interest in the results. The overall quality of evidence was very low. Based on the very low quality evidence, we are very uncertain about the effectiveness of drug treatments for people with NAFLD. There is a possibility that these treatments may not improve liver inflammation and fibrosis and may even cause harm. Further RCTs with larger numbers of participants are needed before we will be able to draw firm conclusions.

We searched databases for randomised controlled trials and for studies with eligible participants who had received diagnostic tests. We found 15 suitable studies with 1282 participants. In all of the studies, some participants had converted to Alzheimer’s disease or other types of dementia after taking part in the study. The follow-up period in the studies varied from less than one year to over four years. We found that the accuracy of the CSF t-tau test for Alzheimer’s disease was variable among the studies. The findings were similar for the CSF p-tau test and the CSF p-tau/ABeta ratio. The main sources of variation were differences in the ways that the studies recruited participants, what threshold they used for a positive result and how the studies were conducted. It is possible that the use of different thresholds contributed most to the lack of consistency in the findings. Due to the low number of studies, we could not assess the impact of each of these factors separately. The included studies were also generally of poor methodological quality, which may also have contributed to the inconsistency in the findings. The overall results were uncertain and should be interpreted with caution.

The evidence is current to April 2015. We included three small studies with a total of 170 participants. All participants were males undergoing robotic-assisted laparoscopic radical prostatectomy (a type of keyhole surgery using a robot to remove the prostate gland). Two studies were conducted in the USA and one in Italy. We did not include any studies on women. We found no clear evidence as to which anaesthetic technique - TIVA or inhalational - is better for reducing pain after surgery, but we did find some low-quality evidence that TIVA may reduce nausea and vomiting and possibly prevent an increase in eye pressure during surgery. None of the studies looked at how the anaesthetic techniques affected other aspects of the surgery or its consequences, such as overall complication rate, length of hospital stay, or costs. Overall, the quality of evidence was low. This was due to the small number of studies, the small number of participants in these studies and the fact that only male participants were included. One ongoing study seems to be well designed and may produce high-quality evidence in the future.

This review summarises the findings from 14 observational studies, with 1,601,515 study participants, evaluating the effects of using topical corticosteroids during pregnancy on pregnancy outcomes. We searched for studies that compared pregnancy outcomes in women who used topical corticosteroids during pregnancy to women who did not use topical corticosteroids. Topical corticosteroids were grouped by strength (mild to moderate potency versus potent to very potent). We found no evidence that women who used topical corticosteroids of any potency were more likely to have an abnormal baby, to give birth prematurely, or to have a low Apgar score than women who did not use topical corticosteroids. Although the combined analysis did not show a clear effect of either mild to moderate or potent to very potent topical corticosteroids on low birth weight, three of the four studies in the potent to very potent group reported an increased risk of low birth weight. Moreover, a protective effect of mild to moderate topical corticosteroids on foetal death was observed, with the risk of foetal death significantly reduced in women who used mild to moderate potency topical corticosteroids during pregnancy. However, this result should be interpreted cautiously because of concerns about unbalanced confounding variables in the one study that reported on this outcome. Overall, the evidence was of low to very low quality. The main limitations in the evidence were imprecision in the effects on low birth weight and inconsistency in the effects on foetal death.

We searched electronic databases and conference proceedings up to July 2014. We found four randomized controlled trials that met our inclusion criteria. All four trials compared treatment with steroids to treatment without steroids and had follow-up periods ranging from two months to one year. These trials were conducted in the USA, Canada, India, and South Africa. They included a total of 611 participants. Notwithstanding careful attention to methodological detail in the conduct of the studies, all four trials had significant loss to follow-up of participants. Three of the four trials were further hampered by small sample sizes, which rendered them underpowered to detect a true effect if one existed. Furthermore, the results of three of the four trials were inconsistent, making pooling of the data impossible for most outcomes measured in this review. As a result, we were unable to draw any firm conclusions regarding the use of topical steroids in bacterial keratitis. None of the four included trials found any difference in best-corrected visual acuity between the steroid and non-steroid groups at one month or later. Two trials reported corneal scarring, with similar results in both groups at the end of the follow-up period. Three trials reported adverse events, and none found any important differences between the steroid and non-steroid groups. One trial reported intraocular pressure (IOP) elevation, with more cases in the non-steroid group. No trial reported on quality of life or cost. The results of this review are consistent with either no effect or a small effect of steroids in bacterial keratitis. However, the body of evidence is far from robust, and further high-quality research is needed before any firm conclusions can be drawn.

This review of four studies with 443 participants suggested that local infusion of clot-dissolving drugs or mechanical devices through a catheter into the affected artery is not better than infusion of the same drugs into a vein for people with acute ischaemic stroke. However, more studies are needed to be sure of this finding. The results should be interpreted with caution because both the participant and the treating physician knew whether the intervention was performed, which may have biased the results. Also, the number of participants enrolled in the studies was small.

This review includes one trial with 120 families and 143 children. The interventions examined were centre-based day care for children younger than five years of age provided by professionals with higher levels of education and training than usual in the area. The control group received no formal day care, and families were encouraged to use local day care services if they wished to do so. Most children in the control group used these external services, which may have contaminated the results of our analysis. We found no evidence of differences between children who received centre-based day care and those who did not in terms of their cognitive development, psychosocial development, parental employment or household income. The quality of the evidence was very low for all these outcomes. This review cannot support or refute the effects of centre-based day care for children younger than five years of age and their families in high-income countries. More high-quality studies are needed to help families and policymakers make informed choices about whether to use centre-based day care. Future research should identify the components of centre-based day care that are important for improving developmental outcomes for children and parents.

A literature search was conducted to identify reports on histologic scoring indices used to assess bowel resection margins in UC. A screening team reviewed the titles and abstracts of potentially relevant articles. Study characteristics and information about the scoring systems were extracted from the remaining full-text articles by two review authors. We searched multiple electronic databases and conference proceedings up to May 2013. We identified 126 reports describing 30 scoring indices. Only 11 of these scoring indices have undergone any validation. None of the 11 have been fully validated. More research is needed to determine the best way to assess whether or not a bowel resection margin is free of cancer in patients with ulcerative colitis.

We searched the literature up to August 2014 and identified three completed randomised controlled trials that compared intramedullary nailing with plating in 213 participants, with useable data from 173 participants of whom 112 were male. The mean age of participants in individual studies ranged from 41 to 44 years. None of the trials compared surgery with non-surgical treatment. All three trials had a high risk of bias, making their results less reliable. Very low quality evidence (the highest level of uncertainty) meant that we could not be sure about the direction or size of effect for any of the outcomes. In terms of function, pain and complications, the results were similar for the two types of surgery. However, with further research it may still be possible to show that one treatment is better than the other. We concluded that there was either no or insufficient evidence to draw definitive conclusions on the use of surgery or the best surgical intervention for these fractures.

We found 11 studies including a total of 38,742 participants: eight studies compared BPLDs versus control (no blood pressure lowering) or placebo (a pretend treatment), and three studies compared different levels of systolic blood pressure. The majority of participants were middle-aged or elderly, and 62% were men. The length of follow-up was between one year and 12 years. We found that BPLD could reduce the risk of recurrent stroke, major vascular events (including heart attack, stroke, and death due to vascular disease), and dementia by 19%, 10%, and 12%, respectively. We found that using an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor or a diuretic (drugs that increase urine output) had similar effects to other types of drugs. We did not find any difference in effect between a low systolic blood pressure target and a higher systolic blood pressure target. We judged the quality of the evidence to be moderate for recurrent stroke and high for major vascular event and dementia. The overall quality of the evidence was limited due to some methodological issues in the included studies.

The evidence to date has shown that beta-sitosterols may help relieve the urinary symptoms of BPH and improve urine flow but have not shown any reduction in the risk of needing surgery or of dying from prostate cancer. More research is needed to fully evaluate the long-term benefits and side effects of this treatment.

We found 34 studies which compared salmeterol either to placebo (a dummy inhaler) or to another drug called salbutamol (also known as albuterol). These included 62,815 adult and child participants who had received a diagnosis of asthma. We were able to combine results from two of the largest studies, which included 51,535 participants. When we looked at the chances of having an asthma-related death, the risk was more than six times greater among people not taking inhaled steroids and taking salmeterol compared to taking salbutamol or a placebo. Inhaled steroids reduce the risk of asthma-related death, but even among people taking them, the risk of death was two times greater with salmeterol than with salbutamol or placebo. Salmeterol increased the chance of experiencing a serious adverse effect by 15%, meaning that one extra serious side effect would occur for every 188 people treated with salmeterol for 28 weeks. It is unclear how this compares in children because there are so few studies involving young people.

The review of trials found no evidence that giving thyroid hormones to preterm babies with RDS reduces the risk of complications or death. Two small studies enrolling 80 babies were included. The design of both studies was poor. Neither study found any benefit from thyroid hormone treatment.

We performed the present systematic review to determine the beneficial and harmful effects of non-absorbable disaccharides for primary and secondary prevention of hepatic encephalopathy in patients with cirrhosis. Evidence is current to May 2016. We identified 38 randomised clinical trials (RCTs) with a total of 1828 participants. The majority of the patients had overt hepatic encephalopathy. The RCTs originated from 11 countries. Ten of the RCTs compared different non-absorbable disaccharides versus each other. Twenty-six RCTs compared a non-absorbable disaccharide versus placebo/no intervention. Two RCTs did not state what they compared. The duration of treatment was at least two weeks and up to 24 weeks. The primary outcome was mortality. Secondary outcomes were hepatic encephalopathy, hepatic failure, hepatorenal syndrome, variceal bleeding, quality of life, safety, and serious adverse events. We found that non-absorbable disaccharides may be associated with a beneficial effect on mortality, hepatic encephalopathy, hepatic failure, hepatorenal syndrome, and variceal bleeding. There was no difference in the beneficial effects between non-absorbable disaccharides compared with placebo/no intervention when preventing hepatic encephalopathy in patients without overt hepatic encephalopathy. There was no difference in the beneficial effects between non-absorbable disaccharides compared with placebo/no intervention when treating overt hepatic encephalopathy. There was no difference in the beneficial effects between non-absorbable disaccharides compared with placebo/no intervention when treating minimal hepatic encephalopathy. There was no difference in the beneficial effects between lactulose and lactitol for the remaining outcomes. No studies compared lactitol with placebo. The evidence was of moderate quality. There was little published information about side effects and complications. The risks of bias of most of the RCTs were unclear or at a high risk of bias.

We found 65 studies with 3598 adult participants who had received a kidney transplant and were being treated with bone medications or placebo (a pretend treatment). On average, studies followed people for about 1 year. We did not find any studies that included children or adolescents. This review is current as of September 2019. In most studies, the first bone medication given to people after a kidney transplant was a bisphosphonate. These medicines are usually taken by mouth (as tablets) and include alendronate, etidronate, ibandronate, or risedronate. Other bone treatments included vitamin D drugs, teriparatide, denosumab, cinacalcet, surgery to remove part of the parathyroid glands, or calcitonin. We did not combine results from studies that compared different bone treatments to each other. Bisphosphonates may reduce bone fractures in adults after a kidney transplant. It is unknown whether any other medicine reduces fractures. We are very uncertain whether any of the treatments reduce death, heart problems, or graft loss. Bisphosphonates may reduce bone pain, but we are very uncertain whether they prevent spinal deformities or avascular bone necrosis (death of bone tissue due to lack of blood supply). Bisphosphonates may increase the risk of low calcium levels in the blood. We are very uncertain whether treatments such as vitamin D have any effect on skeletal, heart, death, or transplant function outcomes. Overall, the certainty of evidence was low for bisphosphonates and very low for all other treatments. Low certainty means that our confidence in the results is limited, and very low certainty means that we are very uncertain about the results.

The four studies included in this review had a total of 317 participants. The studies used different types of magnesium and different doses. The duration of the studies also varied from eight hours to 72 hours. There was no study that measured all of the identified outcomes and met the objectives of this review. Only one trial assessed clinical symptoms of seizure, delirium tremens or components of the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol (CIWA) score. A single outcome (handgrip strength) in three trials (113 people), was able to be combined in an analysis. Handgrip strength did not improve significantly with magnesium. No serious side effects were reported but the quality of the studies was low. Overall, there was insufficient evidence to determine whether magnesium is beneficial or harmful for the treatment or prevention of alcohol withdrawal syndrome.

We included 15 trials with 3057 participants. All patients had acute rhinosinusitis diagnosed by a doctor, some also had X-rays. We last searched for studies in February 2016. Seven studies received industry funding, and three did not state their funding source. One study was funded by a public agency. The other five did not state their funding source. Three out of five studies with missing funding information were small. Industry-funded studies tended to have larger sample sizes. Antibiotics did not make a significant difference in cure rate after 1 week or 2 weeks. However, 5 to 11 more people per 100 would be cured faster with antibiotics than without. This result holds true for both clinical diagnosis and X-ray proven rhinosinusitis. With antibiotics, the chance of having no symptoms after 1 week was 61%, compared to 55% without antibiotics. However, 13 more people per 100 would experience at least one side effect with antibiotics than without. Complications are rare. One person with a brain abscess occurred among 3057 participants. Given the low cure rate with antibiotics and the relatively high rate of side effects, there is probably no place for antibiotics in the treatment of adults with uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis. We do not know how well the results apply to children, people with weakened immune systems, or those with severe sinusitis, because the included studies did not include these groups. The quality of the evidence was high for clinical diagnosis, but only moderate for X-ray proven rhinosinusitis because of inconsistent results between studies.

This review found eight trials which together included information on 2515 people. The main comparison was between D2 and D3 lymphadenectomy. The review found that D2 lymphadenectomy improved survival from disease-related death, but that there was an increase in the number of deaths after surgery. This means that, while some patients will benefit from this type of operation, others will not survive the operation itself.

The objective of this review was to assess the efficacy of oral naltrexone maintenance in retaining people in treatment for heroin addiction. The review authors included 13 randomised controlled trials with a total of 1158 people who had been addicted to heroin and were randomly assigned to receive oral naltrexone or a placebo or were given some other medication or entered a waiting list. However, the quality of the evidence on offer is low and the conclusions are not clear. This could be due to the fact that many studies have methodological problems, the sample sizes are small and short-term follow-up makes it difficult to detect differences between treatments.

We found two studies examining the effects of modifying the consistency of fluids on swallowing difficulties in dementia. Both studies used a fluid called 'nectar', which is thicker than water but thinner than honey. One of these studies also looked at the very thick fluids known as 'honey thick'. However, because of concerns about safety, 'honey thick' fluids are not commonly used and are only recommended for people who need them for eating and drinking. We did not find any studies looking at modifications to food. The two studies were part of a larger study including people with dementia and people with Parkinson's disease. Only information on people with dementia could be analysed for this review. The studies had some design problems, and other aspects of their quality were not well described. We do not currently know whether thickening fluids has a beneficial effect on swallowing in dementia due to the lack of research. More high-quality research is needed before a final conclusion can be drawn.

We found six randomised controlled trials comparing natural cycle IVF with standard IVF, including 788 women. This review of trials found no evidence of a difference between natural cycle IVF and standard IVF in live birth rates, OHSS, clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, multiple pregnancy, gestational abnormalities or cycle cancellations. However, the evidence was limited by imprecision. The largest trial has not yet completed follow up. More evidence is needed before any firm conclusions can be drawn.

A total of 984 participants from 12 studies were included in this review. Only 811 participants were included in the final analysis. The number of participants in each study ranged from 10 to 206, and their average age ranged from 61 to 74 years. The duration of the programmes ranged from six weeks to one year. No reports described any serious side effects due to Tai Chi practice. The quality of evidence of the results ranged from very low to moderate. This review found that Tai Chi is likely to lead to greater increases in the distance walked in six minutes and better lung function following treatment compared with usual care. However, it remains uncertain whether Tai Chi is more effective than other treatments in improving symptoms, physical and psychosocial function, or whether it has any additional effects when added to other treatments. Furthermore, the style and number of Tai Chi forms used in the included studies differed greatly. Future research should aim to identify which style and number of Tai Chi forms are most beneficial for people with COPD.

We identified 72 studies with a total of 2470 participants. This is an update of a Cochrane Review previously published in 2011. Since then, we have located 35 new studies for inclusion. The majority of studies examined whether the use of virtual reality or interactive video games improved functional ability (activities of daily living, walking, balance, etc.) in people with stroke. Most studies compared virtual reality to standard physiotherapy approaches. Some studies compared virtual reality to a'sham' condition (e.g. playing a game that looked like it was interactive but wasn't). Finally, some studies compared virtual reality to no treatment. Overall, the evidence was of low quality. We did not find any evidence to suggest that virtual reality and interactive video gaming were better than standard physiotherapy approaches in improving functional ability (i.e. walking, balance, etc.). However, we did find that virtual reality had benefits when used in addition to standard physiotherapy approaches. Specifically, virtual reality appeared to improve performance in activities of daily living. There was no evidence of serious side effects from virtual reality or interactive video gaming and most studies reported that participants enjoyed playing the games. Future studies should assess the long-term benefits of virtual reality and should aim to conduct their research in larger pools of people.

Study characteristics We searched for trials comparing fluoroquinolones as an additional drug or replacing ethambutol or isoniazid in standard first-line regimens for people with new-onset TB. The review includes five trials with 1330 adults with TB. Most were men (82%) with a median age of 27 years. The length of treatment in the included trials was six months. When added to the standard regimen of rifampicin, isoniazid, and pyrazinamide, fluoroquinolones (ofloxacin, levofloxacin, or moxifloxacin) may have had little or no effect on death, relapse, or side effects. When used instead of ethambutol in the standard regimen, fluoroquinolones may have had little or no effect on death, sputum culture conversion (a decrease in the number of bacteria in the sputum), or side effects. When used instead of isoniazid in the standard regimen, fluoroquinolones may have had little or no effect on death, sputum culture conversion, or side effects. Four-month regimens are currently being tested in six ongoing trials. Quality of the evidence Overall, the quality of the evidence was low or very low. There were concerns about how the trials were conducted and the results presented.
